note: Plain-language practitioner briefs shown above the fold on each experiment page (verdict + the-one-idea/question/finding/why-it-matters + KPI numbers + per-chart how-to-read/takeaway). Authored per experiment from its own README/report and verified chart specs under a strict no-jargon ban-list; all jargon-precise detail stays below the fold. Regenerate with scripts/enrichment/generate_briefs.workflow.js (see docs/site_maintenance.md).

adaptive_cognitive_kernel.verdict_tag: Self-rewiring runtime ties the plain baseline
adaptive_cognitive_kernel.concept_primer: Imagine a calculator that rewrites its own wiring for each instruction in a program, versus an ordinary fixed calculator. This test asks whether the self-rewiring one follows long instruction lists better.
adaptive_cognitive_kernel.plain_question: Does a calculator that rewires itself for each step follow long symbolic programs better than an ordinary fixed one?
adaptive_cognitive_kernel.plain_answer: It depends. The self-rewiring runtime clearly beats its own crippled versions, proving its edits do real work, but it only ties the ordinary fixed calculator on the longest programs. Both collapse toward random-guess accuracy as programs get longer.
adaptive_cognitive_kernel.why_it_matters: Extra self-rewiring machinery is not free proof of smarter computation. Before crediting a fancy mechanism, check it against a plain baseline and on inputs longer than those it trained on.

key_numbers.label: Answer correct, short vs long programs
key_numbers.sub: self-rewiring runtime collapses toward random-guess as programs lengthen

key_numbers.label: Self-rewiring vs ordinary calculator, longest programs
key_numbers.sub: a tie, not a win, on the hardest split

key_numbers.label: Step tracking, real vs scrambled instructions

charts.chart_plain_title: Getting the final answer right on the longest programs
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one method's share of correct final answers on the hardest 12-step programs; taller is better. The self-rewiring runtime and its two crippled versions sit beside the ordinary fixed calculator and a plain direct model.
charts.chart_takeaway: The self-rewiring runtime's bar tops its own crippled versions but sits just below the ordinary fixed calculator, so its extra machinery does not win here.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy fades as programs get longer
charts.chart_read: Each line tracks one method's share of correct final answers as programs grow from 4 to 12 steps; higher is better, and everything past 6 steps is longer than what training covered. The self-rewiring runtime and the ordinary fixed calculator are the top two lines.
charts.chart_takeaway: All lines plunge steeply as programs lengthen, with the self-rewiring runtime tracking the fixed calculator rather than pulling ahead.

charts.chart_plain_title: Following each step correctly on long programs
charts.chart_read: Each bar shows how often a method gets each intermediate step right on 12-step programs; taller is better. Bars compare the self-rewiring runtime run normally versus with its instruction codes scrambled, randomized, or switched off, plus the ordinary fixed calculator.
charts.chart_takeaway: Scrambling or randomizing the instructions crushes the self-rewiring runtime from tall to tiny, proving it does ordered work, yet the plain calculator still tracks steps best.

charts.chart_plain_title: Handling never-before-seen instruction pairings
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars show final-answer accuracy on programs with unfamiliar instruction combinations, at 8 and 12 steps; taller is better. The four methods are the self-rewiring runtime, the fixed calculator, the plain direct model, and the edits-disabled version.
charts.chart_takeaway: The self-rewiring runtime edges ahead at 8 steps but the gap disappears at 12 steps, where every method sinks together.

charts.chart_plain_title: Run-to-run wobble on the hardest programs
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars show final-answer accuracy on the longest programs across three separate training runs; taller is better. Each cluster compares the self-rewiring runtime, the fixed calculator, the edits-disabled version, and the plain direct model.
charts.chart_takeaway: The bounce between training runs is about as big as the gap between the runtime and the fixed calculator, so neither reliably wins.

charts.chart_plain_title: How fast each method learns during training
charts.chart_read: Each line shows accuracy climbing as training proceeds; higher and steeper is better. The self-rewiring runtime and the fixed calculator are the top pair, while the edits-disabled version is the flat bottom line.
charts.chart_takeaway: The self-rewiring runtime and fixed calculator rise together almost identically, while turning off the self-edits leaves learning nearly flat.

charts.chart_plain_title: Real instructions versus scrambled ones across lengths
charts.chart_read: Each line tracks how often the self-rewiring runtime gets each step right as programs grow, comparing real instruction order against scrambled and randomized versions; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The real-order line stays well above the scrambled and randomized lines at every length, confirming the runtime genuinely relies on instruction order.

belief_filter_executor.concept_primer: Picture a detective narrowing a list of suspect number-pairs: each clue rules some out. The model keeps that whole list of still-possible answers and updates it one instruction at a time.
belief_filter_executor.plain_question: Can the model track every still-possible answer and update the whole list correctly, one instruction at a time?
belief_filter_executor.plain_answer: Yes. When the model took one internal step per instruction, it placed over 91% of its confidence on the correct answers, while two simpler methods that skip the step-by-step tracking stayed under 6%.
belief_filter_executor.why_it_matters: Give the model an internal step for each instruction and let it hold the full set of possibilities, and it can execute exact multi-step logic instead of guessing shortcuts.

key_numbers.label: Confidence on correct answers, at the threshold
key_numbers.sub: once the model takes one step per instruction

key_numbers.label: Best rival shortcut method
key_numbers.sub: methods that skip full step-by-step tracking

key_numbers.label: Longest program handled
key_numbers.sub: though trained only up to 8

charts.chart_plain_title: Full-tracking method vs two shortcut methods
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars by program length. Height is the share of confidence placed on the correct answers; taller is better. One bar is the full step-by-step method, two are shortcut methods that skip it.
charts.chart_takeaway: The full-tracking bars tower near or above 90% at every length while both shortcut bars sit near the floor under 6%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Confidence jumps once steps match the program length
charts.chart_read: Each line is one program length. Horizontal axis is how many internal steps the model takes; vertical is confidence on correct answers, higher is better. Watch where each line leaps upward.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line stays near zero, then leaps above 91% exactly when the model's steps reach that program's instruction count, even for longer programs never trained on.

charts.chart_plain_title: One extra step flips the answer from wrong to right
charts.chart_read: Two bars per program length: best confidence when the model has too few steps, versus confidence once it takes one step per instruction. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The left bar stays under 14% and the right bar jumps over 91%, showing the crossover happens right at the one-step-per-instruction point.

charts.chart_plain_title: More training sharpens confidence at every length
charts.chart_read: Two bars per program length compare an earlier training checkpoint against the final one. Height is confidence on correct answers at the one-step-per-instruction point; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The final bars are higher at every length, with the biggest gain on the longest programs, so extra training mainly sharpens an ability that already worked.

charts.chart_plain_title: Smaller-scale rerun shows the same pattern
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars by program length in a smaller version of the task. Height is confidence on correct answers; taller is better. Bars compare the full-tracking method against two shortcut methods.
charts.chart_takeaway: The full-tracking method again holds 92-96% while both shortcut methods stay under 18%, so the advantage is not tied to one problem size.

charts.chart_plain_title: Longer programs leave fewer possible answers
charts.chart_read: Two falling lines against program length. One is the average number of answers still possible; the other is how far the model's answer sits from the truth. Lower means fewer possibilities and a tighter answer.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines fall as programs get longer, because added clues shrink the possibilities from about 13 down to under 2, making longer programs easier to pin down.

bridge_dose_recombination_curriculum.verdict_tag: A few worked examples unlock skill-combining
bridge_dose_recombination_curriculum.concept_primer: The model can do each basic skill alone but fails on tasks that need two combined. A "bridge" example is a fully worked-out solution showing exactly how to fuse two skills.
bridge_dose_recombination_curriculum.plain_question: Does adding just a handful of worked examples that combine two skills teach the model to handle unseen skill combinations?
bridge_dose_recombination_curriculum.plain_answer: Yes. Success on unseen skill-combinations climbed from about 7% with no bridge examples to roughly 30% with a few, all without adding any extra training data. Only genuine combined examples worked; near-miss look-alikes did not.
bridge_dose_recombination_curriculum.why_it_matters: When a model fails at fusing two skills it already has, don't retrain broadly. Add a small number of exact worked examples of the fusion, but keep the dose modest so ordinary tasks don't suffer.

key_numbers.label: Unseen combinations solved, no bridges vs. some
key_numbers.sub: success on withheld skill-pairs after adding a few combined examples

key_numbers.label: Best result on unseen combinations
key_numbers.sub: achieved with four combined examples per pair

charts.chart_plain_title: More combined examples, more unseen combinations solved
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many combined worked-examples were added per skill-pair; vertical is percent of tasks solved, higher is better. The bottom line is unseen combinations; the two upper lines are familiar and reformatted tasks.
charts.chart_takeaway: The unseen-combinations line rises steeply from about 7% to around 30% by two-to-four examples, then flattens, showing a small dose is enough.

charts.chart_plain_title: Only real combined examples work, not look-alikes
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a different training recipe; height is percent of unseen combinations solved, higher is better. Bars run from no combined examples, to near-miss look-alikes, to two and four genuine combined examples.
charts.chart_takeaway: The near-miss bar stays down near the no-example baseline while genuine combined examples reach about 30%, so look-alikes are no substitute.

charts.chart_plain_title: The worked-out steps must line up at teaching and test time
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a variant of the heaviest dose; height is percent of unseen combinations solved, higher is better. The first bar keeps aligned reasoning steps; the others remove or scramble those steps during teaching or testing.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the aligned-steps bar reaches about 30%; every stripped or scrambled variant collapses back to roughly 7-8%.

charts.chart_plain_title: The gains land on some skill-pairs, not all
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is a withheld skill-pair family; bars within it are different training doses; height is percent solved, higher is better. Colors mark no-example, light, heavy, and near-miss recipes.
charts.chart_takeaway: One family jumps to near-perfect with dosing while another never improves at any dose, showing benefits are concentrated, not uniform.

charts.chart_plain_title: Some basic skills fuse far more easily than others
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a basic skill involved in the withheld pair; height is percent of those tasks solved at the heavy dose, higher is better. Bars are sorted from easiest to hardest to fuse.
charts.chart_takeaway: Iteration and text-cleanup skills fuse at about 92% while arithmetic-style skills stay near zero, a wide spread across skill types.

charts.chart_plain_title: Heavy dosing quietly hurts ordinary tasks
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many combined examples were added; vertical is percent of tasks solved, higher is better. One line is familiar tasks, the other is reformatted versions of them.
charts.chart_takeaway: Familiar-task success slides from about 86% down to 58% at the heaviest dose, while reformatted-task success holds steady, revealing a hidden cost.

counterexample_rule_repair.verdict_tag: Reading failed examples fixes the bug
counterexample_rule_repair.concept_primer: The code is broken and nobody says how. All the model gets is a short list of failing cases showing, for each, what the code returned versus what it should have returned. It must infer the hidden rule from those examples.
counterexample_rule_repair.plain_question: Can the model fix buggy code by studying a few failing cases instead of being told the correct rule outright?
counterexample_rule_repair.plain_answer: Yes, once trained to study the failing cases, the model fixed the hidden rule on familiar tasks about 91% of the time, versus near-zero for every version that lacked, ignored, or was given scrambled failing cases.
counterexample_rule_repair.why_it_matters: When you can print concrete failing cases, training a model to actually read them yields large gains. But expect it to fail on rule types that never appeared in its training.

key_numbers.label: Correct fixes on familiar tasks
key_numbers.sub: untrained vs. trained to read failing cases

key_numbers.label: Same model with the failing cases removed
key_numbers.sub: it was genuinely reading the cases

key_numbers.label: A brand-new kind of rule
key_numbers.sub: never seen in training, nothing fixed it

charts.chart_plain_title: Only the model trained to read failing cases fixes the bug
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by task type (familiar, same rules shown differently, brand-new rule); each colored bar is a different training setup; height is the share of bugs correctly fixed, and taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: One bar, the model trained on real failing cases, towers over all others on familiar tasks; every other setup sits near the floor and all collapse to zero on the brand-new rule.

charts.chart_plain_title: Take away the failing cases and the same model fails
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by task type; the three bars are the same trained model given the real failing cases, the cases removed, or the cases scrambled; taller means more bugs fixed.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the real-cases bar has any height; removing or scrambling the cases drops it flat to zero, so the model is reading them, not memorizing fixes.

charts.chart_plain_title: Some rule types survive a new presentation, one does not
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by kind of rule; two colors compare familiar tasks against the same rules shown in a different format; taller means more bugs fixed.
charts.chart_takeaway: Labeling and threshold rules stay near full height in both formats, while one arithmetic rule family drops to zero once the format changes.

charts.chart_plain_title: Every trained version writes valid patches; only one fixes the logic
charts.chart_read: Paired bars per training setup; one color is the share whose patch was well-formed enough to apply, the other is the share that actually fixed the bug; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The well-formed-patch bars are near full for every trained setup, but the actually-fixed bar rises only for the one trained on real failing cases.

charts.chart_plain_title: More training examples and passes roughly double the fix rate
charts.chart_read: One bar per model on a small check set: no training, a small quick training run, then a larger longer one; taller means more bugs fixed.
charts.chart_takeaway: The bar climbs from zero with no training, to about half with the small run, to most bugs fixed with the larger run.

cyclic_transition_ladder.verdict_tag: Wrap-around building blocks crack modular tracking
cyclic_transition_ladder.concept_primer: Picture tracking a hidden number on a clock face: each step nudges it forward or back and it wraps around at the top. The model must apply that same wrap-around rule over and over.
cyclic_transition_ladder.plain_question: When a small network must repeatedly update its guess about hidden clock-style numbers, does it need matching wrap-around building blocks, or is a bigger generic network enough?
cyclic_transition_ladder.plain_answer: Yes, it needs the matching building blocks. Networks given wrap-around parts stayed perfectly accurate on programs three times longer than they practiced on, while generic networks drifted down to single-digit confidence no matter how their inputs were dressed up.
cyclic_transition_ladder.why_it_matters: When a model must apply the same wrap-around update repeatedly, don't just pile on parameters or reformat the inputs; give it the matching structural building blocks. Generic networks plateau and drift on longer programs.

key_numbers.sub: generic network vs. clock-structured network

key_numbers.sub: same comparison, stricter full-state test

charts.chart_plain_title: Only clock-structured networks stay perfect on the longest programs
charts.chart_read: Each group is a different network, from a perfect rule-follower to two generic networks to two given clock-style building blocks. Bars show confidence in the correct answer (and a stricter full-state match); taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two clock-structured networks reach full-height bars matching the perfect rule-follower, while both generic networks sit near the floor: structure, not size, solves it.

charts.chart_plain_title: Generic networks drift down as programs get longer; structured ones stay flat
charts.chart_read: Left-to-right is program length, extending past what the networks practiced on; height is confidence in the correct answer, higher being better. Each line is one network.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two generic lines slope steadily downward as programs lengthen while the structured lines stay pinned at the top: generic networks lose the thread, structured ones don't.

charts.chart_plain_title: Structured networks stay near-perfect even on a much bigger clock
charts.chart_read: Each group is a network; two bars compare its exact-state confidence on the smaller number range versus a roughly tripled range. Taller bars are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The bars barely shrink when the number range triples, staying essentially full height: the structured approach holds up as the problem grows.

charts.chart_plain_title: Softer routing sometimes picks the wrong step yet still answers exactly
charts.chart_read: Left-to-right is task size; height is how often each network picks the exact intended update step, higher being better. Two bars compare a soft-gating network with a hard-picking one.
charts.chart_takeaway: The soft-routing bars dip slightly below the always-perfect hard-routing bars on small tasks, meaning it occasionally mislabels a step but still reads out the right answer.

dense_latent_query_executor.verdict_tag: Step-by-step memory partly tracks hidden numbers
dense_latent_query_executor.concept_primer: Picture keeping a running tally in your head as someone reads out steps that change two hidden numbers. A model can hold such a small memory, updating it one step at a time as it reads each step.
dense_latent_query_executor.plain_question: Can a model with a small step-by-step memory track two hidden numbers through a list of instructions, taught only by one final answer per example?
dense_latent_query_executor.plain_answer: It depends. The step-by-step memory partly learned to track the hidden numbers, clearly beating a model that reads the whole list in one glance. But it stayed rough — on the longest programs only about a fifth of its confidence landed on correct answers.
dense_latent_query_executor.why_it_matters: If you need a model to track a changing state across many steps, updating memory one step at a time beats one-glance reading — but plain memory stays approximate on long chains and needs extra training pressure.

charts.chart_plain_title: Step-by-step memory beats one-glance reading at every program length
charts.chart_read: Bars show how much of the model's confidence landed on correct answers (taller is better) for each program length, from short to long. Dark bars are the step-by-step memory model; light bars read the whole program in one glance.
charts.chart_takeaway: Dark bars sit above light bars at every length — the step-by-step model always does more, though both shrink sharply as programs grow longer.

charts.chart_plain_title: Extra thinking steps only help once they cover the whole program
charts.chart_read: Each line is one program length. The horizontal axis is how many internal thinking steps the model takes; the vertical axis is confidence on correct answers (higher is better). Lines stay flat, then jump.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line stays flat until the thinking steps reach the program's length, then leaps up — extra steps help only when they read more instructions.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much of the hidden numbers the memory still held afterward
charts.chart_read: Bars show how much of the true hidden numbers could be recovered from the model's memory after the fact (taller is better), by program length. Dark bars are the step-by-step model; light bars read everything in one glance.
charts.chart_takeaway: The step-by-step model's dark bars tower over the one-glance bars, yet even they fade to almost nothing on long programs — no near-perfect memory.

charts.chart_plain_title: Scrambling the instruction order erases the step-by-step advantage
charts.chart_read: Bars show confidence on correct answers (taller is better) by program length in an easier setting. Three models: step-by-step memory, one-glance reading, and step-by-step memory with its instructions scrambled out of order.
charts.chart_takeaway: The scrambled-order bars drop down near the one-glance model, well below the properly ordered step-by-step model — reading in order is what drives the gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: Single-number questions are easier than combined-number questions
charts.chart_read: Bars show confidence on correct answers (taller is better) by program length for four question types: asking one hidden number, the other, their sum, or their difference.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two single-number questions stay above the sum and difference questions at every length — combining both hidden numbers is consistently harder.

charts.chart_plain_title: Learning over time: answering first, then a memory read-back test
charts.chart_read: Two separate training runs move rightward over time. One line tracks the model learning to answer questions; the other tracks a later test of how much its frozen memory reveals about the hidden numbers. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both curves climb steeply then level off — the model learns to answer, and its memory turns out to hold much of the hidden-number information.

charts.chart_plain_title: Only ordered step-by-step memory keeps information on longer, unseen programs
charts.chart_read: Bars show how much of the hidden numbers could be recovered from memory (taller is better) by program length, including two lengths longer than any seen in training. Three models: step-by-step, one-glance, and scrambled-order.
charts.chart_takeaway: On the longer unseen programs the step-by-step bars stay tall while both other models collapse to near zero — only ordered stepping carries over.

charts.chart_plain_title: Best-guess accuracy: step-by-step memory versus one-glance reading
charts.chart_read: Two lines by program length show how often the model's single best guess was actually a correct answer (higher is better). One line is the step-by-step memory model, the other reads the whole program in one glance.
charts.chart_takeaway: The step-by-step line runs far above the one-glance line, and the gap widens on the longest programs — stepping helps most when programs get hard.

dense_supervision_ladder.verdict_tag: Richer training feedback is the real lever
dense_supervision_ladder.concept_primer: Picture a small scratchpad that tracks which answers are still possible as a step-by-step program runs. This test asks how much feedback that scratchpad needs during training to track well.
dense_supervision_ladder.plain_question: Does a compact answer-tracker struggle because it is too small, or because we train it with too little feedback?
dense_supervision_ladder.plain_answer: Too little feedback. Showing the model the full set of still-possible answers at every step, instead of one final answer, roughly doubled accuracy on the longest programs, from 9% to 19%. The compact scratchpad was never the limit.
dense_supervision_ladder.why_it_matters: When a small model struggles at multi-step tracking, invest in richer step-by-step training targets before assuming it needs to be bigger; but expect very long chains to stay hard even with the best feedback.

charts.chart_takeaway: The full-feedback line sits above all others at every length, roughly doubling the weak-feedback line on the longest programs.

charts.chart_plain_title: Only rich training leaves the internal state readable
charts.chart_read: Bars show how much of the model's hidden state can be read back as which answers are still possible, on the longest programs. One color reads the state as-is, the other trains a dedicated reader. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the two recipes trained on possible-answer sets yield a readable state; the others sit at near-zero.

charts.chart_plain_title: Giving the model enough internal steps matters
charts.chart_read: Both lines use the richest training. The horizontal axis is program length; the vertical axis is accuracy. The upper line gives one internal step per program instruction; the lower line cuts the steps short. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Accuracy jumps whenever the model gets a full internal step per instruction, though it still fades on the longest programs.

charts.chart_plain_title: A smaller warm-up task shows the same ordering
charts.chart_read: Same setup on an easier version of the task. Each line is a training recipe; the horizontal axis is program length, the vertical axis is accuracy. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The recipes stack in the same order as the main task, showing richer feedback winning is not a fluke.

charts.chart_plain_title: Direct-read questions are easier than arithmetic ones
charts.chart_read: Bars group four question kinds — read A, read B, their sum, their difference — with one bar per training recipe. Taller means the model's best guess was a valid answer more often. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Sum and difference questions sit lower than plain read-A or read-B for every recipe, and full feedback is tallest across all four.

charts.chart_plain_title: Readable internal state fades with length but rich training keeps the edge
charts.chart_read: Each line is a training recipe. The horizontal axis is program length; the vertical axis is how much of the still-possible-answer set can be read from the hidden state. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line drops as programs lengthen, but the full-feedback line stays highest at every length, not only the longest.

charts.chart_plain_title: Longer programs actually narrow down to fewer possible answers
charts.chart_read: Two lines count how many answers stay consistent with the clues as the program runs; the horizontal axis is program length. Lower means fewer candidates remain.
charts.chart_takeaway: By the longest programs only about one to two answers remain, so lower accuracy there reflects harder reasoning, not more choices.

dense_teacher_distillation.verdict_tag: Perfect coaching still yields only approximation
dense_teacher_distillation.concept_primer: Imagine tracking every value two hidden numbers could still be as a recipe of steps runs. A perfect tracker keeps the exact list of possibilities; a rough one keeps only a fuzzy sense of the likely values.
dense_teacher_distillation.plain_question: Can a small fixed-size memory learn to track every possibility exactly if it is shown the perfect answer at every step?
dense_teacher_distillation.plain_answer: No. Even with the teacher's complete answer shown at every step, the small fixed memory learned only a rough approximation, not exact tracking. Bigger memory helped and got closer, but never closed the gap, especially on longer recipes.
dense_teacher_distillation.why_it_matters: Handing a compact model the perfect answer is not enough for exact step-by-step reasoning; the memory's structure is the real limit, so build the tracking structure in rather than only supervising harder.

charts.chart_plain_title: Right answers vs the full picture as recipes get longer
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is recipe length (longer is harder); the vertical axis is how much of the model's confidence lands on the target, higher is better. One line tracks landing on the right answer, the other tracks reproducing the exact full set of possibilities.
charts.chart_takeaway: The right-answer line sits well above the full-picture line and both fall as recipes lengthen, showing it learned a useful shortcut rather than exact tracking.

charts.chart_plain_title: Bigger memory lifts right-answer coverage most
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is recipe length (longer is harder); the vertical axis is the share of confidence landing on the right answer, higher is better. Three lines compare two memory sizes and two internal designs.
charts.chart_takeaway: Doubling the memory size lifts every line the most, while the design tweak adds only a smaller consistent edge.

charts.chart_plain_title: Full-picture coverage collapses as recipes lengthen
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is recipe length (longer is harder); the vertical axis is the share of confidence on the exact full set of possibilities, higher is better. Three lines compare two memory sizes and two internal designs.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line sinks toward the bottom as recipes lengthen; wider memory helps a little but none stays high, so no version tracks exactly.

charts.chart_plain_title: Easier setup: comparing designs and memory sizes
charts.chart_read: Bars group four model versions; each has a pair of bars, one for landing on the right answer and one for reproducing the full set of possibilities. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The compressed-design version has clearly the shortest bars, which is why it was dropped from later tests.

charts.chart_plain_title: A fresh readout finds no hidden exact picture
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is recipe length (longer is harder); the vertical axis is the share of confidence on the exact full set of possibilities, higher is better. One line is the trained readout, the other a fresh simple readout of the same memory.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two lines lie almost on top of each other, meaning no hidden exact picture is stored, so the memory itself is the bottleneck.

end_to_end_structured_slot_executor.verdict_tag: Both purpose-built parts needed to solve it
end_to_end_structured_slot_executor.concept_primer: Imagine a deduction game where you keep a shortlist of every hidden answer still possible, then cross options off as each clue arrives. Here a single model both builds that shortlist and updates it.
end_to_end_structured_slot_executor.plain_question: Can one model both build the starting shortlist of possible hidden answers and correctly update it as each instruction is applied?
end_to_end_structured_slot_executor.plain_answer: Yes, when both jobs use purpose-built machinery. The full learned version keeps about 98% of the answer correct, nearly matching a version handed everything, while swapping either job for a generic part collapses it to a quarter or near zero.
end_to_end_structured_slot_executor.why_it_matters: Getting reliable step-by-step reasoning here required building the right structure into both the setup and the update steps; a generic neural component in either spot quietly breaks the whole chain.

key_numbers.label: Fully learned, both parts purpose-built
key_numbers.sub: share of the answer kept correct on longest puzzles

key_numbers.label: Generic starting-shortlist part instead
key_numbers.sub: same puzzles, setup built the generic way

key_numbers.label: Generic update-step part instead
key_numbers.sub: same puzzles, updates built the generic way

key_numbers.label: Version handed everything (ceiling)
key_numbers.sub: reference for a perfect solver

charts.chart_plain_title: Which parts must be purpose-built to solve the longest puzzles
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one build of the model, pairing a purpose-built or generic starting-shortlist part with a purpose-built or generic update part; height is the share of the final answer kept correct on the longest puzzles. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The four bars using purpose-built parts sit near the top around 98 to 100%, while the two using a generic part fall to about a quarter or near zero.

charts.chart_plain_title: Staying accurate as puzzles get longer than seen in training
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is puzzle length, stretching to three times the training length; the vertical axis is share of the answer kept correct. Each line is one build; higher and flatter is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The purpose-built lines stay near the top across all lengths, while the two generic-part lines slope steeply downward as puzzles grow longer.

charts.chart_plain_title: Holding up on a harder version with far more possibilities
charts.chart_read: Same axes on a harder version with many more possible answers; two lines compare the version handed everything against the fully learned build. Higher and flatter is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned build tracks just below the perfect reference line, still keeping about 95% of the answer correct on the longest puzzles.

charts.chart_plain_title: How fast each build learns during training
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is training progress; the vertical axis is share of answers correct so far. Each line is one build; rising faster and higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The purpose-built builds climb past 97% early in training, while the two generic-part builds flatten out around 60 to 80%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Where the failing builds lose the answer
charts.chart_read: For each build, two bars: how correct the starting shortlist is before any instructions, and how correct the final answer is after the whole puzzle. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The generic-setup build already loses about 40% before starting, while the generic-update build begins decent then erodes from 59% down to 20%.

charts.chart_plain_title: The same ranking repeats on a smaller version
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is puzzle length; vertical axis is share of the answer kept correct, on a smaller version of the task. Each line is one build; higher and flatter is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The same ordering repeats: purpose-built builds stay above 99% while the generic-part lines fall off as puzzles get longer.

charts.chart_plain_title: A faster training speed rescues one build
charts.chart_read: Two builds, each shown with two bars for a lower versus higher training-speed setting; height is share of the answer kept correct on a small warm-up version. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Raising the training speed lifts the update-step build from 87% to 99%, a much bigger jump than the alternative mixer build gets.

episodic_echo_ttt.verdict_tag: Studying real observations did not help
episodic_echo_ttt.concept_primer: Imagine giving the model a few seconds to cram from an episode's notes before it answers a question, then testing whether cramming on the real notes helps more than cramming on scrambled or irrelevant ones.
episodic_echo_ttt.plain_question: If the model briefly studies what it just observed before deciding, does it actually make better decisions?
episodic_echo_ttt.plain_answer: No. Cramming on the real observations left decisions at random-guess level, exactly like doing no cramming, and the single best score came from a scrambled-notes control, so the method failed its own fairness test.
episodic_echo_ttt.why_it_matters: A quick self-study step can shift the model's answers, but unless it beats scrambled-notes controls the shift is not real learning. Always test against corrupted-input controls before trusting such gains.

key_numbers.label: Studying real observations
key_numbers.sub: no change from random guessing

key_numbers.label: Random-guess level
key_numbers.sub: four options, one correct

key_numbers.label: Best score anywhere
key_numbers.sub: and it came from a scrambled-notes control

charts.chart_plain_title: Decision accuracy after self-study: real notes vs scrambled-notes controls
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often each version picked the right answer; one blue group is in-distribution episodes, the other is unfamiliar episodes. Left bar is real-observation self-study; the rest are scrambled or no-study controls. Higher is better; 25% is random guessing.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every bar sits at or near the 25% random line, and the tallest bar is actually a scrambled-notes control, so real study earns nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Does more self-study help? Accuracy across zero, one, and two reps
charts.chart_read: Each line tracks accuracy as the model does more self-study reps (left to right), one line per version plus a flat reference line at random-guess level. Higher lines are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The real-observation line stays flat on the random-guess line as reps increase, while only a scrambled-notes control drifts slightly upward.

charts.chart_plain_title: How wrong its observation guesses were, before and after self-study
charts.chart_read: Each line shows prediction error on unseen observations as self-study reps increase; lower means the model guesses observations better. One line is real-observation study, the others are scrambled or generic controls.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three lines fall by about the same amount, so studying the real observations reduces error no more than studying scrambled ones.

charts.chart_plain_title: Per-run accuracy at two reps on unfamiliar episodes
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars show accuracy for each of three separate training runs, colored by which notes were studied. Higher is better; 25% is random guessing.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the first run shows any spread between versions; the other two sit flat at random, meaning the apparent gains are run-to-run noise.

charts.chart_plain_title: Drop in observation error by what content was studied
charts.chart_read: Bars show how much prediction error fell after two self-study reps, split by whether real, scrambled, or generic content was studied, for both episode types. Bigger downward bars mean a larger error drop.
charts.chart_takeaway: The scrambled and generic controls cut error about as much as the real observations, showing the improvement is not tied to the true episode.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy across reps on unfamiliar episodes
charts.chart_read: Each line tracks accuracy as self-study reps increase on episodes of a kind never seen before, one line per version. Higher is better; 25% is random guessing.
charts.chart_takeaway: Real-observation study hovers just around random while the scrambled-notes control actually drifts below it, so no version reliably helps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Stability of the shared pre-study setup across training
charts.chart_read: Each line tracks observation prediction error over training steps for one of three runs; lower is better. There is no reference line.
charts.chart_takeaway: The lines wobble up and down with no steady descent, and no run ends better than it began, signaling an unstable setup.

execution_conditioned_repair.verdict_tag: Repair training memorizes, does not transfer
execution_conditioned_repair.concept_primer: Researchers trained the model to fix its own broken code after seeing the error it caused, like a student drilling worked examples — then tested whether that skill carried over to brand-new kinds of bugs.
execution_conditioned_repair.plain_question: Does training the model to fix its own broken code, using the error message it saw, help it fix kinds of bugs it never practiced on?
execution_conditioned_repair.plain_answer: No. The training let the model fix 100% of bugs like the ones it practiced on, up from about 10% before, but 0% of unseen bug types — it memorized the practice templates instead of learning to repair.
execution_conditioned_repair.why_it_matters: A perfect score on familiar practice bugs can hide zero real skill; always test a repair model on bug types it has never trained on before trusting it.

key_numbers.label: Familiar-type bugs fixed
key_numbers.sub: untrained model vs after repair training, same bug families

key_numbers.label: Brand-new bug types fixed
key_numbers.sub: every training method, on families never practiced

key_numbers.label: Best a coding-specialist model managed on new types
key_numbers.sub: barely above the 2-of-27 no-error-message control

charts.chart_plain_title: Fixes collapse from perfect to zero on unseen bug types
charts.chart_read: Each group along the bottom is a way of training the model to fix its broken code; bar height is the share of bugs it fixed after its first failed try. One color is bugs like those it practiced, the other is brand-new bug types. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The practiced-bug bars reach full height for the repair-trained methods, while every brand-new-bug-type bar sits flat at zero — memorization, not real repair.

charts.chart_plain_title: A specialist coding model recovers only a sliver on new bug types
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by training method; height is the share of bugs fixed. One color is familiar practiced bugs, the other is unseen bug types. Taller is better. This uses a model already specialized for coding.
charts.chart_takeaway: Familiar-bug bars again reach full height, but the new-bug-type bars barely lift off the floor even with the error message shown — too tiny to credit.

charts.chart_plain_title: Patches apply cleanly yet fix nothing on new bug types
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by how much information the prompt gives the model; one color is the share of patches that apply without error, the other is the share that actually fix the bug. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The apply-cleanly bars stand tall while every actual-fix bar is zero — getting a patch to apply is not the same as fixing the bug.

charts.chart_plain_title: More training memorizes the practice set but adds no transfer
charts.chart_read: Two bars for how long the model was trained; one color is fixes on the 20 practiced bugs, the other is fixes on set-aside bugs it never saw. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Longer training lifts practiced-bug fixes from 15% to 95%, yet the set-aside bugs stay flat at zero — pure memorization.

charts.chart_plain_title: On new bug types, patches pass the shown test but fail hidden ones
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by training method; one color is the share of patches that apply, the other is the share that pass the one visible test but fail hidden tests. A tall second bar is bad — it means gaming, not fixing.
charts.chart_takeaway: For the repair-trained methods nearly every applied patch passes the visible test yet fails the hidden ones — the model games the one test it can see.

charts.chart_plain_title: Trying three fixes per bug still repairs none of the new types
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by training method; one color is the share of bugs fixed when keeping the best of three tries, the other is the share of patches that apply. A taller fix bar is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best-of-three fix bars are all zero while patches still apply — extra attempts buy nothing on unseen bug types.

charts.chart_plain_title: Early pilot cleaned up formatting but never fixed the bug
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by training method; colors show the share of patches that apply, that are valid code, and that actually fix the bug. A taller actual-fix bar is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The apply and valid-code bars climb toward 71% while the actual-fix bar stays at zero for every method — surface polish, no repair.

factor_recombination_ladder.verdict_tag: Learned fixes don't transfer to new skill mixes
factor_recombination_ladder.concept_primer: Think of a cook who nails every recipe they've practiced but can't invent a dish by blending two techniques they've never combined. We tested whether the model, taught to fix bugs with worked solutions, could combine skills in brand-new pairings.
factor_recombination_ladder.plain_question: Once the model learns to fix bugs from worked solutions, can it fix bugs that mix two skills it never saw combined?
factor_recombination_ladder.plain_answer: No. Trained on worked solutions, the model fixed 81% of familiar problem types but only about 8% of brand-new skill combinations, barely above guessing, and no training recipe moved that wall.
factor_recombination_ladder.why_it_matters: Teaching a small model with worked solutions boosts problems resembling its training, but don't expect it to generalize to genuinely novel skill combinations. Cover the combinations you need directly.

key_numbers.label: Fixing familiar problem types
key_numbers.sub: when shown a worked solution

key_numbers.label: Fixing brand-new skill combinations
key_numbers.sub: barely above random guessing

key_numbers.label: Best any recipe reached on new combinations
key_numbers.sub: no training method broke through

key_numbers.label: Same problems, just reworded
key_numbers.sub: still handled well

charts.chart_plain_title: Fixing familiar problems versus brand-new skill combinations
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is one training method; bars show the share of bugs fixed (taller is better) on familiar problems, the same problems reworded, and brand-new skill combinations.
charts.chart_takeaway: The brand-new-combination bars stay tiny (about 8-10%) for every method, while familiar bars reach 64-81% — combining skills is a hard wall.

charts.chart_plain_title: What happens when the worked solution is taken away at test time
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of bugs fixed (taller is better) when the model is given its correct worked solution, none at all, or a scrambled one, for familiar and reworded problems.
charts.chart_takeaway: Performance collapses from 64-81% to near zero once the worked solution is removed or scrambled, proving the model genuinely reads it.

charts.chart_plain_title: Success on each unseen new problem type
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is one unseen new problem type; bars show the share fixed (taller is better) for three training methods.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only a single problem type reaches 33-50%; the rest sit flat at zero, so nearly all new combinations never get solved.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which individual skills carry over to new combinations
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one skill appearing inside a new combination; height is the share fixed (taller is better) for the worked-solution-trained model.
charts.chart_takeaway: Most skills sit at or near zero; only two (ordering at 21% and tuple handling at 17%) show any carryover to novel pairings.

charts.chart_plain_title: Do the extra skill labels matter, or the worked solution?
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is a different test-time prompt; bars show the share fixed (taller is better) on familiar, reworded, and brand-new-combination problems.
charts.chart_takeaway: Stripping the skill labels barely hurts (81% to 75%), but removing the worked solution collapses it to about 22% — the worked solution does the work.

charts.chart_plain_title: Do fixes pass unseen checks, not just the shown ones
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is one training method; paired bars show the pass rate on the checks the model was shown versus hidden checks it never saw (taller is better).
charts.chart_takeaway: Hidden-check bars match or slightly exceed the shown ones, so successful fixes are real, not gaming the visible checks.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training time is nearly identical across methods
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one training method; height is the training time in seconds (shorter is cheaper).
charts.chart_takeaway: All four methods take about seven minutes, so the large accuracy gaps come from the worked-solution content, not from more compute.

feature_factorized_rule_diversity.verdict_tag: Mixing practice types did not improve generalization
feature_factorized_rule_diversity.concept_primer: Think of drilling a coder to fix broken programs, then testing them on bug types never practiced. We compare three practice diets: isolated skills, combined skills, or a mix of both.
feature_factorized_rule_diversity.plain_question: Does mixing isolated-skill and combined-skill practice help the model fix brand-new bug types better than either practice diet alone?
feature_factorized_rule_diversity.plain_answer: No. All three practice diets fixed only about 20-23% of brand-new bug combinations, so mixing bought no extra generalization, even though each diet aced the familiar problems it drilled on.
feature_factorized_rule_diversity.why_it_matters: Do not expect a blended practice diet to unlock generalization to new problem combinations; gains stay on familiar territory. The step-by-step worked reasoning, however, is genuinely essential.

key_numbers.label: Fixing familiar problems
key_numbers.sub: each diet aces the kind it drilled on

key_numbers.label: Fixing brand-new combinations
key_numbers.sub: all three diets tie, no winner

key_numbers.label: Without step-by-step reasoning
key_numbers.sub: removing worked examples collapses repair

charts.chart_plain_title: Each practice diet aces its own problems but ties on new ones
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars show how often each training recipe fixes bugs across three tests: familiar single-skill, familiar combined-skill, and brand-new combinations. Taller is better; the flat gray bar is the untrained model.
charts.chart_takeaway: On the brand-new-combination test all three trained bars sit at nearly equal low height, so mixing practice adds no generalization over either recipe alone.

charts.chart_plain_title: Step-by-step worked reasoning is required, not optional
charts.chart_read: Bars show repair success on brand-new bug combinations for five setups that keep, remove, or scramble the step-by-step worked examples during practice or testing. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the bar keeping intact worked examples rises; every removed or scrambled version drops to near zero, so the reasoning is essential.

charts.chart_plain_title: Nearly all apparent transfer comes from a single problem family
charts.chart_read: For each of five brand-new bug families, grouped bars show repair success under the three practice recipes. Taller is better; each color is one recipe.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the 'sorted join' family reaches full height for every recipe while the other four stay at the floor, so transfer is confined to one family.

charts.chart_plain_title: The worked reasoning must be present when solving, not just when practicing
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars compare the same trained model tested with intact, removed, or scrambled worked examples, across familiar-single, familiar-combined, and new-combination tests. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Removing or scrambling the worked example at test time flattens every group to near zero, so the reasoning must accompany the actual problem.

charts.chart_plain_title: Transfer to new problems is skill-specific, not broad
charts.chart_read: Bars show the mixed-diet model's repair success on new combinations, one bar per underlying skill involved, sorted from highest to lowest. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: A few skills reach full height while most sit near the floor, so the model's transfer is narrow and skill-specific rather than general.

charts.chart_plain_title: Failures are misunderstanding the rule, not bad formatting
charts.chart_read: Paired bars per training recipe: one for how often the code fix is well-formed and applies, one for how often it passes the hidden tests. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The 'applies cleanly' bar is near full for every recipe while 'passes tests' stays low, so failures come from misreading the rule, not formatting.

joint_register_executor.verdict_tag: Tracking linked unknowns step by step works
joint_register_executor.concept_primer: Picture two boxes holding unknown numbers that always differ by a fixed amount. The model keeps a running list of every possible pair and updates that list one instruction at a time.
joint_register_executor.plain_question: Can the model correctly track a set of linked unknown numbers by working through a hidden list of instructions one thinking step at a time?
joint_register_executor.plain_answer: Yes. Given at least one thinking step per instruction, it pinned the exactly correct set of pairs 87 to 98% of the time, while two simpler versions that cannot track the link between the two numbers stayed at random-guess level.
joint_register_executor.why_it_matters: Reasoning that holds a full picture of linked possibilities and spends one internal step per instruction can handle longer problems than it was trained on, but only when its memory can actually represent those links.

key_numbers.label: Correct-set accuracy, longest untrained programs
key_numbers.sub: linked-memory model on 24-instruction programs it never trained on

key_numbers.label: Too few steps vs enough steps
key_numbers.sub: jump once thinking steps match the number of instructions

key_numbers.label: Simpler link-blind models
key_numbers.sub: never beat 1-in-31 odds at any length

charts.chart_plain_title: Only the linked-memory model solves the puzzle
charts.chart_read: Grouped by program length, bars show how much probability each of three models places on the exactly correct set of number pairs; taller is better. The linked-memory model towers over two simpler models stuck at random-guess level.
charts.chart_takeaway: The linked-memory bars stay high at every length while both simpler models flatline at random-guess, showing the links between the two numbers must be tracked together.

charts.chart_plain_title: The answer snaps into place once thinking steps match the instructions
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is the number of internal thinking steps, vertical axis is probability on the correct set of pairs; higher is better. Each line is a different program length; longer programs need more steps.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line stays flat near random-guess, then leaps upward exactly when the thinking steps reach the number of instructions.

charts.chart_plain_title: Extra thinking only helps once it finishes the instructions
charts.chart_read: For each program length, two bars compare the best result with too few thinking steps against the result once steps match the instruction count; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The too-few-steps bars sit near random-guess while the matched bars jump to 87-98%, so steps only help when they run the whole program.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy holds up as programs get longer
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is program length, vertical axis is probability on the correct set once thinking steps match the instructions; higher is better. Two lines are two difficulty settings.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines slope down only gently as programs lengthen, showing accuracy stays high even well past the lengths seen in training.

charts.chart_plain_title: During training, only the linked-memory model actually learns
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is training progress, vertical axis is the share of pairs it gets right while learning; higher is better. One line per model.
charts.chart_takeaway: The linked-memory line climbs to a perfect score early while both simpler models crawl along the bottom, never really learning the task.

charts.chart_plain_title: An easier version of the task cannot tell the models apart
charts.chart_read: On a simpler task with a single known starting pair, the horizontal axis is thinking steps and the vertical axis is fraction correct; higher is better. Two model lines overlap.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both models trace the same line and both reach a perfect score, showing only the harder linked task exposes the difference between them.

latent_executor.verdict_tag: More private thinking steps caused exact answers
latent_executor.concept_primer: Imagine solving a long sum in your head one step at a time: with too few steps you have not finished, but give yourself exactly enough and you land on the right answer.
latent_executor.plain_question: Can a model get an exact answer right by taking more private, invisible thinking steps before it responds?
latent_executor.plain_answer: Yes, giving the model more private thinking steps reliably made it exactly right. Accuracy sat at random-guess level until the steps matched the program length, then jumped to 100 percent, while plainer versions that lacked the right internal setup never improved.
latent_executor.why_it_matters: Extra thinking time only pays off when the model has the right internal setup to carry the work forward; give it that and correctness scales sharply, otherwise more steps buy nothing.

key_numbers.label: Answer right, too few private steps
key_numbers.sub: before the model has enough internal steps to finish

key_numbers.label: Answer right, enough private steps
key_numbers.sub: once internal steps match the program length

key_numbers.label: Longest program handled
key_numbers.sub: trained on short programs, still perfect on much longer ones

key_numbers.label: Simpler versions without the setup
key_numbers.sub: extra thinking time alone never helped them

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy jumps to perfect once the model gets enough private steps
charts.chart_read: Each line is a program of a given length; up-and-right is better. The horizontal axis is how many private thinking steps the model takes, the vertical axis is how often it gets the exact answer. Every line sits flat near the bottom then leaps to the top.
charts.chart_takeaway: Each program stays at random-guess level until the private steps reach its length, then snaps to 100 percent, even for long programs never seen in training.

charts.chart_plain_title: The structured model is perfect where the plainer versions flatline
charts.chart_read: Bars group by program length; taller is better. The tall bars are the structured step-by-step model, the tiny bars beside them are two simpler versions that skip the organized worksheet. Height is how often the exact answer is right.
charts.chart_takeaway: The structured model reaches 100 percent at every length while both simpler versions stay flat at random-guess level, so the organized worksheet is what matters.

charts.chart_plain_title: The structured model learns fast; the plainer versions never learn
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is training progress, vertical axis is how often the answer is exactly right during training; higher and earlier is better. Two lines climb to the top quickly, two stay pinned at the bottom.
charts.chart_takeaway: The structured model rockets to perfect early in training while the simpler versions never rise off random-guess level, no matter how long they train.

charts.chart_plain_title: Extra private steps do nothing for the plainer version
charts.chart_read: Each line is a program length for the simpler model that lacks the organized worksheet. Horizontal axis is number of private steps, vertical axis is exact-answer rate; higher is better. All lines run flat along the bottom.
charts.chart_takeaway: Adding private steps leaves the simpler model stuck at random-guess level, showing that extra thinking time alone buys nothing without the right internal setup.

charts.chart_plain_title: The model is genuinely working partway before the answer locks in
charts.chart_read: For short programs, horizontal axis is number of private steps and vertical axis is how many individual values it gets right, not the whole answer; higher is better. Two lines rise steadily and then reach the top.
charts.chart_takeaway: Partial correctness climbs with each private step before the full answer snaps into place, evidence the model is truly executing the program step by step.

charts.chart_plain_title: The same pattern repeats on a second version of the task
charts.chart_read: Each line is a program length on a different arithmetic setting. Horizontal axis is private steps, vertical axis is exact-answer rate; up-and-right is better. Lines sit flat then jump to the top once steps reach their length.
charts.chart_takeaway: The same sharp rule holds on a second task version, confirming it is a reliable mechanism and not a one-off fluke.

learned_sparse_slot_executor.verdict_tag: Learned memory works small, fails to scale
learned_sparse_slot_executor.concept_primer: Picture a detective's whiteboard listing every suspect still possible, crossing names off as clues arrive. Here a small network keeps a handful of such memory slots and must both set up the board and update it.
learned_sparse_slot_executor.plain_question: Can a small network learn to keep and update a scratchpad of memory slots that tracks the possible answers to a structured, step-by-step reasoning problem?
learned_sparse_slot_executor.plain_answer: It depends. On a small problem, when handed the starting board, the network learns to update it almost perfectly, beating the version that learns everything at once. But on a bigger problem the same approach collapses far below the flawless hand-built version.
learned_sparse_slot_executor.why_it_matters: A plain network can nail short structured reasoning if handed the right starting state, but without built-in arithmetic structure it won't scale to bigger problems. Bake in the structure or stage the learning.

key_numbers.label: Learned updates, small problem
key_numbers.sub: correct-answer probability, near perfect at the harder length

key_numbers.label: Same method, bigger problem
key_numbers.sub: the identical approach collapses when the problem grows

key_numbers.label: Hand-built version
key_numbers.sub: proves the memory design can hold the exact answer

charts.chart_plain_title: On the bigger problem, learned versions trail the hand-built one
charts.chart_read: Each line tracks how much probability lands on the correct final answer (higher is better) as programs get longer, left to right. The flat top line is the hand-built version; the two sloping lines learn part of the job.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both learned lines slope steadily downward and sit far under the flat perfect line, and the gap widens as programs get longer.

charts.chart_plain_title: Getting the full exact state right collapses fastest for learned updates
charts.chart_read: Lines show probability placed on the exact full answer state (higher is better) as programs lengthen. The flat top line is the hand-built version; the lowest line learns the step-by-step updates.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned-update line falls to near one-tenth by the longest program, dropping well below the version that only learns the initial setup.

charts.chart_plain_title: On the small problem, learned updates stay near perfect
charts.chart_read: Lines show probability on the correct answer (higher is better) as programs lengthen. The top line learns the step updates from a ready-made memory layout; the lower lines learn the setup themselves.
charts.chart_takeaway: When handed the starting layout, the learned updater holds a nearly flat top line across lengths, while learning setup and updates together decays fastest.

charts.chart_plain_title: The small-problem success does not carry over to the bigger problem
charts.chart_read: Bars show probability on the exact answer state at the longest program tested (taller is better) for three setups: learned updates on the small problem, and two learned setups on the bigger problem.
charts.chart_takeaway: The tall small-problem bar shrinks to a stub on the bigger problem, showing the same method fails to scale rather than being unable to represent the answer.

charts.chart_plain_title: Memory slots lose focus as programs get longer
charts.chart_read: Lines track how sharply each memory slot is focused on a single value (a diagnostic of internal state, not a score) as programs lengthen. This describes how the slots behave, not which setup is best.
charts.chart_takeaway: Most lines drift downward with length while the learned-update line stays flat, hinting its slots stop sharpening the way the task needs.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training keeps improving but never fully settles on the bigger problem
charts.chart_read: Lines show probability on the correct answer (higher is better) as training proceeds, left to right. The upper line learns only the setup; the lower line learns the step updates.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines are still climbing when training stops, with the learned-update line trailing by roughly ten points the whole way.

charts.chart_plain_title: On the small problem, both setup and update learning matter
charts.chart_read: Lines show probability on the correct answer (higher is better) as programs lengthen, for four combinations of what the model learns versus what it is handed.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the version handed the starting layout keeps a flat top line; learning setup and updates together drops the most across length.

charts.chart_plain_title: Even the easiest problem shows learned updates lagging
charts.chart_read: Bars show probability on the exact answer state at a short program length (taller is better) for five versions, from fully hand-built to fully learned.
charts.chart_takeaway: The hand-built bar reaches the top while every learned-update bar falls short, so the shortfall appears even at the easiest setting.

query_filter_executor.verdict_tag: Answer-only grading taught internal step-by-step reasoning
query_filter_executor.concept_primer: Think of a detective who keeps a running list of every suspect still possible and crosses names off with each new clue, even though only one final question gets asked at the very end.
query_filter_executor.plain_question: Can training a model only on final answers teach it to work through a problem one step at a time in its head?
query_filter_executor.plain_answer: Yes. Graded only on final answers, the model learned to run each program one step at a time and keep an accurate running list of possibilities, staying about 96% correct on programs three times longer than it practiced on, while two simpler methods collapsed.
query_filter_executor.why_it_matters: Grading only the final answer can still teach a model to carry out a multi-step internal procedure, as long as its design lets it hold the working state, so supervision need not spell out every intermediate step.

key_numbers.label: Longest program, step-by-step model
key_numbers.sub: share of answer on the correct value, at three times its training length

key_numbers.label: Longest program, next-best method
key_numbers.sub: same measure for the stronger of the two simpler methods

key_numbers.label: Internal picture accuracy
key_numbers.sub: correct running list at the longest program, though never graded on it

charts.chart_plain_title: Only the step-by-step model stays accurate on long programs
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is program length (longer is harder; the model only ever practiced on the shortest lengths); the vertical axis is how much of its answer lands on the correct value, where higher is better. Three bars per length compare the step-by-step model against two simpler methods.
charts.chart_takeaway: The step-by-step bars stay near the top across every length while both simpler methods shrink toward the floor, so only it handles programs three times longer than it practiced on.

charts.chart_plain_title: Answers snap to correct once thinking steps reach program length
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is the number of internal thinking steps the model takes; the vertical axis is how much of its answer lands on the correct value, higher is better. Each line is a different program length; up and to the right is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line stays flat and low, then jumps sharply upward exactly when the number of thinking steps matches the program's length.

charts.chart_plain_title: The model builds an accurate internal picture it was never graded on
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is program length; the vertical axis is how much of the model's internal running list points at the exactly correct hidden state, which was never used for grading. Higher is better; three bars compare the step-by-step model with two simpler methods.
charts.chart_takeaway: The step-by-step bars sit near the top while both simpler methods hug zero, showing only it forms an accurate internal picture on its own.

charts.chart_plain_title: All four question types answered well at the hardest length
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is the four question types (two ask a single value, two ask a combination of both values); the vertical axis is how much of the answer lands correctly on the longest program, higher is better. Two bars show the final answer and the internal picture.
charts.chart_takeaway: The combination questions match the single-value questions, so even the harder relational questions are answered at the toughest length.

charts.chart_plain_title: Same snap-to-correct pattern repeats in a second setup
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is the number of internal thinking steps; the vertical axis is how much of the answer lands correctly, higher is better. Each line is a program length in an independent, smaller version of the task.
charts.chart_takeaway: Each line jumps from low to near-perfect right when the thinking steps reach the program's length, matching the main setup.

charts.chart_plain_title: With too few thinking steps, combined questions are hardest to guess
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is program length; the vertical axis is the best the model can do when it is not given enough thinking steps to finish, higher meaning easier to guess. Four bars per length split the single-value and combined-value question types.
charts.chart_takeaway: The combined-value bars sit below the single-value bars and all bars shrink as programs get longer, so shortcuts fade fastest for the hardest questions.

charts.chart_plain_title: Extra training mostly polishes the longest programs
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is program length; the vertical axis is how much of the answer lands correctly once the model is given enough thinking steps, higher is better. Two bars compare a mid-training snapshot with the finished model.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both snapshots are already high, and the final model gains the most on the longest programs, so late training mainly sharpens the hardest cases.

qwen35_4b_activation_steering.verdict_tag: Internal nudging did not change behavior
qwen35_4b_activation_steering.concept_primer: Inside an AI, information flows as patterns of internal activity. You can try to steer its answer by adding a chosen pattern into that activity while it works, like nudging a car's wheel mid-drive to change course.
qwen35_4b_activation_steering.plain_question: When an AI solves a multi-step problem, can we detect the correct first step hidden in its internal activity and push that signal back in to make it name that step?
qwen35_4b_activation_steering.plain_answer: No. Feeding the correct-first-step pattern back into the model's internal activity while it worked did not improve how often it named the right first step. It scored about the same as no nudge at all, and even matched a meaningless random nudge. Detectable does not mean usable.
qwen35_4b_activation_steering.why_it_matters: If you can detect a skill sitting unused inside a model's internal activity, do not assume you can switch it on by nudging that activity. Unlocking it likely needs retraining the model's weights or giving it an outside tool.

key_numbers.label: Detected inside the model
key_numbers.sub: a reader finds the correct first step in the internal activity

key_numbers.label: Named correctly, no nudge
key_numbers.sub: how often the model says the right first step on its own

key_numbers.label: Named correctly, nudged
key_numbers.sub: barely different, so the nudge did not help

charts.chart_plain_title: Nudging the wiring toward the right first step barely changes the answer
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often the model names the correct first step, for an easier and a harder problem set. Within each set, four bars: no nudge, nudge toward the true step, toward a wrong step, toward random. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The four bars sit at nearly the same height in both problem sets. Nudging toward the true first step is no taller than doing nothing or nudging at random.

charts.chart_plain_title: Stronger nudging never pulls ahead, and eventually makes things worse
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how hard we nudge; the vertical axis is how often the model names the correct first step. Three lines track nudging toward the true step, a wrong step, or random. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The true-step line hovers barely above the others at gentle nudges but stays flat, then all three lines fall as hard nudging garbles the model's output.

qwen35_4b_active_counterexample_trace_se.verdict_tag: Well-chosen extra tests close the gap
qwen35_4b_active_counterexample_trace_se.concept_primer: Imagine several candidate programs that all match the examples you can see, yet some secretly fail on cases you cannot. The fix: request a few extra test cases, each picked to best expose the wrong candidates.
qwen35_4b_active_counterexample_trace_se.plain_question: When several programs all match the visible examples, can asking for a few well-chosen extra test cases reveal which one is actually right?
qwen35_4b_active_counterexample_trace_se.plain_answer: Yes. Programs matching every visible example still failed hidden tests about 23% of the time, but requesting twelve well-chosen extra test cases lifted the pass rate to 97.5%, beating random questions at every step.
qwen35_4b_active_counterexample_trace_se.why_it_matters: When many answers look equally good on the examples you already have, don't just commit or ask blindly. A handful of deliberately chosen test cases resolves most of the ambiguity cheaply.

key_numbers.label: Right on hidden tests, visible examples only
key_numbers.sub: commit without asking anything extra

key_numbers.label: Right after 12 well-chosen extra tests
key_numbers.sub: targeted questioning

key_numbers.label: Right with 12 random extra tests
key_numbers.sub: same budget, unfocused questions

key_numbers.label: Hardest batch, fully solved
key_numbers.sub: 12 targeted questions

charts.chart_plain_title: Smart extra questions close the hidden-failure gap
charts.chart_read: Left to right, more extra test cases are requested; higher up means more programs pass every hidden test (better). One line asks the most revealing question each time, one asks at random, and a top line shows the best any questioner could do.
charts.chart_takeaway: The smart-question line rises well above the random line at every point, meaning deliberately chosen tests weed out wrong programs far faster.

charts.chart_plain_title: Six smart questions beat random on all three batches
charts.chart_read: Each group of bars is one batch of problems; taller bars mean more programs pass every hidden test (better). Bars go from asking nothing, to six random questions, to six smart questions, to the best-possible questioner.
charts.chart_takeaway: The six-smart-question bar nearly matches the best-possible bar and towers over the random bar on all three batches.

charts.chart_plain_title: Smart questions fully solve the hardest batch
charts.chart_read: Left to right, more extra test cases are requested; higher up means more programs pass every hidden test (better). One line picks the most revealing question each time, one picks at random, and a top line marks the best a questioner could do.
charts.chart_takeaway: The smart-question line reaches the very top by twelve questions, matching the best-possible line, while random questions stall below it.

qwen35_4b_adaptive_evidence_budget_polic.verdict_tag: Model learned when to stop gathering evidence
qwen35_4b_adaptive_evidence_budget_polic.concept_primer: Picture a detective who does not choose which tests to run, but decides after each result whether they know enough to name the culprit or should order one more test. That single stop-or-continue call is what we trained.
qwen35_4b_adaptive_evidence_budget_polic.plain_question: Can the small model learn when it has seen enough evidence to commit to an answer, instead of always running every possible check?
qwen35_4b_adaptive_evidence_budget_polic.plain_answer: Yes. After training, the model solved 92% of tasks while running only about 5 checks on average, matching the accuracy of always running all 10 checks at roughly half the effort, and nearly as efficiently as the best-possible stopping strategy.
qwen35_4b_adaptive_evidence_budget_polic.why_it_matters: A model can be taught to spend effort only when a task needs it, cutting wasted work on easy problems while still nailing the hard ones, rather than paying a flat cost every time.

key_numbers.label: Tasks solved, before vs after training
key_numbers.sub: the untrained model quit almost immediately; training taught it to keep going

key_numbers.label: Checks used on average
key_numbers.sub: same accuracy as running all ten, at roughly half the cost

key_numbers.label: Best-possible stopping uses
key_numbers.sub: the trained model's ~4.8 is close to this ideal

key_numbers.label: Simple fixed cutoff of 3 checks solves
key_numbers.sub: stopping too early on every task leaves most unsolved

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy versus how many checks were spent
