charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is average checks used, the vertical axis is share of tasks solved correctly; up and to the left is better. One line traces simple fixed cutoffs of 3, 6, and 10 checks; separate dots mark the trained model and the best-possible stopping strategy.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained model's dot sits above the fixed-cutoff line, hitting top accuracy at fewer checks, close to the best-possible dot.

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of tasks solved by each stopping approach
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a different stopping rule; bar height is the share of tasks solved correctly, so taller is better. The untrained model and quick-cutoff rules sit at the left; the trained model, best-possible stopping, and the full ten-check budget sit at the right.
charts.chart_takeaway: The untrained model's tiny bar jumps to a tall one after training, matching both the ideal stopper and the full-budget bar.

charts.chart_plain_title: Checks spent on easy versus hard task types
charts.chart_read: The two groups are an easy task type and a hard task type; bar height is average checks used, and lower means less effort. Bars compare the trained model, the best-possible stopper, and a rule that always runs all ten checks.
charts.chart_takeaway: On easy tasks the trained model's bar is short and on hard tasks it is tall, closely tracking the best-possible stopper instead of the flat ten.

qwen35_4b_adaptive_tool_controller.verdict_tag: Smart tool switch helps only partway
qwen35_4b_adaptive_tool_controller.concept_primer: Facing a messy data table, the model can answer straight from memory or pause to run a small program (a "tool"). The controller is a rule deciding which, per task — like knowing when to reach for a calculator.
qwen35_4b_adaptive_tool_controller.plain_question: Can a simple learned rule decide when the model should run a tool instead of just answering a data-table question directly?
qwen35_4b_adaptive_tool_controller.plain_answer: Partly. The learned rule lifts correct answers from 42% to 50% at low extra cost, but a simpler hand-set rule does better, reaching 58%. So the idea works, yet leaves gains on the table.
qwen35_4b_adaptive_tool_controller.why_it_matters: Reaching for a tool pays off only on a narrow slice of table tasks. A cheap switch captures the easy wins; grabbing the harder wins needs calling more tools, not just a smarter switch.

key_numbers.label: Correct without tools
key_numbers.sub: answering straight from the table

key_numbers.label: Correct with learned switch
key_numbers.sub: rule decides when to run a tool

key_numbers.label: Best reachable
key_numbers.sub: ceiling a simpler fixed rule hits

key_numbers.label: Extra effort spent
key_numbers.sub: work per task vs answering directly

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each approach gets the answer right
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of 50 table tasks answered correctly; taller is better. Left is answering directly, then the learned switch, then three reference rules including a best-possible setup.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned switch bar rises above direct answering but stops short of the taller reference bars, so it captures some gains but not all.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy against how much effort each approach costs
charts.chart_read: Each point plots correctness (up is better) against average effort per task (left is cheaper). One line is usable everyday policies; the other is idealized references. Up-and-left is best.
charts.chart_takeaway: The usable line climbs steadily for little extra cost, while the reference points sit far right at the same height, meaning full-effort setups pay roughly four times more for no better accuracy.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which task types the switch actually helps
charts.chart_read: Paired bars per task family show correctness (taller is better) for direct answering versus the learned switch, five tasks each. Same height means no difference.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only two families show the switch bar rising above direct answering, and none drop, so all gains come from a couple of structural-split task types and nothing is made worse.

qwen35_4b_answer_potential_trace_sft.verdict_tag: Answer-fit scoring can't reliably pick good reasoning
qwen35_4b_answer_potential_trace_sft.concept_primer: Imagine grading a student's scratch work not by their final answer, but by how neatly it sets up the correct answer to be written next. This project scored an AI's own step-by-step reasoning that same way.
qwen35_4b_answer_potential_trace_sft.plain_question: When a model writes out its reasoning, can we score each attempt by how well it sets up the known correct answer, then keep only the best-scoring attempts to train on?
qwen35_4b_answer_potential_trace_sft.plain_answer: No. This way of scoring reasoning was ruled out as a way to choose what to train on. Its top pick did edge out picking at random, but the gap was too small to meet the bar, and the model almost never finished its reasoning or produced a readable answer on its own.
qwen35_4b_answer_potential_trace_sft.why_it_matters: Before spending effort curating self-generated reasoning for training, first prove your scoring method picks reasoning the model can actually finish and voice into a real answer on its own. A signal that is statistically real can still be far too weak to act on.

key_numbers.label: Telling better reasoning from worse
key_numbers.sub: just under the 65% mark it had to clear to go ahead

key_numbers.label: Edge over picking at random
key_numbers.sub: real, but short of the +10 points needed to justify training

key_numbers.label: Reasoning that finished on its own
key_numbers.sub: nearly every attempt ran until it was cut off at a length limit

key_numbers.label: Answers correct when readable
key_numbers.sub: on the rare occasions it did commit, it was usually right

charts.chart_plain_title: Answer-fit scoring fell short on every launch requirement
charts.chart_read: Four launch checks run along the bottom. For each, one bar shows what the scoring method actually reached and a second bar the minimum needed to proceed. Taller is better; the reached bar sits below the required bar every time.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every reached bar sits under its required bar, so not one launch check was met and no training was authorized.

charts.chart_plain_title: Reasoning almost never finished, so answers rarely landed
charts.chart_read: Three bars trace the answer stage: how often reasoning stopped on its own, how often a forced answer came out readable, and how often readable answers were correct. The first two are tiny; the last is tall.
charts.chart_takeaway: The first two bars are near zero while the last is tall: reasoning rarely finished or produced a readable answer, yet when it did, it was usually correct.

qwen35_4b_balanced_core_answer_potential.concept_primer: The model can produce dozens of different scratch-work paths for one problem. This test asks whether the paths that make the known answer more likely are actually better lessons than paths chosen by success, chance, or simple brevity.
qwen35_4b_balanced_core_answer_potential.plain_question: Can we teach a small model more effectively by banking its most answer-supporting complete reasoning, or is choosing its shortest complete reasoning just as good?
qwen35_4b_balanced_core_answer_potential.plain_answer: No capability answer yet. The expensive nine-family run has been narrowed to a checksum-preserved three-family core: 331 of 360 tasks are already safely banked, and the mandatory training comparison now includes the strongest early rival, choosing the shortest complete reasoning.
qwen35_4b_balanced_core_answer_potential.why_it_matters: This preserves almost one hundred million tokens of completed work without letting sunk cost dictate an open-ended run. It also makes the test harder to fool: answer-based selection must beat ordinary success filtering, random reasoning, shortest reasoning, and reasoning copied from another task.

key_numbers.label: Balanced tasks safely banked
key_numbers.sub: only 29 more tasks are allowed before the hard stop

key_numbers.label: Reasoning chains already preserved
key_numbers.sub: 97.9 million sampled thinking tokens behind checksums

key_numbers.label: Training strategies compared
key_numbers.sub: answer score, joint score, random, successful, shortest, and task-shuffled

charts.chart_plain_title: Preserved reasoning chains versus the balanced hard stop
charts.chart_read: The first bar is the number of complete reasoning chains already stored; the second is the exact N=64 total when all 360 balanced tasks are done. The small remaining gap is the only additional harvest allowed.
charts.chart_takeaway: Most of the balanced pool is already preserved, so finishing the focused test reuses the sunk cost without reopening the much larger nine-family schedule.

qwen35_4b_balanced_discriminative_bridge.concept_primer: Think of teaching someone to fix broken code by handing them a few practice problems first. The question: does an even, balanced practice set teach better, or a set hand-picked to be the trickiest?
qwen35_4b_balanced_discriminative_bridge.plain_question: Does hand-picking the trickiest practice problems teach the model to fix new code better than a plain, evenly-spread practice set?
qwen35_4b_balanced_discriminative_bridge.plain_answer: No. An even, balanced practice set lifted success on new tasks from about 60% to 99%, while hand-picking the hardest, most-confusing practice problems did clearly worse (around 83-90%) on the same practice budget.
qwen35_4b_balanced_discriminative_bridge.why_it_matters: When adding a small set of practice examples, favor even coverage across task types over clever selection of the hardest cases. Chasing difficulty can quietly regress task types that a balanced set handles cleanly.

key_numbers.label: Even practice set, tough new tasks
key_numbers.sub: share of new tasks solved completely

key_numbers.label: Hardest-picked practice set
key_numbers.sub: same budget, worse than the even set

key_numbers.label: Worked examples scrambled
key_numbers.sub: model genuinely reads the examples

charts.chart_plain_title: Even practice beats hand-picked hard practice on new tasks
charts.chart_read: Four training setups along the bottom, from a plain starter to the even practice set to two hand-picked-hard sets; paired bars show easier versus tougher new tasks; height is share of new tasks solved completely, and taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The even-practice bars stand tallest near 99%, while both hand-picked-hard setups sit lower, so picking harder examples hurt rather than helped.

charts.chart_plain_title: The model truly reads the worked examples it is shown
charts.chart_read: Three ways of showing the worked examples along the bottom: correct examples, no examples, and scrambled examples; height is share of tough new tasks solved completely, and taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Removing the examples costs only a little, but scrambling them collapses success to 16%, showing the model depends on the actual content of the examples.

charts.chart_plain_title: Hand-picking helped a few task types but broke others
charts.chart_read: Five different task types along the bottom, each with three bars for the even set and the two hand-picked-hard sets; height is share solved completely on tough tasks, and taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The even set stays near the top across every task type, while the hand-picked sets crater on some, dropping as low as 17%.

qwen35_4b_bank_the_thoughts.verdict_tag: Teaching correct step-by-step plans beats teaching answers
qwen35_4b_bank_the_thoughts.concept_primer: Imagine training an apprentice to solve puzzles. You can drill them on final answers alone, or teach them to write out the steps that lead to each answer. This page compares those two training styles on the same puzzles.
qwen35_4b_bank_the_thoughts.plain_question: When you train a model to solve multi-step problems, does teaching it to write out its reasoning steps work better than just teaching it the final answers?
qwen35_4b_bank_the_thoughts.plain_answer: Yes. Teaching the model correct step-by-step plans lets it solve more three-step problems than teaching only the final answers (about 33% versus 20% of problems). But the plan must be genuinely correct and written out at solving time — wrong plans actually do worse than teaching nothing extra.
qwen35_4b_bank_the_thoughts.why_it_matters: When training a model to solve multi-step problems, invest in genuinely correct worked-out plans, not just answer keys or the model's own after-the-fact explanations. The quality of the reasoning steps matters far more than merely adding reasoning at all.

key_numbers.label: Trained on answers only
key_numbers.sub: share of three-step problems solved, best of many tries

key_numbers.label: Trained on correct plans
key_numbers.sub: same problems, same data except the steps shown

key_numbers.label: Trained on wrong plans
key_numbers.sub: worse than answers alone — bad steps actively hurt

key_numbers.label: Correct-plan model, steps suppressed
key_numbers.sub: it must write out its plan to solve at all

charts.chart_plain_title: Correct plans beat answers; wrong plans do worse than either
charts.chart_read: Each group is a training style: untrained, answers-only, correct-plans, wrong-plans, and correct-plans with the steps suppressed at solving time. Bar height is the share of three-step problems solved; taller is better. Two bars per group: best of many tries, and a single deployable attempt.
charts.chart_takeaway: The correct-plan bar is tallest, while the wrong-plan bar sinks below the answers-only bar — so teaching bad reasoning steps hurts more than teaching nothing extra.

charts.chart_plain_title: Can the model pick the right next step on its own?
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by training style: untrained, answers-only, correct-plans. Height is how often the model picks the correct next single step without fully solving; the low reference line marks random-guess level. Two bars per group: without writing steps, and with steps written. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The answers-trained bars rise a little above the random-guess line, while the correct-plan bars read zero because that measurement could not be finished — leaving single-step foresight unresolved.

charts.chart_plain_title: The model's own explanations don't help; genuine plans do
charts.chart_read: Three training styles on the same problems with identical solution code, differing only in the steps shown: answers-only, the model's own after-the-fact explanations, and an explicit correct plan. Bar height is the share of problems solved; taller is better. Two bars: best of many tries, and a single attempt.
charts.chart_takeaway: The model's-own-explanations bar matches the answers-only bar, while the explicit-correct-plan bar stands about two-and-a-half times taller — the quality of the steps is what pays off.

qwen35_4b_banking_installs_structure.concept_primer: Solving these multi-step puzzles takes two things, like a recipe: the right sequence of steps (the blueprint) and the right specific numbers to plug into each step. Getting the blueprint is the hard part.
qwen35_4b_banking_installs_structure.plain_question: Can a short training tune-up teach the model the correct step-by-step blueprint for these puzzles, the part it could not figure out on its own?
qwen35_4b_banking_installs_structure.plain_answer: Yes. On fresh puzzles it never trained on, the tune-up took the model from essentially never getting the blueprint right to getting it right about half the time, versus roughly never for the untrained model.
qwen35_4b_banking_installs_structure.why_it_matters: If you must answer in one pass, this tune-up is worth it: it teaches the step-sequence the model cannot invent alone. But if you can test candidate answers with a checker, exhaustive search fills that role and the model's blueprint adds little.

key_numbers.label: Right step-sequence, brand-new puzzles
key_numbers.sub: untrained model vs after the tune-up

key_numbers.label: Fully solved outright
key_numbers.sub: needs both the right steps and right numbers

key_numbers.label: Solved when a checker tests options
key_numbers.sub: trying every blueprint, no model needed

charts.chart_plain_title: Untrained model never gets the blueprint; the tune-up lifts it to about half
charts.chart_read: Bars group three success measures: solving a puzzle on the first try, solving it within a few tries, and just getting the step-order right regardless of exact numbers. One color is the untrained model, the other after the tune-up; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The untrained bars sit flat at zero across all three measures, while the tuned-up model reaches about half on getting the step-order right, so the tune-up installs the missing blueprint.

charts.chart_plain_title: When a checker can test options, brute search beats the model's blueprint
charts.chart_read: Three bars show the share of new puzzles fully solved under three recipes: the tuned model answering in one pass, the model's blueprint with numbers filled in by testing, and trying every possible blueprint with numbers filled by testing. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The try-every-blueprint bar towers near fully-solved, far above the model's own blueprint recipe, meaning exhaustive search makes the model unnecessary once you can test candidate answers.

qwen35_4b_banking_thinking_stack.verdict_tag: Two boosts add up only on easy steps
qwen35_4b_banking_thinking_stack.concept_primer: Picture two ways to help someone solve a multi-step puzzle: drilling them on worked examples beforehand, and giving them time to think it through before answering. This test checks whether using both at once beats either alone.
qwen35_4b_banking_thinking_stack.plain_question: If you both drill the model on examples and give it time to think, do the two boosts add up on a step-by-step puzzle?
qwen35_4b_banking_thinking_stack.plain_answer: It depends. On easy steps where the goal is one move away, the two boosts add up cleanly and together reach the highest score. On hard steps three moves out, only drilling helps; thinking time adds nothing.
qwen35_4b_banking_thinking_stack.why_it_matters: When the answer is close, it pays to stack thinking time on top of drilling. For harder look-ahead moves, lean on drilling alone; thinking time won't plan ahead until the model is actually trained to reason through such steps.

key_numbers.label: Easy step: no help to both boosts
key_numbers.sub: picking the right next move when the goal is one step away

key_numbers.label: Hard step: drilling alone
key_numbers.sub: drilling raises correct picks when the goal is three steps away

key_numbers.label: Hard step: adding thinking time
key_numbers.sub: thinking time adds nothing to planning far-off moves

charts.chart_plain_title: Combining two boosts helps on easy steps, not on hard planning
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by four setups: no help, thinking-time only, drilling only, and both together. Two colors mark an easy step (goal one move away) and a hard step (goal three moves away). Taller means more correct next-move picks; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: For the easy step the 'both' bar is tallest, about the two single boosts added together; the hard-step bars barely rise once drilling is on, so thinking time adds nothing there.

qwen35_4b_bucket_belief_probe_ranker.verdict_tag: Trained question-picker barely beats the simple rule
qwen35_4b_bucket_belief_probe_ranker.concept_primer: Think of Twenty Questions: to find a hidden answer, you pick tests that split the remaining possibilities. A good test rules out many wrong answers at once, so fewer rounds are needed.
qwen35_4b_bucket_belief_probe_ranker.plain_question: Can we train the model to choose smarter narrowing tests than a simple fixed rule already picks?
qwen35_4b_bucket_belief_probe_ranker.plain_answer: No. After training, the model solved 50% of puzzles in three tests, essentially tying the 49% from a simple fixed rule. A strategy allowed to peek at the answer reaches 87%, so almost none of that headroom was captured.
qwen35_4b_bucket_belief_probe_ranker.why_it_matters: Picking better narrowing tests from a fixed shortlist is not something training unlocks here. Real gains need a richer view of the puzzle state or freedom to invent new tests, not just re-ranking the same options.

key_numbers.label: Puzzles solved by a simple fixed rule
key_numbers.sub: share cracked in three tests, the baseline to beat

key_numbers.label: Puzzles solved after training the picker
key_numbers.sub: barely moved, not a real improvement

key_numbers.label: Solved by a strategy that peeks at the answer
key_numbers.sub: the best-possible ceiling, mostly unreached

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of puzzles solved in three tests, by strategy
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one strategy; height is the share of puzzles fully solved after three narrowing tests, so taller is better. The left bars are the simple rule and the untrained and trained model; the two right bars are best-possible strategies that peek at the hidden answer.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained bar sits almost level with the simple-rule bar and far below the answer-peeking bars, showing training added almost nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Solve rate split by puzzle type
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped into two puzzle families, one easier and one harder; within each group, colored bars compare the strategies, and height is the share solved, so taller is better. The answer-peeking strategies are the reference for how much room exists.
charts.chart_takeaway: On the easy family everyone nearly maxes out, but on the hard family the trained model stays near the floor while the peeking strategy climbs, showing the gap lives there and stayed unclaimed.

qwen35_4b_code_abi_compiler_heldout_prim.verdict_tag: Prebuilt toolbox failed on new tasks
qwen35_4b_code_abi_compiler_heldout_prim.concept_primer: Think of a frozen toolbox of reusable code building blocks: the test is whether parts assembled for practice problems can also build brand-new problems the toolbox never saw.
qwen35_4b_code_abi_compiler_heldout_prim.plain_question: Can a fixed set of reusable code building blocks still solve brand-new coding problems it was never tuned for?
qwen35_4b_code_abi_compiler_heldout_prim.plain_answer: No. The toolbox solved 84% of the familiar practice problems but only 14% of brand-new ones, so the planned model-training step was called off before it started because there was nothing meaningful left to measure.
qwen35_4b_code_abi_compiler_heldout_prim.why_it_matters: Before teaching a model to assemble reusable parts, first prove the parts actually cover unseen problems; otherwise you would just be measuring a tiny, familiar slice, not real reuse.

key_numbers.label: New problems the toolbox could solve
key_numbers.sub: familiar practice problems vs brand-new ones

key_numbers.label: New problems, retested on random fresh batches
key_numbers.sub: three random samples, all near the low result

key_numbers.label: Solutions that looked right but were actually wrong
key_numbers.sub: familiar problems vs new problems

charts.chart_plain_title: Reusable toolbox stops working on brand-new problems
charts.chart_read: Bars group three problem sets (familiar practice, brand-new, and a training pool). Taller blue bars mean more problems solved (better); taller second bars mean more answers that looked right but were wrong (worse).
charts.chart_takeaway: The solve rate cliff-drops from a tall calibration bar to a stub on new problems, while the wrong-but-convincing bar jumps up on those same new problems.

charts.chart_plain_title: Every kind of coding task collapses the same way
charts.chart_read: Bars group five task categories, each with a familiar-problem bar and a new-problem bar; taller means more problems solved (better). Compare the two bars within each category.
charts.chart_takeaway: In every category the new-problem bar shrinks to a fraction of the familiar one, even for the category that started at 100% solved, so no task type escapes the drop.

charts.chart_plain_title: Fresh random batches confirm the poor result is real
charts.chart_read: Three bars, one per randomly drawn batch of new problems; height is the share of problems the toolbox solved, where taller is better. A flat, low set of bars means the weak result was not just bad luck.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three random batches land within a narrow low band near 18%, confirming the original low score was typical rather than an unlucky draw.

qwen35_4b_code_abi_oracle_coverage_ladde.verdict_tag: Kit can express most tasks, not auto-solve them
qwen35_4b_code_abi_oracle_coverage_ladde.concept_primer: Imagine a box of pre-built, individually tested code pieces—like a kit of snap-together blocks. The question: can every coding task's solution be built by combining pieces from this fixed kit?
qwen35_4b_code_abi_oracle_coverage_ladde.plain_question: Can a fixed kit of pre-tested code building blocks express most everyday Python coding tasks?
qwen35_4b_code_abi_oracle_coverage_ladde.plain_answer: Mostly. The full kit can build 84% of 160 tasks, up from 13% with a tiny kit. But automatically picking the correct build works only 59% of the time, so the kit is a foundation, not a finished solver.
qwen35_4b_code_abi_oracle_coverage_ladde.why_it_matters: A shared kit of verified building blocks is worth investing in, but you must still check each candidate against real tests before trusting it. Never rely on a single passing example.

key_numbers.label: Tasks the kit can build
key_numbers.sub: share of 160 tasks, tiny kit vs full kit

key_numbers.label: First plausible build is right
key_numbers.sub: of tasks, using the full kit

key_numbers.label: Fooled by a single example test
key_numbers.sub: tasks that passed one test but were wrong, tiny vs full kit

key_numbers.label: Solved by a single block
key_numbers.sub: covered tasks needing no assembly

charts.chart_plain_title: Bigger kit builds more tasks, but auto-picks the right one less reliably
charts.chart_read: Left to right the building-block kit grows from tiny to full. For each kit, one bar shows how many of 160 tasks the kit can build at all, the other how often the first plausible build is actually right. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both bars rise as the kit grows, but the 'first plausible build is right' bar stays far below the 'can be built' bar—expressible does not mean auto-solved.

charts.chart_plain_title: Coverage stays high across task types except the catch-all bucket
charts.chart_read: Each group is a task category. One bar is how many tasks in that category the full kit can build, the other how often the first plausible build is right. Taller is better; bars are shares within each category.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every category clears roughly four-fifths coverage except the mixed 'other' bucket, where both building and auto-picking drop sharply.

charts.chart_plain_title: A single example test fools far fewer builds as the kit grows
charts.chart_read: Left to right the kit grows from tiny to full. Each bar is the share of tasks where a build passed the one shown example yet was still wrong. Here lower is better—fewer tasks fooled by a single test.
charts.chart_takeaway: The fooled-by-one-test bar falls from 58% to 9% as the kit grows, but real checking is still needed since most individual builds remain wrong.

charts.chart_plain_title: Most solved tasks need just one building block
charts.chart_read: Each bar is how many of the 134 solved tasks were built using a chain of that many blocks: a single block, two chained, or three. Taller means more tasks at that complexity.
charts.chart_takeaway: The single-block bar towers over the rest—94 of 134 tasks need just one piece, so genuine multi-block assembly covers only 40 tasks.

qwen35_4b_code_confidence.verdict_tag: Self-rating picks better code without running it
qwen35_4b_code_confidence.concept_primer: After this small AI writes a short program, you can ask it whether the answer is correct and read how sure it sounds — like a student's gut feeling after a test about which answers they got right.
qwen35_4b_code_confidence.plain_question: When a small AI writes code, can it sense which of its own answers are actually right, and does that gut feeling help pick the best one out of several tries?
qwen35_4b_code_confidence.plain_answer: Yes. The model's self-rating separates its correct programs from wrong ones and, used to choose among eight attempts, picks better than simply going with the most common answer — all without running any code. Running even one provided test still beats the self-rating, though.
qwen35_4b_code_confidence.why_it_matters: When you cannot run tests, have the model rate its own answers and keep the ones it trusts most: you will pick better code and can flag shaky answers to hold back. But whenever a test exists, run it first.

key_numbers.label: Picking the right program out of eight tries
key_numbers.sub: going with the most common answer versus the model's own self-rating

key_numbers.label: Accuracy when it answers only what it trusts
key_numbers.sub: keeping just the third of answers the model is most confident on

key_numbers.label: Picking the right program by running one test
key_numbers.sub: the strongest option when a test is available to check against

charts.chart_plain_title: Picking one code answer from eight tries: which method picks best
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a way to pick one of eight code attempts; bar height is how often the chosen program actually works — taller is better. Methods run from random guessing, through reading the model's confidence, to running the provided test.
charts.chart_takeaway: The self-rating bar rises above both the random and most-common-answer bars, so reading the model's confidence picks better code with nothing run — though running the test rises higher.

charts.chart_plain_title: Is it real self-knowledge, or just longer answers?
charts.chart_read: Each bar shows how well a signal separates the model's correct programs from its wrong ones within the same problem; taller is better. Two bars read the model's confidence; the third just measures how long the answer is.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both confidence bars tower over the answer-length bar, so the model's self-knowledge is genuine — not just a habit of writing longer, wordier code.

charts.chart_plain_title: Answer only what it trusts, and accuracy climbs
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many answers the model still attempts as it drops the ones it doubts; the vertical axis is accuracy on what it kept. Higher and toward the left is better; a flat line shows no filtering.
charts.chart_takeaway: As the model drops the answers it doubts (moving left), the self-rating line climbs far above the flat no-filtering line, reaching near-perfect accuracy on what it keeps.

qwen35_4b_commit_slot_jacobian_value_tra.verdict_tag: Template restored answers but the choice stayed unreliable
qwen35_4b_commit_slot_jacobian_value_tra.concept_primer: Imagine handing someone a form that starts "My answer is: ___" instead of a blank page. The starter forces them to commit to one answer rather than write a rambling essay that never lands on a choice.
qwen35_4b_commit_slot_jacobian_value_tra.plain_question: If we let the model think for a while, then force it to fill in a blank with one of twelve set words, does its thinking reliably point to the right word?
qwen35_4b_commit_slot_jacobian_value_tra.plain_answer: No. Forcing a fill-in-the-blank starter did make the model actually commit to an answer instead of rambling: an allowed word was its natural top pick most of the time. But its thinking nudged toward the right word only slightly and too unevenly to trust, so no deeper measurement could open.
qwen35_4b_commit_slot_jacobian_value_tra.why_it_matters: If you need a model to commit to a set answer, a fill-in-the-blank starter reliably forces the format. But do not assume its reasoning points to the right choice; measure that separately before building anything on top of it.

key_numbers.label: Gave any usable answer
key_numbers.sub: plain rambling vs the fill-in-the-blank starter

key_numbers.label: Picked the right word, longest thinking
key_numbers.sub: scrambled thinking words vs real ordered thinking

key_numbers.label: Confidence on allowed answer words
key_numbers.sub: share of the model's certainty landing on a valid answer

charts.chart_plain_title: How often thinking picked the right word, at three thinking lengths
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by how long the model thought, shortest to longest left to right. Within each group: real ordered thinking, no thinking at all, and the model's own thinking words scrambled. Taller means more right answers.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only at the longest thinking length does the real-thinking bar clear both the no-thinking and scrambled bars, and even there the edge is small and shaky.

charts.chart_plain_title: The fill-in-the-blank starter made the model actually answer
charts.chart_read: Three measures taken at the longest thinking length. The first two show how strongly an allowed answer word was the model's natural top pick with the starter; the last shows how often plain rambling produced any usable answer. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two tall left bars beside the tiny right bar show the starter forced real answers where free-form rambling almost never produced one.

qwen35_4b_commit_slot_semantic_power_rep.verdict_tag: Ordered reasoning reliably improved the answer choice
qwen35_4b_commit_slot_semantic_power_rep.concept_primer: Imagine a student jotting rough working on scratch paper, then circling one of twelve short answer choices. Here a language model does the same: it writes out its reasoning, then must pick a single short word as its final answer.
qwen35_4b_commit_slot_semantic_power_rep.plain_question: When a model writes out its reasoning before picking one short answer, does that reasoning actually help it choose correctly, or would any jumble of the same words do just as well?
qwen35_4b_commit_slot_semantic_power_rep.plain_answer: Yes. Across a large fresh batch of problems, the model's ordered reasoning led to the right answer about 27% of the time, roughly double the 14% it managed when the exact same words were scrambled into random order, and clearly better than answering with no reasoning at all.
qwen35_4b_commit_slot_semantic_power_rep.why_it_matters: When you pin a model down to a fixed set of short answer choices, let it reason in its natural order first. Scrambling that reasoning, or skipping it, throws away a real and sizable chunk of its accuracy.

key_numbers.label: Correct with ordered reasoning
key_numbers.sub: share of problems answered correctly when reasoning stayed in order

key_numbers.label: Correct with the same words scrambled
key_numbers.sub: identical reasoning words shuffled into random order

key_numbers.label: Correct with no reasoning at all
key_numbers.sub: picking an answer straight away

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each answering style picks the right word
charts.chart_read: Four bars, one per answering style along the bottom: ordered reasoning, the same words scrambled, no reasoning, and open free-form answering. Bar height is the share of problems answered correctly, so taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The ordered-reasoning bar stands nearly twice as tall as every other bar, showing that keeping the reasoning in its natural order genuinely drives correct answers.

qwen35_4b_confidence_guided_compute.verdict_tag: Picking the most confident answer beats majority vote
qwen35_4b_confidence_guided_compute.concept_primer: Imagine a student who, with each answer, also says how sure they are, and their "very sure" answers really do turn out right more often. This small model gives that kind of trustworthy confidence rating alongside every answer it produces.
qwen35_4b_confidence_guided_compute.plain_question: When a small model answers the same puzzle a dozen times, can its own sense of which answer it is surest about beat simply taking its most common answer?
qwen35_4b_confidence_guided_compute.plain_answer: Yes. Picking the answer the model was surest about lifted accuracy from about 47% to 62% as more tries were gathered, while taking its most common answer stayed stuck near 48% no matter how many tries, and low confidence flagged the problems worth skipping entirely.
qwen35_4b_confidence_guided_compute.why_it_matters: When running a small model many times on one problem, rank its answers by how sure it is rather than how often each appears, and let low confidence tell you when to stop or hand the problem off.

key_numbers.label: Taking the most common answer
key_numbers.sub: stayed flat no matter how many tries were gathered

key_numbers.label: Taking the most confident answer
key_numbers.sub: rose as more tries were gathered

key_numbers.label: Best possible pick
key_numbers.sub: if a perfect judge always chose the right try

key_numbers.label: Accuracy on the surest problems
key_numbers.sub: after skipping the ones it was unsure about

charts.chart_plain_title: More tries only help if you pick by confidence, not popularity
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many answers you collect per problem, more to the right; the vertical axis is accuracy, higher is better. One line picks the most confident answer, another the most common, and a flat top line marks the best-possible pick.
charts.chart_takeaway: The most-confident line climbs steadily while the most-common line stays flat, so extra tries only pay off when you rank answers by the model's own confidence.

charts.chart_plain_title: When gathering more answers helps, and when it barely can
charts.chart_read: Each problem type shows two bars: accuracy from a single answer, and accuracy from the best of twelve answers. The three groups are routine problems, ones needing recall, and ones needing a brand-new rule. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: For recall problems the best-of-twelve bar jumps far above the single answer, but for brand-new-rule problems both bars stay low, so extra tries barely help there.

qwen35_4b_constrained_coverage_dpo.verdict_tag: Cheaper nudge, but more tries still wins
qwen35_4b_constrained_coverage_dpo.concept_primer: Think of giving the model several tries at each coding problem and counting how many problems get at least one working answer. This experiment nudges the model to try more varied approaches instead of just taking more tries.
qwen35_4b_constrained_coverage_dpo.plain_question: Can a small local nudge get the model to solve more coding problems per batch of tries than just letting it take more tries?
qwen35_4b_constrained_coverage_dpo.plain_answer: It depends. The gentle nudge solved a few more problems than the plain model at the same number of tries, without hurting first-try quality, but it still solved fewer than simply letting the plain model take twice as many tries.
qwen35_4b_constrained_coverage_dpo.why_it_matters: The nudge is a cheap, safe gain that does not degrade quality, but it cannot replace simply taking more tries. The promising next step is mixing several generation styles rather than pushing this one nudge harder.

key_numbers.label: Best coverage, plain model with 8 tries
key_numbers.sub: the bar the nudge did not clear

key_numbers.label: First-try success (plain vs nudged)
