charts.chart_plain_title: Problems solved and first-try wins, by method
charts.chart_read: Bars show, out of 24 practice problems, the share solved by at least one of several tries (taller is better) and the share solved on the very first try. Four setups: plain model with 4 tries, plain with 8 tries, the nudged model with 4 tries, and a scrambled-nudge control.
charts.chart_takeaway: The nudged model's 4-try bar clears the plain 4-try bar and the scrambled control on both measures, but stays below the plain model given 8 tries.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much compute each method burns
charts.chart_read: Bars show total words the model had to process across all 24 problems for each setup; shorter is cheaper and better. The plain 8-try setup is the tallest, the nudged 4-try setup among the shortest.
charts.chart_takeaway: The nudged model uses about half the compute of the 8-try plain run, so its coverage comes far cheaper even though it solves slightly fewer problems.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training difficulty over ten short tuning steps
charts.chart_read: Two lines track how hard the tuning task stays over ten steps, one trained on genuine good-vs-bad examples and one on deliberately scrambled examples; flatter and lower means the model is learning steadily rather than breaking down.
charts.chart_takeaway: The real-example line stays flat and steady near its floor across all ten steps, showing the gentle nudge trained cleanly instead of collapsing.

qwen35_4b_context_composition.verdict_tag: Instructions sharpen judgment but cannot spark invention
qwen35_4b_context_composition.concept_primer: Picture tasting a finished dish. Telling which of two given recipes made it is one skill. Writing the recipe from scratch, having only tasted the dish, is a far harder, completely different skill.
qwen35_4b_context_composition.plain_question: When a small AI must tell which of two programs produced a result, or write a program from scratch, can carefully worded instructions in the prompt make it do the job better?
qwen35_4b_context_composition.plain_answer: It depends. Spelling out the steps to follow in the prompt lifted the model's ability to tell which of two programs made a result, from 74% right to 83%. But no wording helped it write a working program from scratch, which stayed stuck near 8 to 13% right.
qwen35_4b_context_composition.why_it_matters: When you need a small model to compare or judge between options, write out the steps to follow in the prompt; it genuinely pays off. But do not expect prompt wording alone to make it invent solutions from scratch, no matter how you phrase it.

key_numbers.label: Telling two programs apart
key_numbers.sub: plain prompt versus a step-by-step procedure spelled out in the prompt

key_numbers.label: A trained-in skill, reachable versus delivered
key_numbers.sub: right when it answered in the required form, versus right overall; it used the wrong form half the time

key_numbers.label: Writing a program from scratch
key_numbers.sub: no wording, worked examples, or trained skill moved it off the floor

charts.chart_plain_title: A spelled-out procedure helps judging; a trained skill gets blocked by wrong formatting
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is a different setup for telling which of two programs made a result. The three bars show how often the model was right overall, how often it was right when it did answer, and how often it replied in the required form. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two right-hand setups use a model with a simulation skill trained in: their right-when-answering bars are tall, but the required-form bars are short, so overall correctness sinks.

charts.chart_plain_title: No prompt trick helps the model invent a working program from scratch
charts.chart_read: Two bars: a plain model and a version with a simulation skill trained in. Each shows how often it could write a correct program from scratch, given only the result to reproduce. Higher is better; both sit near the floor.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both bars barely rise off the bottom, so even a trained skill plus worked examples leaves writing a program from scratch out of reach.

qwen35_4b_context_local_jacobian_clamp.verdict_tag: A technicality disqualified an otherwise perfect edit
qwen35_4b_context_local_jacobian_clamp.concept_primer: Imagine a lookup table pairing words with numbers. The model first notes which word was chosen, then later looks up its number. Researchers can reach inside the model and quietly swap the noted word for a different one.
qwen35_4b_context_local_jacobian_clamp.plain_question: If you reach inside the model and swap the word it noted as chosen, will it then look up the number belonging to the swapped-in word instead?
qwen35_4b_context_local_jacobian_clamp.plain_answer: Yes. Swapping the noted word made the model both name the new word and look up that new word's number on all 48 tests, while random tweaks changed nothing. But a precision slip in one comparison control formally disqualified the run, so it needs a clean redo.
qwen35_4b_context_local_jacobian_clamp.why_it_matters: Editing a concept where the model first registers it, rather than at the final answer, can genuinely steer its later reasoning. Before trusting such an edit, make sure your comparison controls stay numerically exact, since rounding can quietly break them.

key_numbers.label: Times the swap changed the looked-up number
key_numbers.sub: no edit vs. the concept swap, across 48 tests

key_numbers.label: Times random noise changed the answer
key_numbers.sub: meaningless tweaks left the model unchanged

key_numbers.label: Controls that missed the precision rule
key_numbers.sub: the single slip that disqualified the run

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each edit produced the intended word and number
charts.chart_read: Each group along the bottom is one kind of internal edit. Bars show how often the model produced the intended substitute word (one color) and its intended number (other color). Taller means a stronger, more reliable effect.
charts.chart_takeaway: The genuine concept swaps reach the top on both bars, while the random-noise and surface-word controls stay flat at zero, showing the swap itself drove the answer.

charts.chart_plain_title: How strongly each edit tipped the model toward the substitute answer
charts.chart_read: Each group is one kind of edit. Bars above the center line mean the model leaned toward the substitute word or number; below means it kept the original. Two colors track the named word and its looked-up number.
charts.chart_takeaway: The concept-swap edits push both bars well above the line while the control edits stay below it, meaning the swap flipped a firm preference, not a near-tie.

charts.chart_plain_title: Editing early processing stages works; editing late stages does not
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how deep into the model's processing the edit was applied, left being earliest. The vertical axis is how often the edit redirected the answer. Three lines track the word, its number, and a wrong-word swap.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three lines stay pinned at the top through the early and middle stages, then plunge to zero once the edit lands too late, after the model has already used the information.

qwen35_4b_counterexample_directed_dsl.verdict_tag: Targeted examples help some tasks, not all
qwen35_4b_counterexample_directed_dsl.concept_primer: Imagine fixing a buggy recipe. A coach hands you the exact taste-tests that expose your particular mistake, not random ones. Here a program-fixing model gets such targeted examples: worked cases chosen to reveal its likely wrong guesses.
qwen35_4b_counterexample_directed_dsl.plain_question: When a model must fix a broken program from a few worked examples, does hand-picking examples that expose likely mistakes help more than showing random examples?
qwen35_4b_counterexample_directed_dsl.plain_answer: It depends. Picking targeted examples over random ones lifted correct one-attempt fixes from 51% to 58%. But when the model made several tries and kept the best, targeted examples gave no gain, and one whole task type collapsed to always answering wrong.
qwen35_4b_counterexample_directed_dsl.why_it_matters: Curating worked examples to expose likely mistakes can sharpen a model's single-shot fixes, but it is no cure-all: on some tasks the model locks onto one wrong pattern. Combine curation with letting the model try several times, and watch for collapse.

key_numbers.label: Fixed on the first try
key_numbers.sub: share of broken programs repaired correctly, random vs targeted examples

key_numbers.label: Best of several tries
key_numbers.sub: keeping the best of a few attempts, no gain from targeted examples

key_numbers.label: Hardest task type
key_numbers.sub: one family collapsed to always giving the same wrong answer

charts.chart_plain_title: Targeted examples help single-shot fixes but not best-of-several picking
charts.chart_read: Two situations along the bottom: fixing a program in one attempt, and keeping the best of several attempts. Paired bars compare a model trained on random worked examples versus targeted ones. Taller means more programs fully fixed.
charts.chart_takeaway: The targeted-training bar stands taller for one-attempt fixing but shorter for best-of-several, so the extra care pays off only without retries.

charts.chart_plain_title: Targeted training rescues two task types but wrecks a third
charts.chart_read: Three kinds of program-fixing task along the bottom. Paired bars compare training on random worked examples versus targeted ones, showing the share of programs fully fixed on unseen cases; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On one task the targeted bar drops flat to nothing while hitting full marks on another, showing the method's help is wildly uneven.

charts.chart_plain_title: Scrambled examples hurt more than showing no examples
charts.chart_read: Bottom axis shows what worked examples the model saw while fixing: proper matched examples, none at all, or scrambled ones. Each bar is the share of programs fully fixed on unseen cases; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The scrambled-examples bar is by far the shortest, well below the no-examples bar, proving the model genuinely reads the examples rather than ignoring them.

qwen35_4b_coverage_banking.verdict_tag: Training on its own wins unlocks new problems
qwen35_4b_coverage_banking.concept_primer: Picture the model as an archer whose arrows scatter around wherever it aims. Training it on its own rare bullseyes does not widen the spread — it re-aims the whole cluster onto correct answers, so shots that always used to miss now land.
qwen35_4b_coverage_banking.plain_question: If a small model trains only on the coding problems it already solved on its own, does it just get faster at those, or start solving harder ones it never could?
qwen35_4b_coverage_banking.plain_answer: Both — and the second is the surprise. On easy problems it simply commits to the answer it already knew, lifting first-try success from 60% to 80%. On a harder tier it had cracked only 15% of the time even across 16 tries, self-training tripled that to 45% — on brand-new problems, proposing correct solutions it had never once sampled.
qwen35_4b_coverage_banking.why_it_matters: When a small model stalls, harvest its own checkable correct answers and fine-tune on them before paying for a bigger model or a teacher. Where you have enough verified examples, this lifts the whole ceiling, not just sharpens easy wins.

key_numbers.label: First-try success on easy problems
key_numbers.sub: one answer, no extra sampling — it commits to what it already knew

key_numbers.label: Hard problems solved within 16 tries
key_numbers.sub: tripled, on brand-new problems it never trained on

key_numbers.label: Different programs tried per hard problem
key_numbers.sub: variety fell slightly, yet it solved more — aim shifted, not spread

key_numbers.label: The two hardest tiers
key_numbers.sub: stayed unsolved — no wins existed there to learn from

charts.chart_plain_title: How self-training changed solve rates across four difficulty tiers
charts.chart_read: Four difficulty tiers along the bottom, easiest at left. Each tier shows four bars: the base model's single first answer, the base allowed 16 tries, the self-trained model's single answer, and the self-trained model over 16 tries. Taller means more solved; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: At the easiest tier the self-trained single-answer bar jumps up; at the second tier its 16-try bar triples the base's — a whole tier's ceiling lifted. Deeper tiers stay empty.

charts.chart_plain_title: On hard problems, more tries pay off far more after self-training
charts.chart_read: For the harder tier, the share of problems solved as attempts climb from one to sixteen. The lower, flatter line is the base model; the upper, steeper line is the self-trained model. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The self-trained line sits above the base at every attempt count and keeps climbing, ending at three times the base's solve rate — the ceiling itself rose.

qwen35_4b_coverage_vs_selection.verdict_tag: A smarter picker cannot beat just trying more
qwen35_4b_coverage_vs_selection.concept_primer: Picture solving a puzzle by making many rough attempts, then choosing one to submit. Failing can mean the right attempt was never made at all, or it was made but never chosen.
qwen35_4b_coverage_vs_selection.plain_question: When the model cannot solve a hard puzzle, is it because it never produces a correct attempt, or because it produces one but fails to pick it?
qwen35_4b_coverage_vs_selection.plain_answer: It never produces one. Whenever a correct attempt does appear among many tries, a simple check always grabs it, so picking is free; the failures come entirely from the right attempt never being made.
qwen35_4b_coverage_vs_selection.why_it_matters: Building a cleverer picker is wasted effort here, since picking already works perfectly. To solve harder puzzles, the model must be steered to produce the right attempt in the first place, using better tools or lessons from past solutions.

key_numbers.label: One try vs many tries (medium puzzle)
key_numbers.sub: share solved when you make many attempts instead of one

key_numbers.label: Extra gain from a smarter picker
key_numbers.sub: a simple check already grabs every correct attempt made

key_numbers.label: Deep puzzles, even with many tries
key_numbers.sub: a correct attempt almost never appears no matter how many you make

charts.chart_plain_title: On one puzzle machine, every way of picking lands at the same height
charts.chart_read: Bar height is the share of puzzles solved, taller is better; each cluster is a puzzle depth from easy to hard. The four bars are one try, two smarter picking methods, and the best-possible ceiling; the three picking bars sit at identical heights.
charts.chart_takeaway: By depth 3 and 4 every bar collapses to near zero together, showing the right attempt is simply never made no matter how you pick.

charts.chart_plain_title: On the other machine, picking many attempts far beats one try
charts.chart_read: Bar height is the share of puzzles solved, taller is better; each cluster is a puzzle depth. The single-try bar is short while the two picking bars and the best-possible ceiling all rise together and match exactly.
charts.chart_takeaway: The three picking bars tower over the single-try bar yet land exactly on the ceiling, meaning picking adds nothing beyond simply making more attempts.

charts.chart_plain_title: More attempts help on easy puzzles but stall on deep ones
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many attempts you make, the vertical axis is the chance a correct attempt appears, higher is better; each line is a puzzle depth, shallow on top and deep at the bottom.
charts.chart_takeaway: The deep-puzzle lines stay flat and near the floor however many attempts you make, so extra tries cannot conjure a correct attempt that never shows up.

qwen35_4b_crossfamily_laws.verdict_tag: Two skills hold everywhere, one hinges on material
qwen35_4b_crossfamily_laws.concept_primer: Picture a recipe: a list of tiny steps done in order. A small AI must handle recipes built from three completely unrelated kinds of material, letting us see which of its strengths hold up everywhere.
qwen35_4b_crossfamily_laws.plain_question: When a small AI shines or struggles at these recipes, is that a fixed trait of the AI itself, or just an accident of the one material we happened to test?
qwen35_4b_crossfamily_laws.plain_answer: It depends. Two things hold in every material: the AI is near-perfect at following a recipe it is handed, and it reliably fails to guess a hidden recipe from before-and-after examples once several steps stack up. But running steps in its head swings entirely on the material.
qwen35_4b_crossfamily_laws.why_it_matters: When you hand the AI an explicit plan, trust it to carry it out. Do not expect it to invent the procedure from examples alone. And keep whatever it tracks in its head simple, or offload that tracking.

key_numbers.label: Following a recipe you are handed
key_numbers.sub: same in all three materials, at every depth

key_numbers.label: Guessing a hidden recipe from examples
key_numbers.sub: one step vs several steps stacked up

key_numbers.label: Steps in your head, compact-number material
key_numbers.sub: state stays easy to track

key_numbers.label: Steps in your head, shifting-text material
key_numbers.sub: state too hard to track even early

charts.chart_plain_title: Following a handed recipe stays near-perfect in every material
charts.chart_read: The left-to-right axis is how many steps are stacked in the recipe; the up-down axis is how often the AI correctly wrote out a recipe it was handed. Three lines, one per material; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three lines hug the top from one step to many. Handed an explicit plan, the AI carries it out no matter the material.

charts.chart_plain_title: Doing steps in your head depends entirely on the material
charts.chart_read: The left-to-right axis is how many steps are stacked; the up-down axis is how often the AI got the final result right by working steps through in its head, with no writing. Three lines by material; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The lines fan far apart: compact-number material stays high while shifting-text material sits at the bottom even at one step. Trackability, not depth, decides.

charts.chart_plain_title: Guessing a hidden recipe from examples collapses everywhere
charts.chart_read: The left-to-right axis is how many steps are stacked; the up-down axis is how often the AI recovered a hidden recipe using only before-and-after examples. Three lines by material; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line starts moderate and drops toward the floor within a couple of steps. Inventing the procedure from examples fails in all three materials once steps stack.

qwen35_4b_crosssubstrate_structure.concept_primer: Think of each puzzle as a recipe: you first choose the right sequence of steps, then fill in the right amounts. Choosing the sequence is the hard part; the amounts fall out easily once the steps are fixed.
qwen35_4b_crosssubstrate_structure.plain_question: When a small AI model tackles these step-by-step puzzles, is its real difficulty picking the right sequence of steps rather than the exact amounts, and does that hold across very different puzzle types?
qwen35_4b_crosssubstrate_structure.plain_answer: Yes. Across three very different puzzle types, the model almost never finds the right sequence of steps on its own, and that, not choosing amounts, is where it fails. But an outside tool that searches through possible step-sequences and tests each one solves nearly every puzzle.
qwen35_4b_crosssubstrate_structure.why_it_matters: If a small model keeps failing multi-step puzzles, do not assume it needs more training on final answers. The gap is proposing the right sequence of steps, so wrap it with a search-and-check tool instead.

key_numbers.label: Model working alone
key_numbers.sub: share of puzzles it solves, across all three types

key_numbers.label: Model plus a step-search tool
key_numbers.sub: share solved, same three puzzle types

key_numbers.label: Right steps handed over
key_numbers.sub: solved once the sequence is given and only amounts remain

key_numbers.label: Random step-sequences tried
key_numbers.sub: guessing the steps rarely works

charts.chart_plain_title: The model alone fails; an outside search tool solves nearly every puzzle
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars colored by puzzle type (string, register, list); bar height is the share of puzzles solved. The two left bars are the model alone, the third hands it the right steps, the rightmost is an outside search tool. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two leftmost bars sit near the floor while the rightmost bars reach the top: the model alone fails, but the search tool solves almost everything, on all three types.

charts.chart_plain_title: Guessing random step-sequences rarely solves the puzzles, on all three types
charts.chart_read: Three lines, one per puzzle type. The horizontal axis is how many random step-sequences are tried; the vertical axis is the share of puzzles solved. All lines stay low, well below solving everything, even as more sequences are tried.
charts.chart_takeaway: Even at the far right the register line climbs highest yet stays well below full solving: random steps seldom work, so the exact right sequence genuinely matters.

qwen35_4b_decompose_compose_frontier.verdict_tag: Search crosses the wall, planning doesn't
qwen35_4b_decompose_compose_frontier.concept_primer: Faced with a hard three-step puzzle, the model can guess the whole answer in one leap, or break it into single moves and let a calculator check each move before the next.
qwen35_4b_decompose_compose_frontier.plain_question: Can breaking a hard multi-step task into single checked steps let this small model solve puzzles it fails to crack in one leap?
qwen35_4b_decompose_compose_frontier.plain_answer: It depends. Breaking the task into checked steps solves about three times more of the hardest puzzles than one leap does, but a blind step-by-step search does just as well, so the win comes from the step-by-step structure plus the calculator, not the model's own judgment.
qwen35_4b_decompose_compose_frontier.why_it_matters: When a small model stalls on multi-step problems, wrap it in a step-by-step search with an external checker instead of trusting one big attempt, then train it on the solutions found to lock in modest gains.

key_numbers.label: Hardest-task solve rate
key_numbers.sub: one leap vs step-by-step search

key_numbers.label: Genuinely deep tasks solved
key_numbers.sub: one leap never solved these; step search sometimes did

key_numbers.label: One-shot skill after self-training
key_numbers.sub: practicing on the found solutions

charts.chart_plain_title: Solving in one leap vs breaking the task into checked steps
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of hidden tests solved (taller is better) for two-step and three-step tasks, split three ways: solving in one leap, model-guided step-by-step search, and blind step-by-step search.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both step-search bars roughly triple the one-leap bar on the hardest tasks, but blind search matches the model-guided one, so the structure does the work.

charts.chart_plain_title: Solve rate as you allow more checking effort
charts.chart_read: Lines track solve rate (up is better) as the allowed effort grows from left to right, with separate lines for model-guided and blind search at each task difficulty.
charts.chart_takeaway: The guided line climbs fast then flattens early while blind search keeps rising to catch or pass it, meaning guidance saves effort but hits a ceiling.

charts.chart_plain_title: One-shot skill after training on the found solutions
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars compare the untrained model against two self-trained runs across four one-shot solving measures; taller is better everywhere.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every self-trained bar sits above the untrained one, showing that practicing the found solutions modestly lifts the model's own one-shot solving.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much of the hard win was truly hard?
charts.chart_read: Two bars show the step-search solve rate on genuinely deep tasks versus tasks that only looked deep but really need fewer steps; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The look-deep bar is full while the truly-deep bar is short, so most of the headline win came from tasks that were easier than their label suggested.

qwen35_4b_deployable_information_ceiling.verdict_tag: The bottleneck is missing clues, not strategy
qwen35_4b_deployable_information_ceiling.concept_primer: Picture a game of 20 questions to identify a hidden rule: a fair player picks the smartest next question without knowing the answer, while a cheating player who already knows the answer sets the ceiling.
qwen35_4b_deployable_information_ceiling.plain_question: When the model struggles to pin down a hidden rule, is it because it lacks a clever questioning strategy, or because it just needs more clues?
qwen35_4b_deployable_information_ceiling.plain_answer: It needs more clues. In the hardest setup the fair player scores only 4% while the cheating player hits 74%, but simply allowing more questions lifts the fair player to 86%, nearly matching the cheat and closing most of the gap.
qwen35_4b_deployable_information_ceiling.why_it_matters: When a model fails at this kind of deduction, spend effort feeding it more evidence or more questioning steps rather than hunting for a cleverer questioning strategy; the fair strategy is already close to the best possible.

key_numbers.label: Fair player, few clues and questions
key_numbers.sub: hardest setup: 4 clues, only 3 questions allowed

key_numbers.label: Fair player, more questions allowed
key_numbers.sub: same 4 clues but 10 questions allowed

key_numbers.label: Fair player, more starting clues
key_numbers.sub: 16 clues up front, still only 3 questions

key_numbers.label: Cheating player's ceiling
key_numbers.sub: in the hardest setup, knowing the answer in advance

charts.chart_plain_title: Fair player nearly catches the cheat once given more clues or questions
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars compare the fair player against the cheating player who already knows the answer, across three setups from hardest to easier. Height is how often the hidden rule is correctly identified; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: In the leftmost hardest setup the fair player's bar is tiny next to the cheat's, but in the other two setups the two bars stand almost level, showing more clues or questions closes the gap.

charts.chart_plain_title: More questions steadily lift the fair player toward the ceiling
charts.chart_read: Two lines track identification accuracy as the number of allowed questions rises left to right, holding starting clues fixed. One line is the fair player, the other the cheating player; higher lines are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The fair player's line climbs steadily from bottom to top while the cheat's line flattens early, meaning extra questions keep helping the fair player long after the cheat has maxed out.

charts.chart_plain_title: More starting clues sharply raise the fair player
charts.chart_read: Two lines show identification accuracy as the number of clues shown up front increases left to right, with the number of questions held low. One line is the fair player, the other the cheating player; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The fair player's line rises steeply and converges toward the cheat's line, showing that each batch of extra clues shrinks the gap between them from wide to nearly nothing.

qwen35_4b_depth3_dose_response.verdict_tag: More worked examples keep lifting the ceiling
qwen35_4b_depth3_dose_response.concept_primer: Think of teaching by worked examples: the model couldn't solve a certain three-step chained puzzle on its own, so we trained it on solved cases that a separate search program found, then tested it on brand-new puzzles.
qwen35_4b_depth3_dose_response.plain_question: When a small model fails a hard three-step puzzle, is that a permanent limit, or does feeding it more worked examples keep helping?
qwen35_4b_depth3_dose_response.plain_answer: It keeps helping. Solving these new puzzles was a data problem, not a hard ceiling: as we added more worked examples, the share of new puzzles solved rose steadily with no sign of leveling off, and the skill became usable in a single try.
qwen35_4b_depth3_dose_response.why_it_matters: When a small model stalls on hard multi-step tasks, don't assume it can't. Feed it more verified worked examples from a search tool and the skill grows, eventually working in one shot.

key_numbers.label: New puzzles solved with several tries
key_numbers.sub: no examples vs the most examples

key_numbers.label: Solved in one try, no scratch-work
key_numbers.sub: usable out of the box, at the most examples

key_numbers.label: Overlap between test and training puzzles
key_numbers.sub: every solved puzzle was genuinely new

charts.chart_plain_title: Solve rate keeps climbing as more worked examples are added
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by how many worked examples the model trained on, from none up to the most. Within each group, colored bars show different solving styles; taller means more new puzzles solved. Right and taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The bars climb steadily from left to right with no flattening, and the one-try 'no scratch-work' bars appear only at the largest training amount.

charts.chart_plain_title: Untrained model stays at zero while trained ones fan out above it
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many attempts the model gets per puzzle; the vertical axis is the share of new puzzles solved. Each line is a training amount. Higher and to the right is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The untrained line lies flat on zero, while each higher training amount fans out above it, the top line reaching over a third of new puzzles solved.

qwen35_4b_depth_scaling_controls.verdict_tag: More varied practice keeps paying off
qwen35_4b_depth_scaling_controls.concept_primer: Picture teaching the model to solve multi-step puzzles by having it practice on worked examples that a simple search program discovered on its own. More examples, and more varied ones, means more practice.
qwen35_4b_depth_scaling_controls.plain_question: Does feeding the model ever more varied self-found practice examples keep improving it, or does it soon hit a ceiling?
qwen35_4b_depth_scaling_controls.plain_answer: Yes, it kept improving. Doubling the variety of practice examples lifted the solve rate from 38% to 54% with no ceiling in sight, and controls show the boost came from more variety, not just more repetition of the same examples.
qwen35_4b_depth_scaling_controls.why_it_matters: When self-training a small model, invest in gathering more genuinely different solved examples rather than just running more training passes over the same few. Variety is what actually installs new skill.

key_numbers.label: Solve rate with several tries
key_numbers.sub: after doubling practice variety

key_numbers.label: Single best-answer solve rate
key_numbers.sub: the deployable, one-shot number

key_numbers.label: Same practice, more variety
key_numbers.sub: gain from variety alone, at equal effort

key_numbers.label: One step harder puzzle
key_numbers.sub: recipe repeats a level deeper, weakly

charts.chart_plain_title: More varied practice keeps raising the solve rate, no ceiling yet
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many different problem types the model practiced on; the vertical axis is its solve rate. The upper line allows several attempts, the lower line takes the single best answer. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines keep climbing to the right with no flattening, meaning more varied practice keeps adding skill with no ceiling reached yet.

charts.chart_plain_title: The gain comes from variety, not from more repetition
charts.chart_read: Three training setups shown as bars, taller is better: a little practice, then a much larger amount repeating just 40 problem types, then that same large amount spread across 640 different types.
charts.chart_takeaway: The bar jumps only when practice covers more different problem types, showing variety, not extra repetition, drives the improvement.

charts.chart_plain_title: The recipe repeats one step harder, but only weakly
charts.chart_read: Three setups for a harder, deeper puzzle, taller is better: untrained model, model trained only on the easier level, and one also given some harder examples. Each has a several-attempts bar and a single-best-answer bar.
charts.chart_takeaway: Adding harder examples nearly triples the several-attempts bar, but the single-answer bar stays flat, so the new skill installs only weakly.

qwen35_4b_depth_wall_anatomy.verdict_tag: Executes told steps perfectly, cannot deduce them
qwen35_4b_depth_wall_anatomy.concept_primer: Solving a puzzle from a few input-output examples takes two different skills: figuring out which steps were applied to make the output, and carrying out steps once someone hands you the recipe. They are not the same skill.
qwen35_4b_depth_wall_anatomy.plain_question: When a small AI fails to solve a multi-step transformation shown only as a few examples, is it failing to figure out the steps, or failing to carry them out?
qwen35_4b_depth_wall_anatomy.plain_answer: It's failing to figure out the steps. Told the recipe, the model carries out chains of two, three, even four operations almost flawlessly. Left to deduce those same steps from examples alone, it succeeds only just above random guessing past the very first step.
qwen35_4b_depth_wall_anatomy.why_it_matters: If a small model stalls on multi-step tasks, don't spend effort teaching it to execute; it already can. Instead hand it the plan: let cheap external tools search for the steps, and let the model run them.

key_numbers.label: Told the recipe, then run it
key_numbers.sub: solved, even for four-step chains

key_numbers.label: Left to figure out the steps
key_numbers.sub: solved on those same tasks

key_numbers.label: Figuring out one single step
key_numbers.sub: the skill that collapses once steps are chained

charts.chart_plain_title: Given the steps, the model solves; asked to find them, it fails
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is a task type: chains of two to four steps, with or without steps that discard information. Three bars per cluster: solving from examples alone, being told the steps, and being shown the in-between results. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The told-the-steps bars stand near the top across every task type while the other two hug the floor; that whole gap is the difficulty.

charts.chart_plain_title: Solve rate falls off a cliff after the second step
charts.chart_read: Each line tracks share of tasks solved as chains get longer, one line per number of information-discarding steps mixed in. The horizontal axis is chain length; higher on the vertical axis is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line plunges to the floor by the third step, and lines with more information-discarding steps sit no lower; step count, not step type, drives the fall.

charts.chart_plain_title: Even pre-chopped steps and yes/no checks fade as chains grow
charts.chart_read: Two bars per task type: solving when each step is handed over already separated, and simply judging yes/no whether a stated recipe matches the examples. The horizontal axis is task type by chain length; taller bars are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both bars sag as chains lengthen, and the yes/no check sinks toward a coin-flip; even simplified versions of the job break down with length.

charts.chart_plain_title: Many 'three-step' tasks were really shortcuts in disguise
charts.chart_read: Two bars comparing how often a step-by-step search solver cracked each task type: tasks labelled three-step that secretly collapse to fewer steps, versus genuine three-step tasks. Taller means solved more often.
charts.chart_takeaway: The disguised-shortcut bar is full height while the genuine three-step bar is a sliver; the apparent depth limit was mostly a mislabeling of easy tasks.

qwen35_4b_diversity_keyed_coverage_gate.concept_primer: Picture a capable student who fails a problem only because they tried one approach and stopped; ask them for several different attempts and one usually works.
qwen35_4b_diversity_keyed_coverage_gate.plain_question: When our small model fails a coding task, is it because it truly can't do it, or just because it didn't try enough different approaches?
qwen35_4b_diversity_keyed_coverage_gate.plain_answer: Mostly the latter. A handful of tries solved 70% of tasks; asking for many varied attempts lifted that to 89%, rescuing most earlier failures. The ability was there — it just needed more varied shots.
qwen35_4b_diversity_keyed_coverage_gate.why_it_matters: Before retraining a small model to fix failures, first spend effort on diverse retries — much of the apparent weakness is untapped and cheaply recoverable. Re-editing wrong answers, by contrast, added nothing here.

key_numbers.label: Tasks solved with a few tries
key_numbers.sub: handful of attempts per task

key_numbers.label: Tasks solved with many varied tries
key_numbers.sub: after lots of varied attempts

key_numbers.label: Earlier failures later fixed
key_numbers.sub: originally-missed tasks recovered

key_numbers.label: Fixed by re-editing wrong answers
key_numbers.sub: the repair step added nothing

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of tasks solved as retries grow more varied
charts.chart_read: Bars left to right go from a few tries to many varied tries; height is the share of tasks solved, and taller is better. The rightmost bars include a step that re-edits wrong answers.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars climb steadily from 70% to 89%, but the last two are identical — more varied sampling keeps helping while re-editing wrong answers adds nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Originally-missed tasks rescued by each retry strategy
charts.chart_read: Each bar counts how many of the 24 originally-failed tasks a strategy rescued; taller is better. The middle bars are single decoding styles, the right bars combine several.
charts.chart_takeaway: Combining three decoding styles rescues 13 tasks versus 10 for the best single style, showing different styles rescue different tasks rather than the same ones.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tasks solved versus total computing effort spent
charts.chart_read: The line moves right as more computing effort is spent and up as more tasks get solved; up-and-to-the-right is better. It traces the no-training ceiling to beat.
charts.chart_takeaway: The line rises then flattens, so extra effort keeps solving more tasks but with clearly diminishing returns at the high end.

qwen35_4b_error_localization.verdict_tag: Confidence pinpoints where the model first slips
qwen35_4b_error_localization.concept_primer: Imagine solving a long chain of steps, where each answer feeds the next. At every step you feel more or less sure. When you first take a wrong turn, your certainty tends to flinch, and that flinch can be watched.
qwen35_4b_error_localization.plain_question: When a small AI works through a long chain of steps and makes a mistake, can its own moment-to-moment certainty tell us which step it first went wrong on?
qwen35_4b_error_localization.plain_answer: Yes. As the model works a chain, its certainty dips at exactly the step where it first goes wrong, not just at late steps. That beats guessing from step position and lets you redo only from the mistake, fixing over half of single-slip chains for much less work.
qwen35_4b_error_localization.why_it_matters: If a model's step-by-step certainty flags where it first went wrong, you can rerun only from that step instead of the whole chain, making self-correction cheaper. It works most cleanly when there is a single slip to find.

key_numbers.label: Finding the first wrong step
key_numbers.sub: random-guess level, versus watching where certainty dips

key_numbers.label: Beating a guess based on step position
key_numbers.sub: guessing a likely position, versus using the certainty dip

key_numbers.label: Steps redone to fix a chain
key_numbers.sub: redo the whole chain, versus redo only from the flagged step

charts.chart_plain_title: Certainty drops lowest exactly at the step where the model first slips
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis lines up every chain at the step where the model first went wrong (zero), with earlier steps to the left and later ones to the right. The line shows certainty after removing the natural rise that comes just from being further along; lower means less sure.
charts.chart_takeaway: The line hits its lowest point right at zero, the first mistake, after sitting high just before it, so the dip marks where the error began rather than merely late steps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Watching the certainty dip beats guessing the wrong step by position
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a different way of guessing which step holds the first mistake, on chains with exactly one mistake. Bar height is how often it picks the right step, so taller is better. The certainty-dip methods sit beside simpler position-based and random guesses.
charts.chart_takeaway: The certainty-dip bars stand well above the position-guess and random bars, showing the model's own uncertainty carries real information about which step went wrong.

qwen35_4b_executable_program_posttrainin.verdict_tag: Runnable-fix training transfers, but needs worked steps
qwen35_4b_executable_program_posttrainin.concept_primer: Instead of describing a fix in words, the model is trained to rewrite the broken formula as a runnable mini-program the computer then checks by actually running it on real cases.
qwen35_4b_executable_program_posttrainin.plain_question: Can the model learn to fix broken mini-programs by rewriting them, and still succeed on kinds of problems it never trained on?
qwen35_4b_executable_program_posttrainin.plain_answer: Mostly. After training it fully solved unseen problem types about three-quarters of the time, versus a third with no worked-through steps in the prompt, but only when those matching steps were present, and one problem type stayed stubbornly hard.
qwen35_4b_executable_program_posttrainin.why_it_matters: Training a small model to emit runnable fixes can carry over to new problem types, but only if the prompt shows matching worked-through steps. Missing or scrambled steps erase most of the gain, so keep them aligned.

key_numbers.label: Unseen-problem success with worked steps
key_numbers.sub: share of new problems fully solved when the prompt shows matching steps

key_numbers.label: Without vs with worked steps
key_numbers.sub: full success on new problems, no steps versus aligned steps

key_numbers.label: Hardest problem type, after extra practice
key_numbers.sub: the two-part-condition type, before and after adding practice examples

charts.chart_plain_title: Success on unseen problem types across four setups
charts.chart_read: Each group is one training-and-prompt setup; bar height is the share of new problems fully solved. The two bars per group are one best guess versus picking the best of a few tries; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two setups that include matching worked-through steps tower over the two without, solving about three-quarters of new problems versus roughly a third.

charts.chart_plain_title: Per-problem-type success before and after extra practice
charts.chart_read: Each group is one unseen problem type; bar height is the share fully solved. The two bars compare the first-trained model with one given extra practice on combined-condition problems; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The stubborn two-part-condition type climbs from zero to about a third after extra practice, while the other two types stay near-perfect.

charts.chart_plain_title: What happens when the prompt's worked steps are removed or scrambled
charts.chart_read: Each group is one unseen problem type; bar height is the share fully solved. The three bars are a prompt with matching worked steps, no steps, or scrambled steps; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Removing or scrambling the worked steps collapses one problem type from near-perfect to at most a fifth, showing the steps do real work.

qwen35_4b_foofah_adaptive_program_budget.verdict_tag: Cheap rule knows when extra effort pays
qwen35_4b_foofah_adaptive_program_budget.concept_primer: Picture cleaning a messy spreadsheet: the model can give a quick answer, or run five slower step-by-step programs. This tests a cheap rule that decides when those slow programs are worth the extra effort.
qwen35_4b_foofah_adaptive_program_budget.plain_question: Can a simple rule tell when it is worth running the slow, expensive table-fixing programs instead of just taking the quick answer?
qwen35_4b_foofah_adaptive_program_budget.plain_answer: Yes. A one-line rule that runs the slow programs only when the quick answer drops columns reached the best accuracy any program could, 58% correct versus 42% for the quick answer alone, at a fraction of the always-run cost.
qwen35_4b_foofah_adaptive_program_budget.why_it_matters: You do not need a trained judge to decide when to spend expensive compute. A simple check on the shape of the quick answer routes the effort to the few cases that need it and skips the rest.

key_numbers.label: Tables fixed correctly
key_numbers.sub: quick answer alone vs the cheap rule

key_numbers.label: Effort spent per table
key_numbers.sub: always running everything vs the cheap rule

key_numbers.label: Extra tables rescued
key_numbers.sub: cases the quick answer missed, none made worse

charts.chart_plain_title: Cheap rule matches the best possible accuracy
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of messy tables fixed correctly under one approach: the quick answer alone, always running all five slow programs, the cheap rule, and the best any program could do. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The cheap-rule bar ties the best-possible bar and clears the quick-answer bar, so the simple rule captures every available gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: Effort spent per table under each approach
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the average work spent per table for the same four approaches. Shorter is cheaper and better. The quick answer is cheapest but least accurate; always running everything is the priciest.
charts.chart_takeaway: The cheap-rule bar is about half the height of the always-run-everything bar, so it costs far less for the same accuracy.

charts.chart_plain_title: Always running more programs never catches the cheap rule
charts.chart_read: The rising line shows accuracy as you force more slow programs to run on every table; the flat line is the cheap rule that runs them only when needed. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The rising line levels off just below the flat rule line, so no amount of always-on effort reaches what the selective rule gets.

charts.chart_plain_title: The rule's gains come from just two table types
charts.chart_read: Paired bars for each table type show the quick answer versus the cheap rule; taller is better. Most pairs match, meaning the rule leaves them unchanged.
charts.chart_takeaway: Two table types jump from zero to most-correct with the rule while every other type is untouched, so gains are concentrated and nothing is broken.

charts.chart_plain_title: Trying one program first barely helps
charts.chart_read: Paired bars per program strategy compare running that one strategy outright versus first testing it and escalating only on disagreement. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two bars are nearly identical for every strategy and all sit below the cheap rule's level, so the escalate-later trick adds almost nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Requiring programs to agree lowers accuracy
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars at each effort budget compare committing to the first workable program versus demanding two or three programs agree first. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The agreement bars sit below the first-commit bars at every budget, so insisting programs match each other consistently costs accuracy.

charts.chart_plain_title: Looser rules cost far more for the same accuracy
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the total effort spent by a different trigger rule, all of which reach the same accuracy. Shorter is cheaper and better; the last bar always runs everything.
charts.chart_takeaway: The tightest trigger's bar is a small fraction of the always-run bar despite matching its accuracy, so the strict rule buys the same result cheaply.

qwen35_4b_foofah_direct_vs_abi.verdict_tag: Asking directly beats the specialized tool
qwen35_4b_foofah_direct_vs_abi.concept_primer: Imagine reshaping messy spreadsheet tables into a clean format. You can use a pre-built converter that only knows a fixed set of moves, or just ask the AI to write out the finished table itself.
qwen35_4b_foofah_direct_vs_abi.plain_question: Does the pre-built table converter beat simply asking the model to write out the reshaped table itself?
qwen35_4b_foofah_direct_vs_abi.plain_answer: No. Asking the model directly got 55% of tables exactly right, far ahead of the pre-built converter's 18%. The converter added only a tiny extra slice of cases the model missed on its own.
qwen35_4b_foofah_direct_vs_abi.why_it_matters: For reshaping messy tables, reach for the model directly rather than building a specialized converter; keep the tool only as a small backup for the few cases the model misses.

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of messy tables reshaped correctly, by method
charts.chart_read: Bars show the fraction of 250 table-reshaping tasks each method got exactly right. Left bar is asking the model directly; middle bars are the pre-built converter tool; right bar combines both. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The direct-ask bar towers over the converter-tool bars, so simply asking the model outperformed the specialized tool by a wide margin.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which tables each method uniquely got right
charts.chart_read: Bars split the 250 tasks into four groups: solved by both, by direct-ask only, by the converter tool only, or by neither. Taller bars for the useful groups are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The 'direct-ask only' bar dwarfs the 'converter-only' bar, showing the model handles far more cases the tool cannot than the reverse.

qwen35_4b_foofah_ephemeral_program_induc.verdict_tag: Writing a throwaway program did not help
qwen35_4b_foofah_ephemeral_program_induc.concept_primer: Instead of reshaping a messy spreadsheet by hand, the model can write a tiny throwaway script that does the reshaping, test it on one worked example, then run it on the real data.
qwen35_4b_foofah_ephemeral_program_induc.plain_question: Does having the model write and test a small reshaping script beat just having it reshape the spreadsheet directly?
qwen35_4b_foofah_ephemeral_program_induc.plain_answer: No. Answering directly got about 55% of tables exactly right, while the script route managed only 15%. Even combining both perfectly would reach just 59%, barely above answering directly alone.
qwen35_4b_foofah_ephemeral_program_induc.why_it_matters: On this kind of table-reshaping task, let the model answer directly rather than routing through a self-written script. Passing one worked example is a weak safety check and lets wrong scripts through.

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of tables reshaped exactly right, by method
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one method; bar height is the share of 250 tables reshaped exactly right, so taller is better. Direct answering and the two combined bars sit near 55-59%, while the script-only bar is far shorter.
charts.chart_takeaway: The script-only bar is roughly a third the height of direct answering, and combining both barely clears the direct bar, so the script route adds almost nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which method solved which tables
charts.chart_read: Bars count the 250 tables by who got them right: both, direct-answering only, script only, or neither. Taller 'direct only' versus 'script only' shows which approach uniquely rescues more.
charts.chart_takeaway: 'Direct only' towers over 'script only' (110 versus 10), meaning direct answering uniquely solves eleven times as many tables as the script route does.

charts.chart_plain_title: How many scripts survive each checkpoint
charts.chart_read: Bars follow the script route left to right: code written, code that runs, code that passes the one worked example, and code that is actually right on the real data. Each stage drops, showing where scripts fail.
charts.chart_takeaway: The bar collapses from 250 written to 47 passing the worked example, and 9 of those 47 still fail on the real data, so passing one example proves little.

charts.chart_plain_title: Early prompt tests on easy versus harder samples
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars per test show direct answering, the script route, and the two combined. The first two groups are easy hand-picked cases; the last two are a harder mixed sample, where higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On easy cases every bar hits 100%, but on the harder sample the script bar collapses to 12%, revealing the easy cases had flattered the script route.

qwen35_4b_foofah_external_transform_gate.verdict_tag: Fixed toolkit too narrow for outside tasks
qwen35_4b_foofah_external_transform_gate.concept_primer: Think of a fixed toolkit of table-reshaping moves, like a small recipe book, that tries to rebuild each messy spreadsheet into the desired clean layout using only the recipes it already knows.
qwen35_4b_foofah_external_transform_gate.plain_question: Can a fixed toolkit of table-reshaping moves handle a broad outside collection of real spreadsheet cleanup tasks?
qwen35_4b_foofah_external_transform_gate.plain_answer: No. The fixed toolkit rebuilt only 18% of the outside transformation tasks, failing 82% because it simply lacks the needed moves, not because it chose the wrong recipe or saw too few examples.
qwen35_4b_foofah_external_transform_gate.why_it_matters: Before investing in smarter recipe-picking, first widen the toolkit itself. Coverage was all-or-nothing by task type, so the gap is missing building blocks, not better selection.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often the fixed toolkit reproduced each table transformation
charts.chart_read: Bars show three ways of scoring the same 250 tasks: matching just the shown example, actually working on fresh data, and picking the first matching recipe. Height is the share solved; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three bars sit near one-fifth, and picking the first recipe nearly matches the best possible, so the toolkit itself, not the choosing, is the limit.

charts.chart_plain_title: Adding more worked examples did not help
charts.chart_read: Each line tracks the share of tasks solved as the number of shown example pairs rises from one to five. Higher is better. Two lines stay flat; the top line only falls to meet them.
charts.chart_takeaway: The fresh-data line is dead flat across one to five examples, meaning failures come from missing moves in the toolkit, not from too few examples.

charts.chart_takeaway: The unsolved bar towers over the solved one, 41 versus 9, showing the toolkit handles a narrow slice and misses most task types entirely.

qwen35_4b_foofah_program_ensemble_consen.verdict_tag: Making programs agree beats no simpler pick
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_ensemble_consen.concept_primer: Instead of answering directly, the model writes small programs that reshape a messy table, and each program can be checked against one worked example before you trust its answer.
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_ensemble_consen.plain_question: When the model writes several table-reshaping programs, does requiring them to agree beat just using the first one that matches the worked example?
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_ensemble_consen.plain_answer: No. Requiring several programs to agree was safer on each pick but far too cautious; simply using the first program that matched the worked example solved the most tables, 52% versus 44% for asking the model directly.
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_ensemble_consen.why_it_matters: Prefer the simplest reliable filter — trust the first program that reproduces the worked example. Demanding agreement across programs raises confidence per answer but skips too many solvable tables to be worth it.

key_numbers.label: First matching program
key_numbers.sub: use the first program that reproduces the worked example

key_numbers.label: Requiring agreement
key_numbers.sub: make at least two programs agree first — fewer tables solved

key_numbers.label: Best any program could do
key_numbers.sub: ceiling if you always picked a correct program when one existed

charts.chart_plain_title: How many tables each answering strategy solves
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a way of picking the final answer, from asking directly, through the two agreement rules, to using the first program that matches the example, ending at a best-possible ceiling; the height is the share of tables solved exactly, and taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The first-matching-program bar is the tallest usable strategy, standing above both agreement bars and above asking directly.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tables newly fixed versus newly broken by each strategy
charts.chart_read: For each picking strategy, one colored bar counts tables it newly gets right compared with asking directly, and the other counts tables it newly gets wrong; more fixes and fewer breaks is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The first-matching-program strategy fixes 23 tables while breaking only 4, a far bigger net gain than either agreement rule.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often a committed program is actually correct
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a picking strategy, and the height shows how often the program it commits to turns out to be right; taller means more trustworthy picks.
charts.chart_takeaway: Requiring stricter agreement lifts per-pick correctness to 84% from 73%, but that small edge does not make up for the many tables it skips.

charts.chart_plain_title: Programs that match the example, before and after one fix
charts.chart_read: Each group is one of three prompt styles for writing the program, with one bar for programs that matched the example on the first try and another for extra matches gained after a single fix attempt; more matches is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: One fix round nearly doubles the matches for the middle prompt style while barely helping the other two.

qwen35_4b_foofah_program_repair_agent.verdict_tag: Repair adds coverage but not a replacement
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_repair_agent.concept_primer: Instead of typing the answer by hand, the model writes a small runnable recipe that reshapes the table, tries it on a solved worked example, and fixes wherever the result differs.
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_repair_agent.plain_question: Does letting the model write a small conversion recipe, test it on a worked example, and fix its mistakes beat just asking for the answer directly?
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_repair_agent.plain_answer: It depends. On its own the recipe path solved far fewer tables than answering directly (25% vs 55%), but fixing the recipe against the worked example and then keeping whichever path won caught 18 extra tables, reaching 62%.
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_repair_agent.why_it_matters: Treat the recipe path as a complementary second opinion, not a replacement for direct answering, and never auto-trust a recipe just because it matches the worked example.

charts.chart_read: Each bar is one strategy; height is the share of tables transformed exactly right on the unseen real data. The left bars are single strategies and the rightmost combines both paths; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The recipe-only bars sit well below direct answering, yet the combined bar is the tallest, showing the two approaches solve different tables.

charts.chart_plain_title: How each fixing round pays off
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is the fixing round; the lines count tables. One line is tables still being fixed, one is tables now matching the worked example, and one is tables that also match the real data. Bigger gains per round are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The correct-on-real-data line jumps by 40 tables in the first round then adds only a handful more, so fixing helps most early and fades fast.

charts.chart_plain_title: How trustworthy is passing the worked example?
charts.chart_read: Bars count tables among recipes that passed the worked example: the total that passed, how many were truly right on real data, and how many were secretly wrong. A small wrong bar is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Of 78 recipes that passed the example, 16 were still wrong on real data, so passing the example does not guarantee a correct answer.

charts.chart_plain_title: Warning the model not to copy the example's answer
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars compare two instructions across three measures; one color is the plain instruction and the other explicitly forbids copying the example's output. For the two correctness measures higher is better, and for the misleading-pass measure lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The stricter instruction doubled recipe correctness and nearly halved the misleading passes, so telling the model not to memorize the example helped.

qwen35_4b_foofah_program_strategy_portfo.verdict_tag: Test-checked recipes help modestly but cost more
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_strategy_portfo.concept_primer: When cleaning a messy spreadsheet, you can either type out the fixed version by hand, or write a short reusable recipe that rearranges it automatically, then test that recipe against one worked example before trusting it.
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_strategy_portfo.plain_question: When a model reshapes messy tables into a clean target layout, does letting it write and test small recipes beat simply typing out the answer table directly?
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_strategy_portfo.plain_answer: Yes, but only a little. Writing and testing recipes lifted correctly cleaned tables from 42% to 48%, a real gain over typing answers directly. A simpler rule, trusting the first recipe that passes its example, did better at 56%. All of it cost roughly twelve times more effort.
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_strategy_portfo.why_it_matters: Recipes add genuine coverage on a few structural table shapes but spend heavily on every table. Route recipe attempts only to tables likely to benefit, and trust the first recipe that passes its example rather than demanding that several recipes agree.

qwen35_4b_foofah_selective_program_fallb.verdict_tag: Simple check helped, stress-test did not
qwen35_4b_foofah_selective_program_fallb.concept_primer: For each table task the model can either write the answer straight out, or write a tiny program to build it; you can then run that program on the shown examples to see if it reproduces them.
qwen35_4b_foofah_selective_program_fallb.plain_question: Does falling back to a table-transforming program beat answering directly, and does stress-testing that program make the choice safer?
qwen35_4b_foofah_selective_program_fallb.plain_answer: It depends. Trusting the program whenever it reproduced the shown examples raised accuracy from 55% to 62% with zero cases broken, but the extra stress-test on made-up inputs helped nothing and slightly hurt.
qwen35_4b_foofah_selective_program_fallb.why_it_matters: A cheap correctness gate, does the program match the examples you were shown, can be a reliable trust signal. Adding elaborate self-consistency stress-tests can cost effort and quietly reject good answers.

qwen35_4b_foofah_strategy_discovery_live.verdict_tag: Discovered recipes unlock no new tables
qwen35_4b_foofah_strategy_discovery_live.concept_primer: Imagine asking the model to write down a few general recipes for reshaping messy tables, learned from practice examples, then checking whether those recipes help it fix brand-new tables.
qwen35_4b_foofah_strategy_discovery_live.plain_question: Can the model discover reusable table-reshaping recipes that let it solve new tables it otherwise could not?
qwen35_4b_foofah_strategy_discovery_live.plain_answer: No. The discovered recipes nudged accuracy from 42% to 46%, but they solved zero new tables beyond a ready-made stockpile of solutions, which on its own reached 58%.
qwen35_4b_foofah_strategy_discovery_live.why_it_matters: Letting the model invent general strategies sounds appealing, but here it only reshuffled what it could already do. Prefer curating a proven library of solutions over auto-discovered recipes.

qwen35_4b_forced_commit_jacobian_value_t.verdict_tag: Forcing a stop did not produce usable answers
qwen35_4b_forced_commit_jacobian_value_t.concept_primer: Some AI models work through a problem step by step on a hidden scratchpad before writing a final answer. Like a student still scribbling, they sometimes keep working and never decide to stop and write the answer down.
qwen35_4b_forced_commit_jacobian_value_t.plain_question: When a model keeps working on a problem and never wraps up on its own, can you force it to stop and get back a clean, usable answer?
qwen35_4b_forced_commit_jacobian_value_t.plain_answer: No. Forcing the model to stop mid-work did not flip it into answer mode. At every thinking length only about one in eight tries gave even a readable answer, and almost none were correct. It usually kept scribbling or restarted its work instead of filling the answer box.
qwen35_4b_forced_commit_jacobian_value_t.why_it_matters: If you run a model that sometimes never finishes reasoning, do not expect a plain stop signal to yield an answer. Give it an explicit answer slot to fill, not just a cue to quit working.

key_numbers.sub: at the tightest thinking length; still low at longer ones

key_numbers.label: Forced stops that gave a correct answer
key_numbers.sub: same tiny level at every thinking length

key_numbers.label: Tries that never finished on their own
