charts.chart_plain_title: How often forcing a stop yielded a usable answer, by thinking length
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is one thinking-length limit. Its three bars show, out of 48 tries, how often forcing a stop gave a readable answer, how often that answer was correct, and how often the model never finished on its own. Taller readable and correct is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: No thinking length helped: the readable and correct bars stay tiny while the never-finished bar towers near full height, so forcing a stop never produced usable answers.

qwen35_4b_gauntlet_breadth_round1.verdict_tag: Broad self-training installs skill that transfers
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_breadth_round1.concept_primer: Imagine a student who solves a problem correctly in their head but runs out of time before writing the final answer on the line. The page scores as blank, even though the thinking was right.
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_breadth_round1.plain_question: If a small AI is trained on its own correct solutions across many different kinds of puzzles, does it improve on a broad set of unrelated puzzles it never practiced?
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_breadth_round1.plain_answer: Yes. Training the model on its own verified solutions across ten varied puzzle types — and specifically to write down the final answer instead of trailing off — roughly tripled its score on a broad, unpracticed benchmark, from about 14% to 36-45%. The gains reached puzzles it never practiced.
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_breadth_round1.why_it_matters: Before concluding a small model lacks a skill, check whether it is failing to write down any answer at all. Training it on its own verified solutions, weighted toward committing an answer, can unlock broad, transferable gains.

key_numbers.label: Broad benchmark score, first fresh test
key_numbers.sub: small model on puzzles it never trained on

key_numbers.label: Broad benchmark score, second fresh test
key_numbers.sub: a separate fresh set of unrelated puzzles

key_numbers.label: Blank, unusable answers per 100 tries
key_numbers.sub: on one puzzle type after the write-down fix

charts.chart_plain_title: Broad self-training roughly tripled the score on an unpracticed benchmark
charts.chart_read: Two fresh test sets run along the bottom. For each, a short bar shows the untrained model's score and a taller bar shows the model after broad self-training. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every trained bar towers over its untrained partner — the score roughly tripled on puzzles the model never practiced, not a small wobble.

charts.chart_plain_title: The gains reached puzzle types the model never practiced
charts.chart_read: Four puzzle groups run along the bottom, including two held completely out of training and one with zero training examples. A short bar is the untrained score, a taller bar is after training. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The unseen and zero-example puzzles jumped as much as the drilled ones — the skill spread rather than sticking to what was practiced.

charts.chart_plain_title: Blank, unusable answers nearly vanished after the write-down fix
charts.chart_read: Five puzzle types run along the bottom. A tall bar shows how many of 100 tries produced no usable answer before training; a short bar shows after. Shorter is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The nearly-all-blank bars collapse to almost nothing — showing the failure was unwritten answers, not missing ability.

qwen35_4b_gauntlet_frontier.verdict_tag: Small model wins speed or reasoning, never both
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_frontier.concept_primer: Picture a small model as a hiker with one fixed-size backpack, packing for two races at once: a sprint and a marathon. Every item that helps win the sprint crowds out gear the marathon needs. One pack can win either race, never both.
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_frontier.plain_question: If you fine-tune a small model to master both quick tasks and slow, deep reasoning tasks, can one model end up great at both at once?
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_frontier.plain_answer: No. Tune a four-billion-parameter model until it clears the bar on quick tasks and it slips on deep reasoning; tune it the other way and the reverse happens. Each tier is beatable alone — scores climbed from about a tenth right to over half — but both skills draw on one fixed pool of capacity, so no single model holds both records, and splitting the difference loses on both.
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_frontier.why_it_matters: Deploying one small model across mixed work? Don't expect a single fine-tune to top every task. Ship a specialist adapter per tier and route between them, or pick the tier that matters and accept the other slips.

key_numbers.label: Best gain on quick tasks
key_numbers.sub: the speed-tuned recipe — but only +31 on the deep tier

key_numbers.label: Best gain on deep reasoning tasks
key_numbers.sub: a different recipe, first to clear the deep tier — but only +31 on quick tasks

key_numbers.label: Recipes that aced both tiers
key_numbers.sub: none of ten tuning recipes cleared both tiers, across 28 paired tests

key_numbers.label: Task success, untuned vs best tuned
key_numbers.sub: base model vs best tuned model, on a 0-to-100 scale

charts.chart_plain_title: Each recipe wins one task tier; none clears both
charts.chart_read: Three tuning recipes sit along the bottom; each shows two bars — its gain on quick tasks and its gain on deep reasoning tasks, both measured against the untuned model. A line marks the passing bar; taller bars are better. Watch which recipe crosses the line on which tier.
charts.chart_takeaway: The speed recipe's quick bar towers but its deep bar falls short; the reasoning recipe flips it; the middle compromise recipe clears neither — a trade-off you can't split.

charts.chart_plain_title: Where the reasoning fix helped, and the one task it didn't
charts.chart_read: Five kinds of deep reasoning task run along the bottom; each bar shows how much the reasoning-tuned recipe improved on that task over the untuned model. Taller is better, and a bar dipping below zero means the model actually got a little worse there.
charts.chart_takeaway: Four tasks jump — knowing when to decline and triage most of all — but deep repair sits just below zero, the single skill no recipe could teach.

charts.chart_plain_title: Why deep tasks fail: mostly too slow, not truly impossible
charts.chart_read: The hardest items split into two bars: the share the model can't crack even given six tries, versus the share it can solve but sometimes thinks too slowly to fit its answer budget. A taller right bar means the ceiling is time, not ability.
charts.chart_takeaway: Nearly two-thirds are solvable-but-slow, so most of the ceiling is a time-budget wall the model can be trained to compress around — not a hard limit of ability.

qwen35_4b_generator_verifier_gap.verdict_tag: Checking beats doing when the model thinks
qwen35_4b_generator_verifier_gap.concept_primer: Imagine a student who cannot always solve a problem from scratch but can often tell whether a proposed answer is right, especially if they slow down and work through the check step by step instead of eyeballing it.
qwen35_4b_generator_verifier_gap.plain_question: When a small coding model writes several answers to a problem, can it tell on its own which one is actually correct, without running any tests?
qwen35_4b_generator_verifier_gap.plain_answer: Yes, this small coding model can reliably tell whether its own answer is right, but only when it works through the check step by step. Snap judgments barely beat guessing, while deliberate checking picks the best of eight tries almost as well as a flawless chooser could.
qwen35_4b_generator_verifier_gap.why_it_matters: If you can trust a model to grade its own work, let it write several answers and keep the one it judges best, with no test suite needed. Just make it reason through each check first; quick gut reactions barely help.

key_numbers.label: Kept answers that work, best of eight tries
key_numbers.sub: picking at random vs a deliberate self-check

key_numbers.label: Telling its right answers from its wrong ones
key_numbers.sub: snap judgment vs deliberate check; 50% is pure guessing

key_numbers.label: Gap to a flawless picker that self-checking closes
key_numbers.sub: up from 24% without deliberate checking

charts.chart_plain_title: How well does self-checking pick the best of eight tries?
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of problems solved when you keep just one of eight attempts. Left to right: picking at random, a snap self-check, a deliberate self-check, and a flawless picker. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The deliberate-self-check bar reaches 86%, nearly touching the flawless picker at 89% and towering over random picking at 77%, closing most of the reachable gap.

charts.chart_plain_title: Does the model spot the difference between its right and wrong answers?
charts.chart_read: Two pairs of bars compare a snap judgment (lighter) against a deliberate check (darker) on how cleanly the model separates its own correct answers from its wrong ones. Both scores sit at 50% for pure guessing; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The deliberate-check bars stand far above the snap-judgment bars on both scores, showing careful reasoning, not a quick reaction, is what separates right from wrong.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often does the checker just say the answer looks correct?
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of answers the checker waves through as correct. The first two are snap checking and deliberate checking; the third marks how often answers are truly correct. Sitting closer to that true rate is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The snap checker waves through 91% as correct, well above the true 77%, while deliberate checking sits closer at 83%, so it is genuinely judging rather than rubber-stamping.

qwen35_4b_graphir_self_repair.verdict_tag: Self-patching diagrams lose to plain one-liners
qwen35_4b_graphir_self_repair.concept_primer: The model can write its solution two ways: as a compact one-line formula, or as a numbered wiring diagram whose steps you can check against failing examples and then patch.
qwen35_4b_graphir_self_repair.plain_question: Does writing the program as a checkable step-by-step diagram and then letting the model patch its own mistakes solve more unseen tasks than a plain one-liner?
qwen35_4b_graphir_self_repair.plain_answer: No. The plain one-liner solved the most unseen tasks, about 29%. The step-by-step diagram plus a self-patch step reached only about 24%, so the extra machinery did not pay off.
qwen35_4b_graphir_self_repair.why_it_matters: A more elaborate, self-correcting format is not automatically better; here the simpler direct format won. Save the patch step for cases whose flaws resemble the ones it was actually trained to fix.

key_numbers.label: Plain one-liner approach
key_numbers.sub: unseen tasks fully solved (35 of 120)

key_numbers.label: Diagram plus self-patch
key_numbers.sub: unseen tasks fully solved (29 of 120)

key_numbers.label: Patching pre-broken diagrams
key_numbers.sub: patch works when the flaw is planted, not self-made

charts.chart_plain_title: Diagram approach never beats the simple one-liner
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one way of writing the program; bar height is the share of 120 unseen tasks fully solved, and taller is better. The left bar is the plain one-liner, the middle is the step-by-step diagram alone, and the right adds a self-patch step.
charts.chart_takeaway: The leftmost one-liner bar is tallest; even after the patch step the diagram bars stay shorter, so the diagram approach never catches up.

charts.chart_plain_title: Patching does fix deliberately broken diagrams
charts.chart_read: Two bars show a batch of deliberately broken diagrams before patching (left) versus after the patch step (right); height is the share of unseen tasks fully solved, and taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The after-patch bar towers over the before bar, so the patching skill is real, yet it does not carry over to the model's own construction mistakes.

charts.chart_plain_title: Each approach wins and loses on different task types
charts.chart_read: Task types run along the bottom, with three bars each: plain one-liner, diagram alone, and diagram plus patch; height is the share solved, and taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Wins and losses flip by task type: the diagram aces one family the one-liner completely fails, but drops to zero on two families the one-liner fully solves.

qwen35_4b_humaneval_adaptive_budget.verdict_tag: Smart stopping did not improve accuracy
qwen35_4b_humaneval_adaptive_budget.concept_primer: Picture a quiz-solver that drafts several candidate answers, then runs sample inputs through them and keeps the answer shared by the biggest group; the model's only job is to say "commit now" or "run one more sample input."
qwen35_4b_humaneval_adaptive_budget.plain_question: Can the model boost accuracy by deciding when to stop running sample inputs versus running one more?
qwen35_4b_humaneval_adaptive_budget.plain_answer: No. Every stopping strategy landed at the same 17% correct-answer rate, and letting the model spend more sample inputs never moved the pick onto a right answer, even though a correct answer was available for every single task.
qwen35_4b_humaneval_adaptive_budget.why_it_matters: Deciding when to stop testing is a dead end when the sample inputs cannot tell right answers from wrong ones. Effort belongs in a better way to pick the winning answer, not in tuning when to quit.

key_numbers.label: Answers picked that were correct
key_numbers.sub: identical across every stopping strategy

key_numbers.label: Tasks with a correct answer available
key_numbers.sub: a right answer always existed to be found

key_numbers.label: Sample runs the trained model used
key_numbers.sub: cheaper stopping, but zero accuracy gain

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy stays flat no matter how the model decides to stop
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars across five stopping strategies. The taller bar in each pair shows how often a correct answer was even available (always full height); the shorter bar shows how often the picked answer was actually correct. Higher is better; the short bars never rise.
charts.chart_takeaway: The correct-answer bar sits at the same low height for every strategy while availability stays at full height, meaning the pick almost never lands on the right answer that was there all along.

charts.chart_plain_title: How many sample runs each stopping strategy used
charts.chart_read: One bar per strategy showing the average number of sample inputs run before committing; shorter means cheaper. The trained model's bar is well below the run-everything bar, but accuracy from the other chart is flat across all of them.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained model's short bar shows it learned to stop early and save sample runs, yet since accuracy never changes, that saving buys nothing useful.

qwen35_4b_humaneval_code_confidence.verdict_tag: One direct yes/no self-check picks better code
qwen35_4b_humaneval_code_confidence.concept_primer: Picture a coder who wrote several versions of a program but can't run any to see which works. To choose one, they simply ask themselves "is this one correct?" and trust how strongly their gut says yes.
qwen35_4b_humaneval_code_confidence.plain_question: When a model writes several code solutions and can't run any tests to check them, can it reliably pick its own best answer to submit?
qwen35_4b_humaneval_code_confidence.plain_answer: Yes. Asking the model one direct "is this correct?" question and reading how strongly it says yes picked a working solution about 84% of the time, beating a blind random pick (77%) and scoring by the model's average word-by-word certainty across the code (79%).
qwen35_4b_humaneval_code_confidence.why_it_matters: When you can't run tests to check generated code, don't rank candidates by averaging the model's word-by-word certainty. Ask it one direct yes/no question about correctness and submit the answer it most confidently endorses.

key_numbers.label: Blind random pick
key_numbers.sub: how often a randomly chosen solution works

key_numbers.label: Average word-by-word certainty
key_numbers.sub: picking by confidence spread across the whole code

key_numbers.label: Direct yes/no self-check
key_numbers.sub: picking the solution it most confidently calls correct

key_numbers.label: Best possible ceiling
key_numbers.sub: how often the very best of its tries works

charts.chart_plain_title: Which method picks the best code solution when tests can't be run
charts.chart_read: Four bars, one per selection method; bar height is how often the chosen solution actually works, so taller is better. Left to right: blind random pick, average word-by-word certainty, the one direct yes/no self-check, and a perfect-hindsight ceiling.
charts.chart_takeaway: The direct yes/no self-check bar rises clearly above both random pick and word-by-word averaging, landing close to the perfect-hindsight ceiling.

qwen35_4b_hypothesize_verify_wall.verdict_tag: Taught methods fail to crack deep recipes
qwen35_4b_hypothesize_verify_wall.concept_primer: Picture a few before-and-after photos of a dish; you must reconstruct the recipe -- the exact sequence of steps -- that produced them. This experiment asks a small AI to do the same with data-transforming steps.
qwen35_4b_hypothesize_verify_wall.plain_question: If you explicitly teach a small AI a step-by-step method for guessing the hidden recipe behind before-and-after examples, can it finally handle longer, deeper three-step recipes?
qwen35_4b_hypothesize_verify_wall.plain_answer: No. Teaching the step-by-step search method never let it handle the longer three-step recipes -- those stayed stuck near the bottom. It did nearly double success on the two-step recipes it practiced on, so the model clearly learns the method well but cannot stretch it one step deeper.
qwen35_4b_hypothesize_verify_wall.why_it_matters: When a model cannot reconstruct longer, deeper recipes, teaching it a smarter step-by-step search method will not fix it -- the limit is the depth itself. Train on the recipe lengths you actually deploy, and hand deeper puzzles to external search tools.

key_numbers.label: Two-step recipes solved (lists)
key_numbers.sub: practiced depth: the taught method works

key_numbers.label: Two-step recipes solved (strings)
key_numbers.sub: practiced depth: the taught method works

key_numbers.label: Three-step recipes solved
key_numbers.sub: one step deeper: no real change

charts.chart_plain_title: Teaching the method doubles two-step recipes but nothing one step deeper
charts.chart_read: Bars group by recipe length: two-step (practiced) versus three-step (one deeper). For each, the untrained model sits beside the method-trained model, on list and on string puzzles. Height is the share of puzzles whose step-structure it guessed right; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The method-trained bars tower over the untrained ones at two steps but collapse to the same low height at three steps -- the taught skill stops one step short.

charts.chart_plain_title: None of three teaching routes cracks the three-step recipe wall
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one attempt on the three-step puzzles: the untrained model, then three ways of adding the search method -- a prompt hint, a reused skill, and direct training. Height is the share of three-step puzzles solved; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All four bars sit near the floor -- no teaching route rises meaningfully above the untrained model, so the deep-recipe wall stays standing.

charts.chart_plain_title: A search skill learned on other puzzles does not carry over
charts.chart_read: Left pair checks the skill still works on the number puzzles it was trained for -- both bars tall, a faithful rebuild. The right pairs put that same skill against the plain untrained model on list and string puzzles. Height is accuracy; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The skill matches its target on its own number puzzles but drops to the floor -- even below the untrained model on lists -- on the new puzzles: it does not transfer.

qwen35_4b_icl_retrieval_vs_induction.verdict_tag: Applies stated rules but cannot infer them
qwen35_4b_icl_retrieval_vs_induction.concept_primer: Imagine a game where each digit turns into another digit by some rule. You can come to know that rule two ways: someone tells it to you outright, or you study a few worked examples and infer it yourself.
qwen35_4b_icl_retrieval_vs_induction.plain_question: If you show a model a few worked examples of a brand-new pattern, can it figure out the underlying rule and apply it correctly to a fresh case?
qwen35_4b_icl_retrieval_vs_induction.plain_answer: It depends on whether the rule is handed over. Told a brand-new digit-mapping outright, the model applies it almost perfectly. But shown only a few examples of that same rule and asked to infer it, the model fails, landing at random-guess level.
qwen35_4b_icl_retrieval_vs_induction.why_it_matters: When you need a model to follow an unfamiliar rule, state the rule explicitly rather than hoping a few examples teach it. Examples reliably help only when the underlying pattern is already familiar to the model.

key_numbers.label: Told the new rule, then applied it
key_numbers.sub: applies a scrambled digit-order almost perfectly

key_numbers.label: Left to infer the new rule from examples
key_numbers.sub: random-guess level, about 1 in 10

key_numbers.label: Effect of adding more examples
key_numbers.sub: inferring a new rule got worse, not better

charts.chart_plain_title: Following a stated rule works; inferring it from examples fails
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often the model outputs the right digit on two tasks: being told the rule versus inferring it from examples. Each task is split into a familiar everyday order and a scrambled made-up order. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both bars stay tall when the rule is stated, but the scrambled-order bar collapses to random-guess height once the model must infer the rule, so inferring, not applying, is the wall.

charts.chart_plain_title: Adding more examples does not help the model infer a new rule
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many worked examples the model sees; the vertical axis is how often it then infers the rule correctly. Two lines track a familiar everyday order and a scrambled made-up order. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines slope downward as examples increase, sinking toward random-guess level, so piling on more examples makes inference worse and the limit is unfamiliarity, not scarce data.

qwen35_4b_independent_code_abi_coverage_.verdict_tag: Fixed toolkit misses most new tasks
qwen35_4b_independent_code_abi_coverage_.concept_primer: Think of a pre-stocked toolbox of generic coding building blocks, assembled and locked before any test. A task counts as covered if some single tool from the box already solves it.
qwen35_4b_independent_code_abi_coverage_.plain_question: Can one fixed, general-purpose toolbox of coding building blocks solve a useful share of brand-new programming tasks?
qwen35_4b_independent_code_abi_coverage_.plain_answer: No. The locked toolbox solved only about 14% of brand-new tasks, down from 37% on the familiar tasks it was shaped around, too few to justify building anything on top of it.
qwen35_4b_independent_code_abi_coverage_.why_it_matters: A generic prebuilt toolbox does not stretch to unseen problems. Reusable coverage has to be proven on fresh tasks before investing in tooling; a passing sample check is not proof.

key_numbers.label: Tasks solved from the toolbox
key_numbers.sub: familiar tasks vs brand-new tasks

key_numbers.label: Solved across three fresh task samples
key_numbers.sub: steady low level, not a fluke

key_numbers.label: Sample-passing matches that still fail
key_numbers.sub: passing the quick check but wrong on hidden checks

charts.chart_plain_title: Toolbox coverage drops sharply on brand-new tasks
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of tasks the fixed toolbox can solve, for the familiar tuning set, brand-new tasks, and a broader task pool. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The brand-new bar falls to less than half the familiar bar, showing the toolbox does not carry over to unseen problems.

charts.chart_plain_title: Coverage stays low across three fresh task samples
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one random sample of brand-new tasks; height is the share the fixed toolbox solves. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three bars sit near the same low level, so the weak coverage is consistent, not an unlucky draw.

charts.chart_plain_title: Every kind of task is poorly covered
charts.chart_read: Bars break brand-new tasks into categories like text, list, and number tasks; height is the share the toolbox solves. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: No category rises above about a fifth, and number tasks nearly bottom out, so the gap spans every domain, not one weak spot.

charts.chart_plain_title: Passing the quick check rarely means a real solution
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often a toolbox match that passes the visible quick check turns out wrong, per task group; two shades track task-level and match-level failure. Lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On brand-new tasks over nine in ten quick-check passes still fail hidden checks, so the quick check gives false confidence.

qwen35_4b_independent_retrieval_consensu.verdict_tag: Independent agreement never produced usable picks
qwen35_4b_independent_retrieval_consensu.concept_primer: Imagine asking several people who worked out an answer separately, then trusting only answers they all independently agree on, so you can pick a correct solution without having the answer key.
qwen35_4b_independent_retrieval_consensu.plain_question: Can several independently derived solutions, by agreeing with each other, tell us which answer is correct when we have no answer key?
qwen35_4b_independent_retrieval_consensu.plain_answer: No. Requiring independent solutions to agree committed an answer on zero of 24 hard problems, because the independent versions rarely converged; a looser close-match pool committed sometimes but mostly on wrong answers.
qwen35_4b_independent_retrieval_consensu.why_it_matters: Forcing variety among your reference solutions can backfire, making them less relevant and less likely to agree. Genuine independent agreement needs a richer, more relevant source library, not just diversification.

key_numbers.label: Committed answers, requiring independent agreement
key_numbers.sub: Independent solvers never agreed enough to pick anything

key_numbers.label: Problems the diversified pool could solve
key_numbers.sub: Diversifying lowered it below the close-match pool

key_numbers.label: Answers that looked right but were wrong (diversified pool)
key_numbers.sub: Most passing-looking candidates were actually incorrect

key_numbers.label: Best a perfect picker could reach
key_numbers.sub: Even the ideal pick tops out here

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each picking method commits, and whether it was right
charts.chart_read: Bars count how many of 24 hard problems each picking method committed an answer on; green is answers that were actually right, red actually wrong. Taller green and shorter red is better. The far-right bar shows the best any picker could do.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two independent-agreement bars sit flat at zero, so requiring independent solvers to agree never produced a single committed answer, right or wrong.

charts.chart_plain_title: Diversifying the source pool lowered its quality
charts.chart_read: For two ways of pulling reference solutions, one bar shows the share of problems the pool could actually solve (higher is better) and the other the share of answers that looked right but were wrong (lower is better). Compare the diversified pool against the close-match pool.
charts.chart_takeaway: The diversified independent pool has the lower solve share and the taller wrong-answer bar, so forcing variety made the source material worse, not better.

qwen35_4b_interactive_policy_curriculum.verdict_tag: Coaching the model mid-task made it worse
qwen35_4b_interactive_policy_curriculum.concept_primer: Some tasks aren't one answer to one question. They're like fixing a bug: look around, try a change, run a check, then commit — a chain of moves where each step depends on the one before.
qwen35_4b_interactive_policy_curriculum.plain_question: If we let the model work through these multi-step tasks and have an expert correct each move it actually makes, does its step-by-step problem-solving get better?
qwen35_4b_interactive_policy_curriculum.plain_answer: No. The coaching backfired. After training on the corrected moves, the model scored 25 points lower on the practice tasks and 33 points lower on unseen ones. It kept the polished style of good work but stopped doing the check-before-commit step.
qwen35_4b_interactive_policy_curriculum.why_it_matters: When you coach a model by correcting its own moves, make sure it keeps making real decisions, not just copying the look of good work. A fluent style can hide a lost skill, so check the actual step-by-step choices.

key_numbers.label: Score on practice tasks
key_numbers.sub: correct multi-step solutions, before and after coaching

key_numbers.label: Score on unseen tasks
key_numbers.sub: task families the coaching never touched

key_numbers.label: Basic single-step skills
key_numbers.sub: held steady all the way through coaching

charts.chart_plain_title: Coaching lowered scores on both practice and unseen multi-step tasks
charts.chart_read: Three bars show the score change after coaching, measured against the model's strongest existing version, on practice tasks, on unseen tasks, and on simple single-step skills. Bars below zero mean worse; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both multi-step bars plunge far below zero while the single-step bar barely dips, so coaching wrecked the sequence decisions but left basic skills intact.

qwen35_4b_inventory_shortlister_training.verdict_tag: Training did not build a usable shortlist
qwen35_4b_inventory_shortlister_training.concept_primer: Imagine building a small gadget that needs two parts chosen from a 512-item parts catalog: the model must shortlist a handful of likely parts for each slot so the correct pair is among them.
qwen35_4b_inventory_shortlister_training.plain_question: Can extra training teach the model to shortlist the right parts from a 512-item catalog so the correct pair is included?
qwen35_4b_inventory_shortlister_training.plain_answer: No. After training, the model's shortlist still contained the correct two-part combination 0% of the time, exactly matching the untrained and scrambled-catalog versions, so the extra teaching added nothing here.
qwen35_4b_inventory_shortlister_training.why_it_matters: When a model cannot surface the right choice from a large catalog, adding more same-format training will not rescue it; invest instead in smarter search or better-designed test questions, which measurably narrowed the field.

key_numbers.sub: the shortlist never included the correct two-part combination

key_numbers.label: Options left after six test questions
key_numbers.sub: designed questions narrow the field far more than random ones

key_numbers.label: Picked the correct program

charts.chart_takeaway: Every bar sits flat at zero, so training left the model no better than the untrained or scrambled versions at surfacing the right pair.

charts.chart_takeaway: The designed-question bar is clearly taller, meaning well-chosen questions lead to the correct program more often, though it still fails most of the time.

charts.chart_takeaway: The line drops sharply and then flattens around a low level, confirming the model did learn the format, so the zero score is not a fitting failure.

qwen35_4b_jacobian_transport_control_rep.verdict_tag: Editing stored internal signals rewrites the answer
qwen35_4b_jacobian_transport_control_rep.concept_primer: As a language model reads a prompt, it stashes key facts as internal signals, like scribbling a reminder on a notepad. Later steps in its reasoning read that notepad to build the final answer.
qwen35_4b_jacobian_transport_control_rep.plain_question: If we find the exact internal signals where a model has stored a fact and overwrite them with a different fact, does it change both its immediate answer and a later step that depends on it?
qwen35_4b_jacobian_transport_control_rep.plain_answer: Yes. Overwriting that specific handful of internal signals made the model give the planted answer every single time, and also flipped a later lookup that reuses it. Random edits of the same size and shape never changed the answer, so the effect traces to those exact signals.
qwen35_4b_jacobian_transport_control_rep.why_it_matters: If you can locate the internal signals that hold a fact, you can steer a model's output precisely. Testing against same-sized random edits confirms the change comes from those signals, not from any nudge.

key_numbers.label: Immediate answer matches the planted fact
key_numbers.sub: share of trials, before the edit versus after the targeted edit

key_numbers.label: Later dependent step matches the planted fact
key_numbers.sub: a downstream lookup that reuses the stored fact also flipped

key_numbers.label: Same-size random edits
key_numbers.sub: random nudges of equal strength never produced the planted answer

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each kind of edit produced the planted answer
charts.chart_read: Each group of bars is one kind of edit. Bar height is how often the model gave the planted answer, shown for both the immediate answer and a later dependent step. Higher means the edit worked.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only edits aimed at the specific stored signals reached the planted answer nearly every time; same-size random edits and a wrong-fact edit stayed flat at zero.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which answer the model leaned toward after each edit
charts.chart_read: Each bar shows how strongly the model leaned toward the planted answer versus the original one, for the immediate answer and the later step. Above zero prefers the planted answer; below zero prefers the original.
charts.chart_takeaway: The targeted edit pushed both bars above zero, flipping the model's preference to the planted answer, while random edits and a readout-only check stayed well below zero.

qwen35_4b_jacobian_value_transport.verdict_tag: Editing the word said didn't change the reasoning
qwen35_4b_jacobian_value_transport.concept_primer: Imagine editing a note someone reads aloud. Swap the word and they say the new word. But if they must also do a calculation based on that word, changing the label alone might not change the number they reach.
qwen35_4b_jacobian_value_transport.plain_question: If you reach inside a small language model and overwrite which concept it is about to name, does that also change the answers the model calculates from that concept?
qwen35_4b_jacobian_value_transport.plain_answer: No. The edit reliably overwrote which concept the model spoke aloud, jumping from never to about three-quarters of the time. But it never once changed a follow-on answer the model calculated from that concept, so it controls speech, not reasoning.
qwen35_4b_jacobian_value_transport.why_it_matters: When you edit a model's internals, check whether its downstream answers actually change, not just the word it speaks. Being able to overwrite an output is not proof the model reasons from the edited meaning.

key_numbers.label: How often the model said the new concept
key_numbers.sub: at the deepest effective layer, up from never

key_numbers.label: How often the follow-on answer changed
key_numbers.sub: unchanged at every layer and every edit strength

key_numbers.label: New concept said, simpler editing method
key_numbers.sub: a plainer method reached far less than the tested edit

charts.chart_plain_title: Editing the deepest layer changes the spoken word but not the calculation
charts.chart_read: Left to right goes deeper into the model; up is how often the edit made the model produce the new answer. One line is the spoken word, one the follow-on calculation, one a plainer editing method. Higher means the edit took.
charts.chart_takeaway: At the deepest layer the spoken-word line jumps up sharply, while the follow-on-calculation line never leaves the floor — the edit reached speech but nothing computed from it.

charts.chart_plain_title: Stronger edits flip the spoken word but never budge the calculation
charts.chart_read: Left to right, the edit gets stronger. Up means the model leans toward the new answer, with the middle break-even line marking a genuine flip. One line tracks the spoken word, the other the follow-on calculation.
charts.chart_takeaway: The spoken-word line climbs across the break-even line as the edit strengthens, while the calculation line stays pinned far below it, unmoved by any strength.

qwen35_4b_joint_shortlister_ladder.verdict_tag: Model can't read a reshuffled codebook
qwen35_4b_joint_shortlister_ladder.concept_primer: Each question ships with a codebook that pairs every tool with a nickname, and the nicknames are reshuffled each time, so the model must actually read it. Its job: name two tools by their current nicknames.
qwen35_4b_joint_shortlister_ladder.plain_question: Can the model name two tools correctly by looking each one up in a codebook that gets reshuffled for every question?
qwen35_4b_joint_shortlister_ladder.plain_answer: No. The model never once produced the correct two-tool answer, in any version of the test. Training nudged single-tool guesses up slightly, but reshuffling the codebook barely changed that, so it wasn't truly reading the lookups.
qwen35_4b_joint_shortlister_ladder.why_it_matters: Handing a model a large, reshuffled lookup table and asking it to name several items at once is too hard an interface; give it a short list of pre-filtered candidates to judge instead.

key_numbers.label: Both tools named right
key_numbers.sub: never once, in any version of the test

key_numbers.label: One tool named right, untrained then trained
key_numbers.sub: training nudged it up, but reshuffling the codebook kept the same score, so not real reading

key_numbers.label: Training error, start to end
key_numbers.sub: the model learned the answer format but not how to use the codebook

charts.chart_plain_title: The correct two-tool answer never once appeared, in any version
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is one version of the test: untrained model, trained, trained on the harder designed cases, and trained with the codebook reshuffled. Bars show how often the model's shortlist held the correct both-tool answer, or just the left or right tool. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The both-tool bar is flat at zero in every cluster, and the single-tool bars barely rise and don't fall when the codebook is scrambled, so the small lift isn't genuine reading.

charts.chart_plain_title: Designed clues narrow the possibilities, but the model can't cash it in
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many tools are in the codebook; the vertical axis is how many answers still fit the clues shown, so fewer means less guessing. Lines from designed clues sit below their random-clue counterparts. Lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Designed-clue lines run below random-clue lines at every codebook size, yet that smaller pool of possibilities never turned into a single correct model answer.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training error dropped fast, then flattened partway down
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is training progress; the vertical axis is the model's error while learning, where lower means a better fit to the training answers. The single line falls steeply at the start, then wanders in a middling band. Lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The line plateaus in a mid-range band rather than near zero, matching a model that learned the answer format but not how to read the codebook.

qwen35_4b_language_proposal_wall.verdict_tag: Model follows rules but cannot infer them
qwen35_4b_language_proposal_wall.concept_primer: Imagine sorting mail two ways. Either someone hands you the sorting rule to follow, or you must figure out the hidden rule yourself just by watching a few already-sorted examples. These are very different skills.
qwen35_4b_language_proposal_wall.plain_question: Can a small AI figure out an unstated rule just by studying a few worked examples, the way it can follow a rule it is simply handed?
qwen35_4b_language_proposal_wall.plain_answer: No. Handed a rule to follow, the AI applied it correctly most of the time. But asked to discover the same kind of hidden rule from a few example pairs, it scored at pure guessing level. It can execute rules but not infer them.
qwen35_4b_language_proposal_wall.why_it_matters: Do not expect a small model to reverse-engineer an unwritten rule from a handful of examples. If you know the rule, state it plainly in the prompt; leaving it to be inferred is likely to fail.

key_numbers.label: Following a rule it was handed
key_numbers.sub: apply the given rule to a fresh case

key_numbers.label: Figuring out the hidden rule
key_numbers.sub: must infer the rule from example pairs, no thinking

key_numbers.label: Figuring it out with step-by-step thinking
key_numbers.sub: thinking helps but stays error-prone

charts.chart_plain_title: Following a given rule works; inferring the rule from examples fails
charts.chart_read: Four bars show the share of correct answers on the simplest task. The two left bars follow a rule the AI was told; the two right bars ask it to infer the rule itself. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The follow-the-rule bars tower over the infer-the-rule bars; inferring sits at guessing level without thinking and reaches only half with it.

charts.chart_plain_title: Inferring the rule stays at guessing level across every difficulty
charts.chart_read: Two lines track the share of correct answers as the rule grows more complex, meaning more steps to chain together, from left to right. One line follows a given rule, the other infers it. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The infer-the-rule line hugs the bottom at guessing level across every difficulty, while the follow-the-rule line starts high but sags as steps pile up.

qwen35_4b_language_reasoning_wall.verdict_tag: Model chains multi-step reasoning in plain language
qwen35_4b_language_reasoning_wall.concept_primer: Imagine a scavenger hunt where each person tells you who comes next. Starting from someone in the middle, you follow those pointers a set number of steps forward, then name the person you land on.
qwen35_4b_language_reasoning_wall.plain_question: When a small AI has to follow a chain of who-comes-next pointers several steps forward, can it do this reliably if the puzzle is written in plain English?
qwen35_4b_language_reasoning_wall.plain_answer: Yes. In plain English the model follows the chain three to four steps forward almost perfectly, naming the right person 94 to 100 percent of the time, and only slips at five or six steps. The stumble at the third step that many expected never showed up.
qwen35_4b_language_reasoning_wall.why_it_matters: If you need a small model to follow multi-step chains, phrase the task in plain, natural language rather than as raw code or symbol tables. Everyday wording lets it reason through three or four steps reliably instead of stalling early.

key_numbers.label: Three steps forward, plain English
key_numbers.sub: names the right person almost every time

key_numbers.label: Four steps forward, plain English
key_numbers.sub: still nearly always correct

key_numbers.label: Invented links, short vs long chain
key_numbers.sub: aces three steps, loses the trail by six

charts.chart_plain_title: Following a chain in plain English holds up for several steps
charts.chart_read: Left to right, the puzzle asks the model to follow the chain more steps forward. Higher means it named the right person more often. Three lines: plain-English wording, invented link-words, and the same chain written as computer code.
charts.chart_takeaway: The plain-English line hugs the top through four steps and only sags at five or six, with no cliff at step three, unlike a general step limit.

qwen35_4b_latent_composition_probe.verdict_tag: Hidden answer only for simple problems
qwen35_4b_latent_composition_probe.concept_primer: Like a word on the tip of your tongue, we peeked at the model's internal activity to check whether it already knows an answer even when it can't say it out loud.
qwen35_4b_latent_composition_probe.plain_question: When the model can't solve a multi-step problem, does it secretly already know the answer inside its head?
qwen35_4b_latent_composition_probe.plain_answer: It depends on difficulty. On simple problems the answer is clearly present inside even when the model won't say it; on the hardest problems it's near random-guess level inside, meaning the model never actually worked it out.
qwen35_4b_latent_composition_probe.why_it_matters: Coaxing out hidden answers can help only on easy cases where the answer already exists inside. For truly hard problems, you must teach the model the missing skill, not just read its mind.

key_numbers.label: Answer readable inside, simplest problems
key_numbers.sub: clearly present internally, yet the model says it only 44% of the time

key_numbers.label: Says it out loud vs holds it inside, simplest
key_numbers.sub: the gap between what it knows and what it expresses

key_numbers.label: Answer readable inside, hardest problems
key_numbers.sub: barely above chance, so the model never figured it out

charts.chart_plain_title: For simple problems the answer hides inside; for hard ones it's gone
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by problem difficulty (1, 2, or 3 steps). Height shows how often the first step is recovered: one bar reads it straight from the model's internal activity, the others show how often the model says or writes it; the lowest bar is the random-guess floor. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: For simple problems the internal-read bar towers over the spoken bars, but for the hardest problems every bar collapses to the random-guess floor.

charts.chart_plain_title: Where inside the network the first step gets figured out
charts.chart_read: Moving left to right walks deeper through the model's internal stages; height is how often the first step can be read out there. Three lines, one per difficulty level (1, 2, 3 steps). Higher is better; the bottom is random-guess level.
charts.chart_takeaway: The simple-problem line climbs to near-perfect by the middle stages and stays flat, while the harder lines never lift off the floor.

qwen35_4b_latent_decomposition.verdict_tag: Self-training installs the missing forward planning
qwen35_4b_latent_decomposition.concept_primer: Think of solving a puzzle that needs three moves in a row. There is a difference between planning your first move toward a far-off goal and simply recognizing when you are one move from finished.
qwen35_4b_latent_decomposition.plain_question: Can the model solve a three-move puzzle by planning one move at a time, and does training on its own solutions help?
qwen35_4b_latent_decomposition.plain_answer: It depends. On its own the model can recognize a finish but cannot plan a first move toward a distant goal, so guiding itself does worse than random. But training it on its own solved puzzles installs genuine forward planning, lifting every step.
qwen35_4b_latent_decomposition.why_it_matters: Do not expect the model to plan multi-step solutions unaided; it only recognizes near-finished states. Training it on its own verified solutions adds real planning that carries over to new roles.

key_numbers.label: Planning the first of three moves
key_numbers.sub: how often the correct opening move is picked, untrained vs after self-training

key_numbers.label: Spotting the final move
key_numbers.sub: untrained model recognizing a one-move-away finish

key_numbers.label: Full puzzles solved by self-guidance
key_numbers.sub: same effort budget, untrained vs self-trained

key_numbers.label: Random-guess level
key_numbers.sub: what pure chance would score

charts.chart_plain_title: Picking the right next move, three distances from the goal
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars: left to right the puzzle sits farther from solved (three moves away) to one move away; height is how often the model picks the correct next move (higher is better). The three colors are the untrained model versus two doses of self-training; a random guess lands near 3%.
charts.chart_takeaway: The untrained bar for the first move is almost invisible (about 1%, below random), while every trained bar rises at every distance, showing self-training adds real forward planning.

charts.chart_plain_title: Puzzles solved as the model is allowed more attempts
charts.chart_read: Lines plot share of puzzles solved (higher is better) as allowed attempts per puzzle grow left to right. One line is exhaustive brute-force, one is random guessing, and two single dots mark the untrained model and the self-trained model both using the same modest attempt budget.
charts.chart_takeaway: At the same budget the untrained dot sits below the random line while the self-trained dot jumps far above it, showing self-training turns a harmful guide into a useful one.

qwen35_4b_learn_from_failures.verdict_tag: Rewarding right-over-wrong backfires; practice more instead
qwen35_4b_learn_from_failures.concept_primer: Think of a chef who can taste two dishes and pick the better one almost every time, yet that judging skill is separate from cooking a great dish from scratch on the first try.
qwen35_4b_learn_from_failures.plain_question: Can the model get better at answering by learning to prefer its own correct attempts over its wrong ones?
qwen35_4b_learn_from_failures.plain_answer: No. Training it to favor its correct answers over its wrong ones broke its ability to write answers at all, dropping its one-shot solve rate to zero. Simply practicing longer on the correct answers alone worked far better.
qwen35_4b_learn_from_failures.why_it_matters: Being good at judging your own answers does not make you good at producing them. To lift real single-shot performance, just train longer on verified-correct examples rather than on correct-versus-wrong comparisons.

key_numbers.label: One-shot solve rate, practice longer
key_numbers.sub: Tripled by simply training twice as long on correct answers

key_numbers.label: One-shot solve rate, reward right-over-wrong
key_numbers.sub: Preference training collapsed the model's ability to answer

key_numbers.label: Judging its own answers
key_numbers.sub: It reliably tells its right answer from its wrong one

charts.chart_plain_title: Practicing longer beats rewarding correct-over-wrong answers
charts.chart_read: Six training recipes along the bottom; height is the share of unseen problems solved, higher is better. Each recipe has two bars: solved within several tries, and solved on the single best-guess try.
charts.chart_takeaway: The train-twice-as-long bar is tallest on both measures, while the reward-correct-over-wrong bars shrink toward zero, meaning preference training loses to plain extra practice.

charts.chart_plain_title: Rewarding correct-over-wrong quickly breaks the model
charts.chart_read: Left-to-right is more rounds of reward-the-correct-answer training; height is share of problems solved, higher is better. Lines track single best-guess and several-tries success, with a flat line marking the practice-longer winner.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both success lines nose-dive toward zero after a brief early blip and never reach the flat winner line, showing the training rapidly destroys the model's answering ability.

charts.chart_plain_title: Propping up correct answers softens but never fixes the collapse
charts.chart_read: Left-to-right is more effort spent holding up the correct answers during training; height is share solved, higher is better. Two flat reference lines mark plain practice and the practice-longer winner.
charts.chart_takeaway: The training lines rise slightly with more propping-up but stay below even plain practice, confirming the fix eases the collapse without recovering lost performance.

qwen35_4b_learned_active_trace_policy.verdict_tag: Learned tester wins on some task types
qwen35_4b_learned_active_trace_policy.concept_primer: Imagine guessing a hidden rule from a handful of worked examples, then being allowed to try a few extra inputs to narrow it down. A good tester picks the inputs that rule out the most wrong guesses.
qwen35_4b_learned_active_trace_policy.plain_question: Can a small AI learn on its own which extra inputs to test, so it rules out wrong guesses better than a fixed, hand-written testing rule?
qwen35_4b_learned_active_trace_policy.plain_answer: It depends. The trained tester chose better inputs than the hand-written rule on two of the three task types, but lost on the main target task. And overall its chosen tests ruled out only about half as many wrong guesses as a perfect tester would.
qwen35_4b_learned_active_trace_policy.why_it_matters: If you want a model to choose its own diagnostic tests, expect real gains on some task types but not automatically the one you care about most. Always check each task type before trusting a learned tester over a simple rule.

key_numbers.label: Simpler everyday tasks, one extra test
key_numbers.sub: share of tasks fully solved: fixed rule versus the learned tester

key_numbers.label: Sparse-example tasks, one extra test
key_numbers.sub: learned tester edged ahead of the fixed rule here

key_numbers.label: Main target task, one extra test
key_numbers.sub: the fixed rule stayed ahead of the learned tester

key_numbers.label: Wrong guesses ruled out per test
key_numbers.sub: learned tester's picks versus a perfect tester's best picks

charts.chart_plain_title: With one extra test, who solves the most tasks?
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is a task type. Bars show the share of tasks fully solved after one extra test, comparing a random pick, a fixed hand-written rule, the learned tester, and a perfect tester. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned tester's bar tops the fixed rule on the two right-hand task types but falls short on the main task at left; the perfect tester leads everywhere.

charts.chart_plain_title: Main task: solving rate as extra tests are added
charts.chart_read: On the main target task type, each line tracks the share of tasks fully solved as the tester is allowed more extra tests, left to right. Higher lines are better, with a perfect tester on top.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned tester's line starts below the fixed rule at one test, then climbs to match it by three tests; the perfect tester stays clearly above both.

charts.chart_plain_title: Sparse-example tasks: solving rate as extra tests are added
charts.chart_read: For tasks that give only a few worked examples, each line shows the share fully solved as extra tests increase from left to right. Higher is better, with a perfect tester as the top reference.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned tester's line leads the fixed rule for the first one to two tests, then dips below it at three; the perfect tester leads the whole way.

charts.chart_plain_title: How many wrong guesses each test rules out
charts.chart_read: Each pair of bars is a task type. Bar height shows how many wrong guesses a single test rules out on average, comparing the learned tester's pick with a perfect tester's best pick. Taller means more informative.
charts.chart_takeaway: Across all three task types the learned bar is under half the height of the perfect tester's, showing its chosen tests carry a real but only mild signal.

qwen35_4b_live_tool_dagger.verdict_tag: Writing and running code recovers cases
qwen35_4b_live_tool_dagger.concept_primer: Faced with a table to reshape, the model can either answer from memory or write a small program, run it, and check the result — with a "decider" step picking which answer to submit.
qwen35_4b_live_tool_dagger.plain_question: Does letting the model write and run a small program, instead of only answering from memory, help it reshape tables correctly without making things worse?
qwen35_4b_live_tool_dagger.plain_answer: Yes. Writing and running a small program, with a decider choosing when to trust it, solved 2 of 12 new tables that answer-only got wrong, and never broke a case the model would otherwise have gotten right.
qwen35_4b_live_tool_dagger.why_it_matters: Adding a run-the-code check with a decider is a safe, cheap upgrade: it only recovers misses and causes no new mistakes. A trained decider reaches the same result with far less tool effort than always running code.

key_numbers.label: Tables solved: answer-only vs writing code
key_numbers.sub: cases recovered that answering from memory missed

key_numbers.label: Right answers broken by adding tool use
key_numbers.sub: no correct case was ever made wrong

key_numbers.label: Work to solve the same two tables
key_numbers.sub: always-run needs 12 programs plus 18 fixes; trained decider uses 6 programs, no fixes

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each strategy reshapes the new table exactly right
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one answering strategy; height is how often it got the table exactly right on new, unseen tasks — taller is better. The far-left bar answers from memory only; the middle bars write and run a program; the scrambled-decider bar is a sanity check.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every write-and-run strategy clears the answer-only bar, which solves none, while the scrambled-decider control also drops to zero — running the code is what recovers the extra cases.

charts.chart_plain_title: Familiar practice tasks versus new tasks: the drop-off
charts.chart_read: Two bars per strategy: the lighter bar is familiar practice tasks, the darker bar is new unseen tasks; height is how often the table came out exactly right, taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every strategy's new-task bar is far shorter than its practice bar — accuracy falls sharply on unfamiliar tables, and even ideal choices top out at 2 of 12.

charts.chart_plain_title: Same accuracy, very different amount of tool work
charts.chart_read: For strategies that all score the same on new tasks, the bars show tool effort on 12 tasks: one color is programs written, the other is fix attempts. Lower bars mean cheaper.
charts.chart_takeaway: Always-running code needs 12 programs plus 18 fixes, while the trained decider reaches the identical score with 6 programs and no fixes — a large cost cut for equal accuracy.

qwen35_4b_long_horizon_answer_potential_.verdict_tag: Weak early signal, training test still pending
qwen35_4b_long_horizon_answer_potential_.concept_primer: A small AI model writes out its reasoning step by step before giving a final answer, like a student showing their work. Some of that scratch work genuinely helps reach the right answer, while some is just rambling that leads nowhere.
qwen35_4b_long_horizon_answer_potential_.plain_question: When a small model writes out its reasoning before answering, can we reliably pick the genuinely helpful chains by measuring how much each one lifts the model's confidence in the correct answer?
qwen35_4b_long_horizon_answer_potential_.plain_answer: No firm answer yet. Early checks show that ranking reasoning by how much it lifts the model's confidence in the correct answer carries only weak predictive power, and a blunt shortcut, simply picking the longest reasoning, predicts success just as well. The training comparison is still pending.
qwen35_4b_long_horizon_answer_potential_.why_it_matters: If you plan to train a small model on its own strongest reasoning, first confirm your quality score actually beats a crude baseline like choosing the longest chain. Early signs here suggest it may not, so validate before committing compute.

key_numbers.label: Reasoning chains, early capped run
key_numbers.sub: small pilot that cut thinking off early

key_numbers.label: Reasoning chains, calibration batch
key_numbers.sub: gathered to test whether the scoring works

key_numbers.label: Reasoning chains, full pool
key_numbers.sub: large set gathered with thinking allowed to finish

charts.chart_plain_title: How many reasoning chains were gathered at each stage
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a project stage; its height, on a log scale, is how many reasoning chains were collected. The left bar is a small early run that capped thinking length; the middle and right bars gather far more, letting each chain finish naturally.
charts.chart_takeaway: The right two bars dwarf the left even on a compressed log scale, showing this effort gathers vastly more reasoning and lets each chain finish instead of being cut short.

qwen35_4b_meta_induction.concept_primer: Someone shows you a few pairs — a number goes in, another comes out — and asks you to crack the hidden rule behind them, then use it on a brand-new number. That skill is figuring out a pattern from examples.
qwen35_4b_meta_induction.plain_question: Can a small AI be trained to crack a hidden rule from a few examples and apply it — even to a kind of rule it never practiced on?
qwen35_4b_meta_induction.plain_answer: Yes, but only when it works out loud, step by step. Trained to reason through a hunt-and-check routine, the small AI cracked hidden rules of a type it never practiced on — matching its accuracy on practiced rules — while it sat at random-guess level when forced to answer in one shot.
qwen35_4b_meta_induction.why_it_matters: If a small model can't figure out patterns from examples, don't just train it to blurt answers — that damages skills it already had. Instead, teach it a general hunt-and-check routine it can reason through step by step.

key_numbers.label: Cracking a rule type it never practiced (by reasoning)
key_numbers.sub: as accurate as the rule types it did practice on

key_numbers.label: Answering in one shot vs reasoning step by step
key_numbers.sub: same trained model: one-shot is random-guess, reasoning is near-perfect

key_numbers.label: Random-guess level
key_numbers.sub: one right answer in ten by luck alone

charts.chart_plain_title: Reasoning out a hunt-and-check routine cracks even a rule it never practiced
charts.chart_read: Each bar is how often the AI cracked a hidden rule from examples while reasoning step by step. The three left bars are rule types it practiced on; the rightmost, marked never-trained, it never practiced. Higher is better; random-guessing would land near one in ten.
charts.chart_takeaway: All four bars stand tall and level — the never-practiced rule on the right reaches the same height as the practiced ones, so the reasoning routine generalizes.

charts.chart_plain_title: Same trained model: near-perfect when it reasons, random-guess when forced to answer instantly
charts.chart_read: Each bar is how often the AI cracked a hidden rule, under different setups. The two rightmost bars are the same reasoning-trained model — one forced to answer in a single shot, one allowed to reason step by step. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The last bar towers at full height while the one beside it sits near the floor — same model, so the rule-cracking happens in the step-by-step reasoning, not stored in memory.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training to blurt answers lifts rule-finding partway, then stalls below the ceiling
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is more training examples; the vertical is how often the AI cracks a hidden rule. One line is the practiced rule type, another a different type. A flat upper line marks the ceiling (applying a rule it's simply told); a lower flat line marks random-guessing. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The practiced-rule line climbs with more data but flattens well below the ceiling, and the other rule type barely moves — blurt-answer training installs a narrow skill, not general rule-finding.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training to blurt answers gains some rule-finding but wrecks applying a rule it's told
charts.chart_read: Four bars pair two skills before and after training the AI to blurt answers directly: applying a rule it's simply told (left pair) and cracking a hidden rule from examples (right pair). Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The 'applying a told rule' bar collapses after training while rule-cracking only partly rises — the shortcut training trades away a skill the AI already had.

qwen35_4b_metacognitive_boundary.verdict_tag: Its answers reveal doubt its words hide
qwen35_4b_metacognitive_boundary.concept_primer: Picture a student who writes an answer faintly when guessing but firmly when certain — yet always says "yes, I'm sure" if asked aloud. Their real doubt shows in how firmly they commit, not in what they say.
qwen35_4b_metacognitive_boundary.plain_question: When this model answers a puzzle it may only be guessing on, can it sense which of its own answers are about to be wrong?
qwen35_4b_metacognitive_boundary.plain_answer: It depends where you look. Yes — how firmly the model commits to each answer reveals which ones are wrong, flagging them almost perfectly. No — asking it directly whether it's right gives a flat, coin-flip reply you cannot trust at all.
qwen35_4b_metacognitive_boundary.why_it_matters: When you need a model to flag its own likely mistakes, measure how firmly it commits to each answer rather than asking it whether it's right — the direct question tells you nothing reliable.

key_numbers.label: Spotting its own wrong answers from how firmly it commits
key_numbers.sub: the firmness behind each answer flags nearly every wrong one

key_numbers.label: Spotting them by asking it directly instead
key_numbers.sub: the direct self-check is no better than random guessing

key_numbers.label: Accuracy once it skips the answers it commits to weakly
key_numbers.sub: abstaining on low-confidence answers cleans up the results

charts.chart_plain_title: Internal answer-strength tracks accuracy; asking it directly does not
charts.chart_read: Four task types line up left to right, from ones the model has mastered to ones it can only guess at. Each shows three bars: how often it's actually right, how firmly it commits to its answer, and its reply to a direct 'are you right?'. Bars matching the accuracy bar mean the confidence is honest.
charts.chart_takeaway: The commitment-strength bars rise and fall in step with actual accuracy across all four tasks, while the direct self-check bars stay flat — the internal signal is honest, the spoken one isn't.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which clue best spots the model's own wrong answers
charts.chart_read: Within the single task type where the model is right about four times in ten, five different clues compete to predict which specific answers are correct. Taller bars predict better; the halfway mark is pure coin-flip.
charts.chart_takeaway: The commitment-strength bar stands tallest, while the direct self-check bar sits at coin-flip level — even below a plain guess from prompt features — so only the internal signal truly knows.

qwen35_4b_model_in_loop_counterexamples.verdict_tag: Fancier case-picking did not beat simple
qwen35_4b_model_in_loop_counterexamples.concept_primer: Think of a tutor giving a student extra practice problems to fix a weak spot. The problems can be a simple fixed set of tricky cases, or ones hand-picked to specifically trip up this student.
qwen35_4b_model_in_loop_counterexamples.plain_question: Does hand-picking practice cases that specifically trip up the model beat a simple fixed set of tricky cases, given the same training effort?
qwen35_4b_model_in_loop_counterexamples.plain_answer: No. Picking cases to specifically trip up the model did not beat a simple fixed set. Both lifted the model's weak spot from 64% to a perfect 100%, and the simple fixed set was actually a touch cleaner.
qwen35_4b_model_in_loop_counterexamples.why_it_matters: Spend effort on covering the tricky case types rather than on the fancier trip-it-up selection loop, at least until your test set is hard enough that the simple set stops solving everything.

key_numbers.label: Tricky-case success, before vs after adding practice
key_numbers.sub: getting the whole little program right on unseen inputs

key_numbers.label: Hardest case type, before vs after
key_numbers.sub: the one weak spot both practice sets fully repaired

key_numbers.label: Same practice with clues scrambled
key_numbers.sub: shows the model actually reads the worked-out steps, not just their presence

charts.chart_plain_title: Both practice sets fully fixed the model's weak spot
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often each trained version gets the whole little program right on unseen inputs; taller is better. Three versions: no extra practice, simple fixed practice, and hand-picked trip-it-up practice. Each has a single-guess bar and a best-of-a-few-tries bar.
charts.chart_takeaway: The no-extra-practice bars sit around two-thirds while both practice versions reach the top, so extra practice, not which kind, drives the fix.

charts.chart_plain_title: Where practice helps: it repairs the one failing case type
charts.chart_read: Bars group by the three tricky case types; taller is better. Within each group, the no-extra-practice version sits beside the two practice versions, showing how often each fully solves that case type on unseen inputs.
charts.chart_takeaway: The no-practice version only clears the text-length case type a quarter of the time while both practice versions clear every type, pinpointing where the fix lands.

charts.chart_plain_title: The worked-out steps genuinely guide the answer
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often the model gets the whole program right when its prompt has correct worked-out steps, no steps, or scrambled steps; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Accuracy drops from near-perfect to about a third once the steps are scrambled, proving the model reads the steps rather than treating them as decoration.

qwen35_4b_native_thought_jacobian_value_.verdict_tag: Model never finished thinking within its budget
qwen35_4b_native_thought_jacobian_value_.concept_primer: Imagine a student who must work out a problem on a small scratch pad with a fixed amount of space, then write a final answer. If they fill the whole pad without finishing, no answer ever appears.
qwen35_4b_native_thought_jacobian_value_.plain_question: When a small AI model works through a puzzle in its own words on a limited scratch pad, does it ever reach a final answer before running out of room?
qwen35_4b_native_thought_jacobian_value_.plain_answer: No. On every one of the 48 attempts, the model filled its entire scratch pad and stopped without writing any final answer. Because it never reached an answer on its own, the rest of this study, which needed finished answers to examine, was canceled.
qwen35_4b_native_thought_jacobian_value_.why_it_matters: Before building tools that read or steer a model's internal confidence from its own reasoning, first confirm it actually finishes that reasoning in the space allowed. Give more room or simpler puzzles, otherwise there is nothing to measure.

key_numbers.label: Attempts that reached a final answer
key_numbers.sub: none of the 48 tries finished on their own

key_numbers.label: Attempts that ran out of scratch-pad room
key_numbers.sub: all 48 tries filled the space and stopped

key_numbers.label: Puzzles tried
key_numbers.sub: each attempted three separate times

charts.chart_plain_title: How often the model reached an answer versus ran out of room
charts.chart_read: Four bars, one per outcome, with height showing the share of all 48 attempts. Bars for reaching a natural stop, a readable answer, and a correct answer are what you want to be tall; the last bar tracks running out of room.
charts.chart_takeaway: The three answer bars sit flat at zero while the run-out-of-room bar stands at full height, meaning the model exhausted its scratch pad every single time without answering.

qwen35_4b_native_thought_seam_budget_lad.verdict_tag: It never stopped thinking to give an answer
qwen35_4b_native_thought_seam_budget_lad.concept_primer: Picture a test-taker handed scratch paper and told to write DONE the moment they are ready to commit an answer. A small reasoning model works the same way: it thinks privately on scratch, then must signal it is finished before any answer comes out.
qwen35_4b_native_thought_seam_budget_lad.plain_question: If you give a small reasoning model more and more room to think, will it eventually decide it is finished and hand you an answer on its own?
qwen35_4b_native_thought_seam_budget_lad.plain_answer: No. Across all 48 runs the model never once signaled it was finished, even when given more than a thousand tokens of thinking room. It was not stuck in a loop — the scratch work showed it genuinely kept re-checking the task, just never deciding it was done. Extra room bought nothing: the test needed most runs to stop on their own, and none did.
qwen35_4b_native_thought_seam_budget_lad.why_it_matters: If your pipeline waits for a small reasoning model to end its own thinking, it may wait forever. Build an explicit stop-and-answer step instead of trusting voluntary closure, and verify the forced answer actually parses.

key_numbers.label: Times it stopped and answered on its own
key_numbers.sub: traces that ended reasoning voluntarily

key_numbers.label: Times it used every token still thinking
key_numbers.sub: hit the full 1,024-token ceiling

key_numbers.label: Stop rate the test demanded to pick a budget
key_numbers.sub: minimum needed; it got zero

key_numbers.label: Thinking tokens generated, none reaching a stop
key_numbers.sub: sampled across the whole ladder

charts.chart_plain_title: How often the model stopped on its own versus ran out of room
charts.chart_read: Three thinking-budget sizes along the bottom: 256, 512, and 1,024 tokens. Each has two bars — the share of 48 runs that stopped and answered on their own, where taller is better, and the share that used every token without stopping.
charts.chart_takeaway: The stopped-on-its-own bars sit flat at zero for every budget while the ran-out bars stand at full height, so more room changed nothing at all.

qwen35_4b_neurosymbolic_repl_substrate.concept_primer: Picture a coder who writes a program, runs it, sees the wrong output, and edits it — instead of just rewriting from scratch a few times. This test lets the model do exactly that, using real results from running its code.
qwen35_4b_neurosymbolic_repl_substrate.plain_question: Does letting the model run its own code and fix its mistakes beat simply having it try several fresh times?
qwen35_4b_neurosymbolic_repl_substrate.plain_answer: No. Running the code and feeding back the real result barely helped (about 29% of tasks solved), while just trying five fresh independent times did better (about 34%). The extra tries were the smarter use of the same effort.
qwen35_4b_neurosymbolic_repl_substrate.why_it_matters: For a small model, don't burn effort on run-and-fix loops at answer time — plain independent retries do as well or better. Durable gains come instead from training it on the answers it can already verify are correct.

key_numbers.label: Run-and-fix loop
key_numbers.sub: tasks solved when the model runs its code and revises

key_numbers.label: Just try five fresh times
key_numbers.sub: tasks solved by independent attempts, same effort

key_numbers.label: Learning from its own wins
key_numbers.sub: single-shot accuracy after training on verified successes

charts.chart_plain_title: Fixing its own code barely beats a single try
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one strategy; bar height is the share of unseen tasks solved, and taller is better. Left to right: one try, run-and-fix using real results, run-and-fix told nothing, and five fresh independent tries.
charts.chart_takeaway: The rightmost bar (five fresh tries) is clearly tallest, while the run-and-fix bar sits barely above a single try — live feedback adds almost nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: The fix-it loop lands on or below the plain retry curve
charts.chart_read: The rising line shows accuracy on unseen tasks as the model makes more independent attempts; higher is better. The two separate dots are the run-and-fix loops, placed at how much effort they cost.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both fix-and-retry dots sit on or under the rising line, so the same effort spent on fresh tries does as well or better.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training on its own verified wins lifts accuracy
charts.chart_read: Two task groups (one-shot answers, and best-of-five coverage); paired bars compare the untrained model against one trained on its own verified solutions. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained bars are taller in both groups, and coverage rises rather than shrinking — a real gain with no loss of variety.

charts.chart_plain_title: Repeated self-training compounds but flattens out
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is training rounds (0 = untrained); three lines track one-shot accuracy, best-of-five coverage, and the very hardest tasks on unseen problems. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The top lines climb then plateau by round three, while the hardest-task line stays flat near the bottom — the gains hit a ceiling.

qwen35_4b_offline_hard_negative_coverage.verdict_tag: Gentle nudge helps, but first tries suffer
qwen35_4b_offline_hard_negative_coverage.concept_primer: Imagine coaching someone by showing right answers next to convincing-but-wrong ones, so they learn the difference. This experiment gives the fixed model that kind of side-by-side coaching without retraining it from scratch.
qwen35_4b_offline_hard_negative_coverage.plain_question: Can a small coaching update help the model solve more coding problems without wrecking the quality of its very first answer?
qwen35_4b_offline_hard_negative_coverage.plain_answer: It depends. A gentle coaching update nudged the share of problems eventually solved from 62% up to 67%, matching what you'd get by just trying more often, at half the computing effort. But the first answer got worse, so the pre-set bar was not cleared.
qwen35_4b_offline_hard_negative_coverage.why_it_matters: A light-touch update can make a fixed model cheaper to squeeze answers from, but it shifts probability around rather than adding skill. Guard first-try quality explicitly, and never overtrain: heavy coaching broke the model completely.

key_numbers.label: Problems eventually solved
