key_numbers.label: Effort to reach that 67%
key_numbers.sub: vs solving more by simply trying more times

key_numbers.label: First answer correct
key_numbers.sub: dropped after coaching — the catch

key_numbers.label: Heavy coaching version
key_numbers.sub: overtraining broke every answer

charts.chart_plain_title: Problems solved versus first-try quality across coaching recipes
charts.chart_read: Each recipe has two bars: one is the share of problems solved after a few tries, the other is how often the first answer is right. Higher is better for both. The leftmost pair is the untrained model.
charts.chart_takeaway: The gentle-coaching recipe's solved-bar tops the untrained model while its first-try bar sits lower, and the two heavy-coaching recipes flatline at zero.

charts.chart_read: Two bars whose height is the computing effort used. Both recipes end up solving the same share of problems; a shorter bar is better because it means less effort.
charts.chart_takeaway: The gentle-coaching bar is under half the height of the try-more-often bar, so the same problems get solved for roughly half the effort.

charts.chart_takeaway: The two heavily coached bars drop to zero usable answers, while every light-touch recipe stays near three to four.

qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_scaling_str.verdict_tag: Answer stays reachable but hard to pin down
qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_scaling_str.concept_primer: Picture a mini-program as a fill-in-the-blank recipe: each blank takes one interchangeable step from a growing toolbox. You watch a few example inputs and their outputs, then try every step to guess which recipe produced them.
qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_scaling_str.plain_question: As the toolbox of interchangeable steps grows into the hundreds, can you still guess the mystery recipe from a few examples by trying every combination of steps?
qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_scaling_str.plain_answer: It depends. The correct recipe always stays somewhere in the pile you search, even with hundreds of steps. Recipes with a single blank still get picked out from a few examples nearly every time, but recipes with two blanks spawn so many look-alikes that examples alone pin the right one under half the time.
qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_scaling_str.why_it_matters: When your toolbox is large and recipes have two blanks, don't trust a few examples to single out the answer on their own. Plan to narrow the candidates first, or ask for a few extra targeted examples to break the tie.

key_numbers.label: Right recipe picked from examples alone, at 512 steps
key_numbers.sub: single blank always solved; two blanks under half

key_numbers.label: Combinations checked per puzzle (two blanks, 512 steps)
key_numbers.sub: every possible pair of steps is tried

key_numbers.label: Correct recipe still somewhere in the searched pile
key_numbers.sub: the answer never falls out of reach

key_numbers.label: After three extra targeted examples (two blanks, 512 steps)
key_numbers.sub: asking for more clues recovers most accuracy

charts.chart_plain_title: Picking the right recipe from examples as the toolbox grows
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is the toolbox size, from a handful of steps to hundreds. The vertical axis is how often a few examples alone pick the exact right recipe; higher is better. One line is single-blank recipes, one is two-blank.
charts.chart_takeaway: Single-blank recipes stay high throughout, dipping only slightly, while two-blank recipes sag toward half as the toolbox grows and look-alike combinations pile up.

charts.chart_plain_title: Would checking only a fixed shortlist still catch the answer?
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is toolbox size. The vertical axis is how often the correct two-blank recipe still falls inside a capped shortlist of combinations you agree to check; higher is better. Each line is a different shortlist size.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three capped shortlists catch the answer for small toolboxes, then collapse toward zero once the toolbox passes roughly sixty steps, so a fixed shortlist stops working.

charts.chart_plain_title: Asking for a few extra examples to recover the right pick
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many extra targeted examples you request. The vertical axis is how often you then pick the exact right two-blank recipe at the largest toolbox; higher is better. One line is a practical question-picking rule, one is the best-possible questions.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines climb sharply from under half with no extra examples to the seventies or eighties after three, so a few well-chosen clues recover most of the lost accuracy.

charts.chart_plain_title: One two-blank recipe shape is far harder to pin down
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often examples pick the exact right two-blank recipe, at two toolbox sizes, for two recipe shapes: an arithmetic scale-and-remainder shape versus a compare-two-things-and-choose shape. Taller bars are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The compare-and-choose shape sits near zero to a quarter while the arithmetic shape stays two-thirds or better, because comparing reveals too little to separate look-alikes.

qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_search_pilo.verdict_tag: Search recovers any operation on its list
qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_search_pilo.concept_primer: Picture guessing which hidden rule turns a list of numbers into one number: add them up, keep the biggest, keep the first. You can only ever land on a rule that already sits on your list of guesses.
qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_search_pilo.plain_question: When several hidden rules all take a list of numbers and return a single number, can testing them against examples reveal exactly which rule a program uses?
qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_search_pilo.plain_answer: Yes, as long as the rule sits on the search's list of options. Given the full list of seven rules, the search always contained the right one and picked it correctly about nine times in ten with no extra help. A shorter list that left the rule out found it zero times.
qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_search_pilo.why_it_matters: When building a system that identifies hidden rules from worked examples, invest in a complete list of candidate rules. The matching machinery already works well; the real limit is making sure every rule you might need is actually on the list.

key_numbers.sub: full list of seven rules, judged on the rules it had never carried before

key_numbers.label: Picked the right rule after a couple of probing checks
key_numbers.sub: one or two extra example checks close the remaining gap

charts.chart_plain_title: Only a full rule list recovers the hidden operations
charts.chart_read: Two groups of bars compare a short three-rule list against the full seven-rule list. In each group, one bar shows whether the right rule was even among the guesses, the other whether it was actually picked. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both bars sit flat at zero for the short list but leap to full and near-full height for the complete list; the omitted rules are recoverable only once they are included.

charts.chart_plain_title: A couple of extra checks push accuracy to perfect
charts.chart_read: Lines track how often the right rule gets picked as the number of extra probing checks rises from none up to three. Two lines use the full seven-rule list; a flat bottom line uses the short list. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The full-list lines climb from about nine in ten to a perfect score within one or two checks, while the short-list line never leaves zero.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each rule gets picked correctly
charts.chart_read: One bar per rule shows how often the search picked it correctly using the full seven-rule list, spanning both rules newly added to the list and rules it already carried. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Most bars reach near the top; greatest-common-divisor and first-element sit lowest because they most often look identical to other rules on the examples.

qwen35_4b_opsd_pressure_locality_audit.verdict_tag: Hint fails to localize the correctness signal
qwen35_4b_opsd_pressure_locality_audit.concept_primer: Picture two solutions that read the same until one fork, where a correct choice and a convincing wrong choice split apart; the question is whether handing a grader a retrieved recipe helps it pick the right side there.
qwen35_4b_opsd_pressure_locality_audit.plain_question: Does giving the grader a retrieved recipe help it pick the correct side at the fork where good and bad code split?
qwen35_4b_opsd_pressure_locality_audit.plain_answer: No. The recipe added essentially no help at those forks, no better than a scrambled, meaningless hint. It only boosted the grader when its own wording happened to overlap the code's surface text.
qwen35_4b_opsd_pressure_locality_audit.why_it_matters: Before training a model to imitate its own best answers using a retrieved recipe, first check that the recipe helps at the exact spots where right and wrong code diverge; otherwise training just reinforces familiar wording, not correctness.

key_numbers.sub: basically no change from giving no hint

key_numbers.label: Extra help from a scrambled fake hint
key_numbers.sub: the meaningless control scored the same

key_numbers.label: Extra help when hint wording matched the code
key_numbers.sub: but only surface overlap, not real correctness

key_numbers.label: Extra help from an answer-leaking hint
key_numbers.sub: and only because it contains the solution

charts.chart_plain_title: Extra help the hint adds at the real decision points

The four bars are the recipe hint, a scrambled fake hint, an answer-leaking hint, and cases where the hint wording overlapped the code.
charts.chart_takeaway: The recipe-hint bar sits at zero, level with the scrambled fake hint; only the wording-overlap bar towers up, so the hint helps by matching surface text, not by teaching correctness.

charts.chart_plain_title: How strongly each grader already favors the correct code
charts.chart_read: Bars show how strongly each grader already favors the correct branch over the wrong one; taller means a stronger, more confident preference. Left to right: no hint at all, the recipe hint, a scrambled hint, and an answer-leaking hint.
charts.chart_takeaway: All four bars are nearly the same height, meaning the grader already favored the correct branch on its own, so adding the recipe hint changes almost nothing.

charts.chart_takeaway: The recipe-hint-on-correct-pieces bar is tallest, hinting at broad help, but this optimistic view collapses at the specific forks that actually separate right code from wrong.

qwen35_4b_oracle_distilled_semantic_veri.verdict_tag: Trained grader wins at home, not away
qwen35_4b_oracle_distilled_semantic_veri.concept_primer: Imagine several programs that all pass the checks you can see, but only some pass secret checks you cannot. The job is to pick the one that will pass the secret checks too.
qwen35_4b_oracle_distilled_semantic_veri.plain_question: Can we teach the model to reliably pick which surface-correct program will also pass the hidden checks?
qwen35_4b_oracle_distilled_semantic_veri.plain_answer: It depends. Training the model to judge candidates lifted the right-pick rate from 72% to 81% on familiar problems, but on a new problem set the untrained model judged slightly better (94% vs 90%). Both crushed naive picking.
qwen35_4b_oracle_distilled_semantic_veri.why_it_matters: Training a small model to grade its own candidate programs pays off when new work resembles the training problems; on unfamiliar problems the untrained model may already grade better, so test before trusting it.

key_numbers.label: Right pick, new problem set
key_numbers.sub: here the untrained grader edged out the trained one

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each way of picking lands a fully-correct program
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often the chosen program also passes the hidden checks (taller is better), across six ways of choosing. Two colors compare the familiar problem set against a new one; the last bar is the best-possible pick.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained grader's bar is tallest on the familiar set but slightly shorter than the untrained grader on the new set; every grader towers over naive picking.

charts.chart_plain_title: Most programs that pass visible checks still fail the hidden ones
charts.chart_read: For each problem set, one bar counts programs per task that pass the visible checks and a second, shorter bar counts how many of those also pass the hidden checks. A bigger gap means picking is harder.
charts.chart_takeaway: On the familiar set barely half the surface-correct programs survive the hidden checks, versus about three-quarters on the new set, so choosing well matters more there.

qwen35_4b_oracle_probe_synthesis_mdp.verdict_tag: Better test questions help; headroom stays untapped
qwen35_4b_oracle_probe_synthesis_mdp.concept_primer: Imagine guessing a hidden rule by asking a few test questions and watching each answer. Every answer rules out some possibilities. Sharper questions narrow things down faster, so which questions you ask matters as much as how many you ask.
qwen35_4b_oracle_probe_synthesis_mdp.plain_question: When a model must pin down a hidden rule using only a few test questions, does giving it sharper questions to choose from, or training it to choose, help it succeed?
qwen35_4b_oracle_probe_synthesis_mdp.plain_answer: It depends. Handing the model a sharper set of eight test questions lifted success from about 43% to 49%, and training it on worked examples nudged that a little higher. But trying every possible question did far better, so most of the room to improve stays untapped.
qwen35_4b_oracle_probe_synthesis_mdp.why_it_matters: If you want a model to pin down hidden rules with only a few test questions, invest in generating sharper candidate questions rather than only teaching it to pick among a fixed handful. The biggest gains come from better questions, not more selection training.

charts.chart_takeaway: Every strategy climbs once the questions get sharper, and training adds a small bump, but the exhaustive-search bar towers far above all the trained models.

charts.chart_takeaway: On the hard rule type trained models stay near zero, yet exhaustive search reaches nearly three-quarters, showing the needed questions exist but current methods rarely find them.

qwen35_4b_oracle_process_grpo.concept_primer: Think of a game like Guess Who: you get only a few questions, each one rules out some suspects, and choosing questions wisely pins down the hidden answer faster.
qwen35_4b_oracle_process_grpo.plain_question: Can a small AI learn to ask smarter narrowing questions to identify a hidden answer, instead of just guessing?
qwen35_4b_oracle_process_grpo.plain_answer: Yes. Training taught the model to pick better questions, solving nearly half the puzzles versus about a third when untrained, closing roughly 78% of the gap to a perfect question-picker.
qwen35_4b_oracle_process_grpo.why_it_matters: You can train a small model to steer a search process rather than answer directly. Control runs with scrambled clues collapsed, confirming it learned real signal, not formatting tricks.

key_numbers.label: Puzzles solved, untrained then trained
key_numbers.sub: share of hidden answers pinned down within three questions

key_numbers.label: Best a perfect picker could reach
key_numbers.sub: the ceiling if every question were chosen ideally

key_numbers.label: Trained but fed scrambled clues
key_numbers.sub: falls back to random-guess level

charts.chart_plain_title: Puzzles solved within three questions, by question-picker
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one question-picker; height is the share of puzzles where it pinned down the hidden answer within three questions. Taller is better. The far-right bar is the perfect picker.
charts.chart_takeaway: Trained pickers rise well above random guessing and the untrained model, nearly matching the perfect picker; feed them scrambled clues or feedback and they collapse back toward guessing.

charts.chart_plain_title: More questions solve more puzzles; trained pickers pull ahead
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is how many questions are allowed, zero to three; vertical is share of puzzles solved. Every line starts together at zero questions, then higher lines are better. The top line is the perfect picker.
charts.chart_takeaway: All lines meet at the start, then trained pickers climb faster with each added question, tracking the perfect picker's curve far more closely than the untrained model.

charts.chart_plain_title: Gains land on information-rich puzzles, not sparse ones
charts.chart_read: Bars group two puzzle types: an information-rich one where questions reveal a lot, and a sparse one where they barely narrow the suspects. Height is share solved; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Training lifts scores sharply on information-rich puzzles but barely moves the sparse ones, where even a perfect picker solves under one in ten, a hard ceiling.

qwen35_4b_overthinking_content_ladder.verdict_tag: More thinking room, more reasoning payoff
qwen35_4b_overthinking_content_ladder.concept_primer: Before answering, the model can write out its working on a scratchpad. The test: does that genuine step-by-step reasoning actually help, or would the same benefit come from just having more blank space to scribble?
qwen35_4b_overthinking_content_ladder.plain_question: When the model is given far more room to think first, does its genuine reasoning stop mattering, outpaced by the sheer extra space?
qwen35_4b_overthinking_content_ladder.plain_answer: No. Giving the model more room to think makes its genuine reasoning matter more, not less. Its edge over the same words in scrambled order grew from about 11% to 15% as the room quadrupled, overturning the fear that longer thinking washes coherence out.
qwen35_4b_overthinking_content_ladder.why_it_matters: Let the model write out real, ordered reasoning and give it ample room. What lifts accuracy is the coherence of what it writes, not raw length or extra scratch time, and that payoff holds up as you extend it.

key_numbers.label: Reasoning's edge as thinking room grows
key_numbers.sub: how much genuine reasoning beats the same words scrambled, at short then long thinking

key_numbers.label: Payoff of meaningless filler
key_numbers.sub: padding the scratchpad with blank dots gave no gain over not thinking at all

key_numbers.label: Result of pasting a wrong problem's reasoning
key_numbers.sub: another task's thinking sends the model to the wrong answer almost every time

charts.chart_plain_title: Genuine reasoning's edge over scrambled thinking grows with more room
charts.chart_read: The line tracks how much more often the code passes with genuine reasoning versus the exact same words shuffled into random order. Left to right is more thinking room; higher means real reasoning matters more.
charts.chart_takeaway: The line climbs instead of falling toward zero, so more thinking room makes coherent reasoning matter more, refuting the idea that extra thinking washes its value out.

charts.chart_plain_title: Five kinds of pre-answer thinking compared across thinking-room sizes
charts.chart_read: Each line is one kind of thinking the model does before answering, plotted as room increases left to right; height is how often the code passed. Genuine reasoning sits on top, a foreign task's thinking at the bottom; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Genuine thinking stays far above scrambled words, blank filler and no-thinking (which cluster together), while foreign thinking sits near the floor, so coherent reasoning alone carries the gain.

qwen35_4b_pareto_policy_integration.verdict_tag: No complementary specialist pair to merge
qwen35_4b_pareto_policy_integration.concept_primer: Two specialist models were each trained to excel at one kind of task, with a plan to fold both into a single model. That only pays off if each specialist genuinely beats the other at its own kind of task.
qwen35_4b_pareto_policy_integration.plain_question: We had two specialist models built from the same starting point — one aimed at short tasks, one at long tasks. Does each actually beat the other at its own specialty?
qwen35_4b_pareto_policy_integration.plain_answer: No. The long-task specialist did beat the short-task one on long work, by about 5 percent. But the short-task specialist lost even on short work, by about 2 percent — so the two were not truly complementary, and the plan to merge them was called off.
qwen35_4b_pareto_policy_integration.why_it_matters: Before combining two specialists into one model, confirm each truly wins at its own job on the exact tasks that matter. If one specialist loses even on its home turf, there is nothing worth merging — just keep the stronger model.

key_numbers.label: Short specialist's edge on short tasks
key_numbers.sub: It lost the work it was built for

key_numbers.label: Long specialist's edge on long tasks
key_numbers.sub: The only clear win

key_numbers.label: Short-task test rounds the short specialist won
key_numbers.sub: Negative in both rounds

charts.chart_plain_title: Only the long-task specialist won both of its test rounds
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one test round. Height shows how much the specialist assigned to that task type beat the other model; above the zero line is better. The left two bars are short tasks, the right two are long tasks.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both short-task bars sit below zero while both long-task bars rise above it — the short-task specialist lost even on the work it was built for.

charts.chart_plain_title: Only the long-task specialist clears the bar for merging
charts.chart_read: Two roles side by side: the short-task and long-task specialists. For each, the taller bar is its average edge over the other model and the shorter bar a cautious worst-case estimate. Above zero is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both of the short specialist's bars fall below zero, while both of the long specialist's rise above it — so only the long specialist passes.

qwen35_4b_partial_structure_search.verdict_tag: Small model cannot judge unfinished program skeletons
qwen35_4b_partial_structure_search.concept_primer: Imagine seeing only the first few step-labels of a recipe, with no ingredients or amounts filled in, and guessing whether it could ever be finished into a working dish. That guess is what this experiment tested.
qwen35_4b_partial_structure_search.plain_question: Can the model look at an unfinished list of program steps and reliably tell whether it can still be completed into a working solution, so search can skip dead ends?
qwen35_4b_partial_structure_search.plain_answer: No. Its guesses landed at random-guess level within each problem, and letting it think longer did slightly worse than a quick one-glance read. Because it could not separate finishable skeletons from dead ends, it could not steer the search, so that path was stopped before any search ran.
qwen35_4b_partial_structure_search.why_it_matters: Before trusting a small model to prune a search by judging half-finished plans, check that it can actually rank finishable versus dead-end options within a single problem. Extra thinking time will not rescue a signal that carries no information.

key_numbers.label: Thinking's skill at telling finishable from dead-end skeletons
key_numbers.sub: landed at coin-flip level, no real information

key_numbers.label: A quick one-glance read's skill at the same job
key_numbers.sub: the best of the group, still far too weak to trust

key_numbers.label: Good finishable options kept when narrowing to the top four
key_numbers.sub: thinking retained fewer than the quick read

charts.chart_plain_title: Judging half-finished skeletons was no better than guessing
charts.chart_read: Five ways to score unfinished skeletons, left to right: the model thinking, a quick one-glance read, next-step likelihood, a surface shortcut, and random. One bar-color rates telling finishable from dead-end; the other rates keeping good options. Taller bars are better; near the middle means guessing.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every bar clusters near the middle of the scale, none standing out. The thinking bars are no taller than the random bars, so its judgment carried no usable signal.

qwen35_4b_passk_coverage_rl.verdict_tag: Coaching for coverage lost to plain sampling
qwen35_4b_passk_coverage_rl.concept_primer: Ask the model for several draft solutions per coding problem; "coverage" is the share of problems where at least one draft actually works, like buying several lottery tickets to raise your odds.
qwen35_4b_passk_coverage_rl.plain_question: Does lightly coaching the model to spread its drafts wider solve more coding problems than simply telling it to be more creative when it drafts?
qwen35_4b_passk_coverage_rl.plain_answer: No. The coached model solved about 44% of unseen problems with four drafts each, worse than the 69% from simply asking the untouched model to draft more creatively, and even below its own uncoached 50%.
qwen35_4b_passk_coverage_rl.why_it_matters: Before investing in extra training to widen a model's answers, first try just dialing up its creativity when it drafts; that cheaper knob beat the trained version here.

key_numbers.label: Problems solved with four creative drafts
key_numbers.sub: untouched model, most creative sampling

key_numbers.label: Problems solved after coaching
key_numbers.sub: the trained version, four drafts each

key_numbers.label: Change from coaching
key_numbers.sub: coaching lowered its own starting point

key_numbers.label: Training rounds that gave any signal
key_numbers.sub: most rounds were all-fail or all-pass

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of unseen problems solved by at least one draft
charts.chart_read: Bars compare four ways of drafting; height is the share of problems where at least one draft works, so taller is better. The coached model is the third bar; the other three are the untouched model at different creativity or draft counts.
charts.chart_takeaway: The coached bar is the shortest, below even the plain untouched model, while the most-creative untouched sampling stands tallest.

charts.chart_plain_title: First-draft accuracy and how different the drafts are
charts.chart_read: For each drafting method, one bar shows how often the very first draft is correct and another shows how different the drafts are from each other; higher is better on both. The coached model is the third group.
charts.chart_takeaway: The coached model has the lowest bars on both measures, so it also weakened first-draft quality and made drafts more alike.

charts.chart_plain_title: Useful learning signal across the training rounds
charts.chart_read: Each point is one training round; the line shows how many of eight drafts passed. Rounds sitting at zero (all failed) or at eight (all passed) teach the model nothing; only in-between rounds help.
charts.chart_takeaway: Most points sit flat at zero or eight, so only a handful of rounds gave any usable teaching signal, explaining why the coaching stalled.

qwen35_4b_prefix_value_guided_search.verdict_tag: Draft-first search does not beat plain sampling
qwen35_4b_prefix_value_guided_search.concept_primer: Normally the model writes each solution from scratch, then checks which ones pass. This test instead hands it promising half-written code drafts to finish, asking whether starting from a good draft finds answers more cheaply.
qwen35_4b_prefix_value_guided_search.plain_question: Does starting from promising half-written code drafts help the model solve more programming problems, for the same compute, than writing complete solutions from scratch?
qwen35_4b_prefix_value_guided_search.plain_answer: No. Every draft-first approach solved the same share of problems as plain from-scratch writing, about 75%, and never more. Starting from good drafts changed how the model explored but produced no extra solved problems, so the idea was set aside.
qwen35_4b_prefix_value_guided_search.why_it_matters: Before building machinery to score and pick promising code drafts, confirm it actually solves more problems. Here it did not: the drafts added variety but no new answers, so the added complexity was not worth it.

key_numbers.label: Problems solved, plain writing
key_numbers.sub: share of the 12 problems solved

key_numbers.label: Problems solved, best draft-first
key_numbers.sub: same share, no improvement

key_numbers.label: Compute per method
key_numbers.sub: units of compute; drafts cheaper but solve no more

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of problems solved by each search method
charts.chart_read: Each group is one search method: plain from-scratch writing versus five draft-first variants. Darker bars show the share of the 12 problems solved at all; lighter bars the share solved on a first pick. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every draft-first bar tops out at the same solved share as plain writing and none rises above it, so the drafts add no new solutions.

charts.chart_plain_title: Compute used by each search method
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one search method; bar height is the total compute it burned, so shorter is cheaper. Plain from-scratch writing sits on the left; the draft-first variants follow.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best draft-first bar is about four times shorter than plain writing, so it is cheaper, but only because the answers were already known when picking that draft.

charts.chart_plain_title: How varied the generated programs are
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one search method; taller bars mean the finished programs differ more. Two shades: how differently the programs run, and how differently they are written. Neither direction is inherently better here.
charts.chart_takeaway: Draft-first bars are much taller for how-differently-they-run, meaning more varied behavior, yet that extra variety never converted into more problems solved.

qwen35_4b_probe_the_parameter.verdict_tag: The exact number is not hidden inside
qwen35_4b_probe_the_parameter.concept_primer: The model solves each puzzle with a hidden rule that has a specific number in it, like "add 3." We test whether that number lives inside the model's mind or can just be read straight off the example inputs and outputs.
qwen35_4b_probe_the_parameter.plain_question: When the model solves these puzzles with a hidden rule like "add 3," is the exact number stored inside its mind, or just readable from the examples?
qwen35_4b_probe_the_parameter.plain_answer: Just readable. Simple arithmetic on the examples recovers the exact number as well as, or better than, peeking at the model's internal activity, so it is not hidden knowledge to unearth. The kind of action, by contrast, the model genuinely does work out internally.
qwen35_4b_probe_the_parameter.why_it_matters: Before hunting for hidden ability in a model, try a dumb baseline: if plain arithmetic on the examples recovers a detail equally well, that detail is not locked in the weights. Spend effort only where the model truly computes something.

key_numbers.label: Reading the kind of action
key_numbers.sub: from inside the model vs from the examples — the model wins, so it is computed internally

key_numbers.label: Reading the exact number
key_numbers.sub: from inside vs from the examples — the examples win, so it is not stored inside

key_numbers.label: Puzzles solved once given the number
key_numbers.sub: handing the model the right number is what unlocks the puzzle

charts.chart_plain_title: Can you read the hidden rule from the model, or from the examples?
charts.chart_read: Each pair of bars shows how often a hidden-rule detail is correctly recovered from the model's internal activity versus from the examples alone; taller is better. Left group is the kind of action, right group is the exact number.
charts.chart_takeaway: The model bar tops the examples bar for the kind of action but drops below it for the exact number — so the action is computed inside, the number is merely read off the examples.

charts.chart_plain_title: Giving the model the exact number is what lets it solve the puzzles
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of puzzles solved under different hints; taller is better. One color is solved on the first try, the other solved within a few tries. Hints range from nothing, to the kind of action only, to the full rule including its number.
charts.chart_takeaway: The bar for the full rule with its number towers over the bar for the kind of action alone, and a deliberately wrong number solves nothing — the exact number is what unlocks solving.

qwen35_4b_probe_to_prompt.verdict_tag: Naming the exact first step lifts solving
qwen35_4b_probe_to_prompt.concept_primer: This AI solves puzzles by writing short programs, where each puzzle needs a few chained steps. The hard part is figuring out the correct first step; get that wrong and the whole answer collapses. We test whether hints in its instructions help.
qwen35_4b_probe_to_prompt.plain_question: If we write a hint about a puzzle's first step directly into the AI's instructions, does it go on to solve more of these multi-step puzzles?
qwen35_4b_probe_to_prompt.plain_answer: Yes, but only when the hint names the exact first step, including its specific value. That made the AI solve six times as many two-step puzzles on the first try, from 3% to 19%. A vaguer hint naming only the kind of step helped only when the AI got several tries.
qwen35_4b_probe_to_prompt.why_it_matters: When you already know a puzzle's concrete first step, write it plainly into the model's instructions rather than trying to nudge its internals. Give the exact value, not just the category, and expect help only on the simpler, shorter puzzles.

key_numbers.label: First-try solve rate, two-step puzzles
key_numbers.sub: no hint vs a hint naming the exact first step

key_numbers.label: Solve rate across several tries, two-step puzzles
key_numbers.sub: same exact-first-step hint in the instructions

key_numbers.label: First-try rate with a kind-only hint
key_numbers.sub: naming just the step's type stayed at 2%

charts.chart_plain_title: Naming the exact first step lifts single-try solving; naming only its kind does not
charts.chart_read: Each group of bars is a different hint written into the AI's instructions, from no hint through a deliberately wrong one. The two bars per group are solve rate across several tries and solve rate on the first try. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The exact-first-step hint towers over every other on both bars, while the kind-only hint rises only for the several-tries bar, not the first-try one.

charts.chart_plain_title: The exact-step hint helps two-step puzzles but not harder three-step ones
charts.chart_read: Each group of bars is a hint written into the instructions. One bar shows first-try solve rate on two-step puzzles, the other on harder three-step puzzles. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The exact-first-step bar jumps for two-step puzzles but stays flat on the floor for three-step ones, where no hint moves the needle at all.

qwen35_4b_real_sample_verify_commit.verdict_tag: Choosing the answer is not the bottleneck
qwen35_4b_real_sample_verify_commit.concept_primer: Imagine solving each problem by writing several rough drafts, then handing in the one you judge best. You can fail two ways: none of your drafts is actually correct, or a correct draft exists but you hand in the wrong one.
qwen35_4b_real_sample_verify_commit.plain_question: When a coding model writes several draft solutions and then picks one to submit, do failures come from picking the wrong draft, or from no correct draft ever being written?
qwen35_4b_real_sample_verify_commit.plain_answer: No, picking was not the problem: the trained picker submitted a correct draft whenever one existed in the pool. On easier problems nearly every draft was already right; on harder ones, failures came entirely from no correct draft ever being written, not from bad picking.
qwen35_4b_real_sample_verify_commit.why_it_matters: If your coding model already writes several drafts and picks one to submit, invest in getting it to produce a correct solution at all, not in smarter picking. The picker is likely not where you are losing problems.

key_numbers.label: Harder problems where a correct draft exists
key_numbers.sub: going from one draft per problem to many drafts each

key_numbers.label: Correct answers the picker actually submitted (harder set)
key_numbers.sub: matches every correct draft that existed — nothing lost to bad picking

key_numbers.label: Easier problems solved
key_numbers.sub: almost every single draft was already correct

charts.chart_plain_title: How often a correct answer gets submitted, easier versus harder problems
charts.chart_read: Two groups of bars, one per problem set. Within each: the ceiling of problems where a correct draft existed, the result of grabbing the first draft that passed the visible example tests, and the trained picker's choice. Taller means more problems solved.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained picker's bar reaches the same height as the ceiling on both sets — it loses nothing. The harder set's ceiling itself is simply lower.

charts.chart_plain_title: Chance a correct draft exists as you write more drafts per problem
charts.chart_read: Two lines, the harder set and the easier set. The horizontal axis is how many drafts you write per problem; the vertical axis is the share of problems where at least one draft is correct. Higher and rising is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The easier set's line starts near the top and barely moves; the harder set's line climbs then flattens well below it — more drafts stop helping.

charts.chart_plain_title: How many drafts you must write with smart early stopping
charts.chart_read: Two groups of bars, harder set and easier set. Bars show the average number of drafts written per problem: always writing the maximum, an ideal stop-as-soon-as-solved rule, and a learned stopping rule. Fewer drafts is cheaper and better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned stopping rule's bars are far shorter than always writing the maximum, especially on the easier set — the same results for a fraction of the drafts.

charts.chart_plain_title: Do smarter picking rules beat simple ones for choosing a submission
charts.chart_read: Each group of bars is one picking rule, from the best-possible pick down to simple habits like taking the first draft that passed the visible tests or the answer most drafts agree on. Bar height is share of problems solved; taller is better. Shown for both sets.
charts.chart_takeaway: Nearly every bar sits at the same height within each set — smarter picking rules do not beat simple ones; only a couple dip slightly on the harder set.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much accuracy each early-stopping rule keeps
charts.chart_read: Three stopping rules side by side per set: an ideal stop-as-soon-as-solved rule and two practical learned rules. Bar height is the share of problems still solved after stopping early; taller means less accuracy given up. Shown for the harder and easier sets.
charts.chart_takeaway: On the easier set all three bars match; on the harder set the practical rules sit slightly below the ideal one — a small accuracy cost for stopping early.

charts.chart_plain_title: Drafts actually written versus the maximum allowed
charts.chart_read: Two lines, harder and easier set. The horizontal axis is the number of drafts allowed per problem; the vertical axis is how many were actually written before a draft passed the visible tests and writing stopped. Lower means it stopped sooner.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines bend below the maximum at higher limits — writing stops early once a draft passes the visible tests, and the easier set stops sooner than the harder one.

qwen35_4b_reliability_exec_opsd_audit.verdict_tag: Cheap tricks could not spot correct code
qwen35_4b_reliability_exec_opsd_audit.concept_primer: A small AI writes several versions of a program. Some pass the quick sample checks a user can see, yet are secretly wrong on hidden cases. The goal here is a cheap way to tell the genuinely-correct version from the secretly-wrong one.
qwen35_4b_reliability_exec_opsd_audit.plain_question: When a small coding AI writes several programs that all pass the quick sample checks, can cheap tricks reliably pick out the version that is actually correct?
qwen35_4b_reliability_exec_opsd_audit.plain_answer: No. Three low-cost tricks all failed. Ranking programs by the model's own confidence picked fewer genuinely-correct versions than simply taking the first that passed the sample checks; making the writing more predictable did not help; and giving a helper a worked failing example nudged the AI no better than scrambled nonsense did.
qwen35_4b_reliability_exec_opsd_audit.why_it_matters: Before paying to train a picker, cheaply check that your reliability signal beats both a scrambled-nonsense control and a plain first-pass rule. If a hint helps no more than nonsense, it carries no real information, so look for independent evidence instead.

key_numbers.label: Genuinely-correct programs picked when ranking by the model's own confidence
key_numbers.sub: fewer than simply taking the first program that passes the sample checks

key_numbers.label: Secretly-wrong programs picked by that same confidence ranking
key_numbers.sub: more wrong picks than the simple first-pass rule, which made selection worse

key_numbers.label: Tasks actually solved at the best writing setting
key_numbers.sub: making the AI's writing more predictable did not raise this

charts.chart_plain_title: Does a worked failing example point the AI at the correct code?
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a type of hint given to a helper model; taller means it nudged the AI more strongly toward the genuinely-correct code. Bars include a scrambled-nonsense hint and an answer-leaking hint for comparison. Taller is a stronger nudge.
charts.chart_takeaway: The real hint's bar sits below the scrambled-nonsense bar and far below the answer-leak bar, so the hint carries no genuine task-specific information.

charts.chart_plain_title: Ranking by the model's own confidence picks worse programs
charts.chart_read: Two bars per method count committed programs out of 24 tasks: one for genuinely-correct picks, one for secretly-wrong picks. More correct and fewer wrong is better. Methods are the first passing program, the confidence ranking, and a perfect picker.
charts.chart_takeaway: The confidence-ranking method has a shorter correct bar and a taller wrong bar than simply taking the first passing program, so it makes selection worse.

charts.chart_plain_title: Making the AI's writing more predictable does not solve more tasks
charts.chart_read: Across three writing settings, from most predictable to most varied, two rates over 24 tasks: how often a workable program appeared and how often the chosen program was actually correct. Higher is better for both.
charts.chart_takeaway: No setting stands out: the most predictable setting solves slightly more tasks but yields fewer workable candidates, a wash with no clear winner.

qwen35_4b_repo_search_compress_bank.verdict_tag: Training only on clean successes broke recovery
qwen35_4b_repo_search_compress_bank.concept_primer: Imagine teaching someone to fix cars by only ever showing repairs that went perfectly the first try. They memorize the smooth cases but never learn what to do when a fix fails and needs another attempt.
qwen35_4b_repo_search_compress_bank.plain_question: If we train a small coding assistant only on cleaned-up examples of successful bug fixes, does it get better at repairing unfamiliar code it has never seen before?
qwen35_4b_repo_search_compress_bank.plain_answer: No. It became flawless on the exact kinds of code repositories it studied, but on unfamiliar ones its success rate dropped sharply — worse than simply letting the untrained model try more times. Stripped of any failure examples, it kept resubmitting the same rejected fix.
qwen35_4b_repo_search_compress_bank.why_it_matters: When training a coding assistant, keep the messy recovery steps — the fixes that failed, the tests that failed, and the second attempts that finally worked. Examples where everything went right teach a narrow script, not real troubleshooting on new problems.

key_numbers.label: Familiar code repositories repaired
key_numbers.sub: after training on trimmed successes, it became perfect on the kinds it had studied

key_numbers.label: Unfamiliar code repositories repaired
key_numbers.sub: the very same training roughly halved its success rate on code it had never seen

key_numbers.label: Just sampling the untrained model more
key_numbers.sub: plain repeated tries beat the trained assistant on the unfamiliar code

key_numbers.label: Resubmitted the identical rejected fix
key_numbers.sub: on one unfamiliar family it re-sent the exact patch the tool had already refused

charts.chart_plain_title: Training helped the family it practiced on, but hurt brand-new families
charts.chart_read: Two groups of coding tasks: the repository family the model trained on, and four families it never saw. Each shows the share of tasks solved before training versus after. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: After training, the practiced family reached every task while the unseen families dropped from about two-thirds to a third — the skill did not carry over.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training examples balanced equally across the four repair steps
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one step of fixing code — inspect it, edit it, run the test, submit it — and its height counts the practice examples for that step. Equal-height bars mean the training set weights every step the same, not skewed toward one.
charts.chart_takeaway: All four bars sit at the same height, confirming the practice set covered inspecting, editing, testing, and submitting in equal amounts, so no single repair step was starved of examples.

qwen35_4b_retrieval_adapt_verify_scale.verdict_tag: Lookups help but trusting them fails
qwen35_4b_retrieval_adapt_verify_scale.concept_primer: Think of handing the model a cookbook of already-solved problems: for a task it can't crack alone, it finds the closest recipe and tweaks it to fit the new problem.
qwen35_4b_retrieval_adapt_verify_scale.plain_question: When the model gets stuck, does looking up a similar solved problem fix it, and can the system tell which fix to trust?
qwen35_4b_retrieval_adapt_verify_scale.plain_answer: Partly. Looking up a similar solved problem rescued a third of the stuck tasks, double a random lookup. But no automatic check could reliably tell the good fixes from the wrong ones, so most couldn't be safely trusted.
qwen35_4b_retrieval_adapt_verify_scale.why_it_matters: Retrieving and adapting past solutions is a real way to unstick a small model, but the payoff only lands if you can verify which candidate is right. Invest next in stronger checks, not more candidates.

key_numbers.label: Tasks the model solved on its own
key_numbers.sub: 56 of 80 problems

key_numbers.label: Stuck tasks rescued by looking up a similar solved problem
key_numbers.sub: 8 of 24 — double the 17% from random lookup

key_numbers.label: Best possible coverage if every good rescue were picked
key_numbers.sub: only with flawless picking

key_numbers.label: Committed answers that were actually wrong
key_numbers.sub: the shipped example checks couldn't separate right from wrong

charts.chart_plain_title: Meaning-matched lookup rescues the most stuck tasks
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a lookup strategy; bar height is the share of the 24 stuck tasks it rescued into the candidate pool, so taller is better. The meaning-matched lookup is tallest and plain copying is shortest.
charts.chart_takeaway: The meaning-matched bar reaches a third of stuck tasks, roughly double the random-lookup bar, showing that matching by meaning is what recovers stuck tasks.

charts.chart_plain_title: Every automatic picker commits as many wrong answers as right ones
charts.chart_read: For each picking method there are paired bars, one for correct answers committed and one for wrong ones, out of 24 stuck tasks; fewer wrong is better. Only the last method, a cheat that peeks at hidden answers, has no wrong bar.
charts.chart_takeaway: The wrong-commit bar matches the correct-commit bar for every real method, meaning the checks cannot separate right from wrong; only the peeking baseline avoids mistakes.

qwen35_4b_same_prefix_advantage_routing.verdict_tag: Design locked; the decisive test hasn't run
qwen35_4b_same_prefix_advantage_routing.concept_primer: Picture a student stuck halfway through a problem. Two tutor versions of the same model, one fast and one careful, each try to finish from that exact spot. You watch which one finishes correctly more often.
qwen35_4b_same_prefix_advantage_routing.plain_question: When the model stalls partway through an answer, can we reliably tell which tutor version finishes it best, and then train the model to copy that tutor?
qwen35_4b_same_prefix_advantage_routing.plain_answer: Not yet. The plan is fully locked and the plumbing is verified: the model, the two tutors, and the scoring machinery all load and behave correctly. But the central test, whether a genuinely better tutor even exists at these stuck spots, has not been run.
qwen35_4b_same_prefix_advantage_routing.why_it_matters: Before teaching a model to imitate a helper that looks better, confirm the helper truly wins on a fresh, separate batch of attempts. A choice judged on the very same attempts used to make it can easily fool you.

key_numbers.label: Attempts to pick the better tutor
key_numbers.sub: finish-the-answer tries from stuck spots

key_numbers.label: Separate attempts to confirm the pick
key_numbers.sub: an untouched set, so the choice cannot fool itself

key_numbers.label: Training batches each round: copy tutors vs stay near original
key_numbers.sub: three-quarters imitates the tutors, one-quarter guards against drifting

charts.chart_plain_title: Equal attempts for picking a tutor and double-checking it
charts.chart_read: Two bars show how many finish-the-answer attempts the plan reserves. The left bar picks which tutor looks best; the right, equally tall bar is a separate untouched set kept only to confirm that pick. A large confirm set is the careful choice.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two bars are exactly the same height: every attempt spent picking a tutor is matched by a fresh, untouched attempt spent verifying that pick.

charts.chart_plain_title: Each training round copies the tutors but keeps ties to the original
charts.chart_read: Three bars show how many training batches each round spends. Two equal bars copy the fast tutor and the careful tutor; a shorter third bar practices staying close to the original model so it does not drift. Taller copying bars mean more imitation.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two tutor bars are equal and each taller than the anchor bar: three-quarters of training imitates the tutors, one-quarter guards against drifting from the original.

qwen35_4b_sampler_portfolio_scheduler.verdict_tag: Shippable schedulers didn't beat sampling more
qwen35_4b_sampler_portfolio_scheduler.concept_primer: Think of several dice, each weighted to favor different answers. A 'portfolio' rolls a few styles and a manager decides which style deserves more rolls on each problem, hoping to beat just rolling one die harder.
qwen35_4b_sampler_portfolio_scheduler.plain_question: Can mixing several answer-writing styles and picking the best one per problem solve more coding tasks than just generating more tries from a single style?
qwen35_4b_sampler_portfolio_scheduler.plain_answer: No. Every manager we could actually ship tied or fell behind simply rolling one style harder. Only a perfect-hindsight picker did better, solving 75% of problems versus 67% for sample-more, showing the payoff exists but isn't yet reachable.
qwen35_4b_sampler_portfolio_scheduler.why_it_matters: The room to improve is real but only a smarter style-picker can claim it. Invest in predicting which style will help each problem, not in more blind sampling or scaling one style.

key_numbers.label: Best a shippable manager reached
key_numbers.sub: problems solved — same as just sampling more, no gain

key_numbers.label: If you always picked the right style
key_numbers.sub: hindsight-only ceiling, not something you can deploy yet

key_numbers.label: Compute for that ceiling
key_numbers.sub: about 22k vs 44k units, cheaper than sampling more

charts.chart_plain_title: Which answer-writing style wins, per problem, across six approaches
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one approach for choosing generation styles; height is the share of 24 coding problems solved, so taller is better. The far-right bar is a perfect-hindsight picker; the others are approaches you could actually ship.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the rightmost hindsight bar rises clearly above the rest, while every shippable approach sits level with or below simply sampling more from one style.

charts.chart_plain_title: Solving power of five style schedules on a larger 80-problem pool
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a schedule for spending generation effort; height is the share of 80 problems solved, taller is better. The far-right bar throws every candidate answer at each problem regardless of cost.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained-scheduler bar actually dips below the plain fixed schedules, and the towering far-right bar shows lots of solvable problems nobody's approach reached.

charts.chart_plain_title: Compute spent by each approach on the 24-problem set
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one approach; height is the total compute it burned across 24 problems, so shorter is better. Read it alongside the first chart, which shows how many problems each solved.
charts.chart_takeaway: The perfect-hindsight bar is one of the shortest, about half the compute of sampling-more, yet it solved the most problems, so its payoff is cheap not costly.

qwen35_4b_simulation_keystone_repair.verdict_tag: Repairing the core skill did not transfer
qwen35_4b_simulation_keystone_repair.concept_primer: Picture the model as a bundle of separate habits, each tied to one shape of question and one shape of answer. Here the shaky habit was tracing a multi-step process in your head.
qwen35_4b_simulation_keystone_repair.plain_question: If we perfectly fix a small model's ability to trace a process step by step, do the harder skills that seem to lean on it improve too?
qwen35_4b_simulation_keystone_repair.plain_answer: No. We fully repaired the step-by-step tracing habit, yet every harder skill that appeared to depend on it stayed flat. The improvement stayed locked to the exact question-and-answer shape we trained, disproving the idea that fixing a foundation ripples outward.
qwen35_4b_simulation_keystone_repair.why_it_matters: For a small fixed model, do not expect repairing a foundational skill to spread to related tasks. Training buys only the exact task shape you drill, plus close look-alikes. When a piece is missing, hand the model a tool instead.

key_numbers.label: Tracing accuracy, short vs long process
key_numbers.sub: one step chained versus four steps chained

key_numbers.label: Longest process: deliberate vs snap answer
key_numbers.sub: pausing to think more than doubles accuracy

key_numbers.label: Accuracy kept per added step
key_numbers.sub: each new step in the chain costs more than the last

charts.chart_plain_title: Tracing a process step by step gets worse as steps pile up
charts.chart_read: Moving right chains more steps into the process; height is how often the model traces the whole thing correctly, so higher is better. The upper line pauses to think first, the lower line answers immediately.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines fall steeply, from near-perfect at one step to about a third correct by four steps, so the tracing habit is genuinely broken on longer chains.

charts.chart_plain_title: Deliberating first generally beats answering immediately
charts.chart_read: Same left-to-right count of chained steps and the same higher-is-better correctness. Two lines pause to think, two answer immediately; within each pair, one chain includes steps that throw information away and one does not.
charts.chart_takeaway: The deliberate lines mostly sit above the snap-answer lines, and the snap-answer information-discarding chain sits lowest, so thinking helps rather than hurts the tracing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Each extra step costs more accuracy than the one before
charts.chart_read: Each bar shows how much of the previous length's accuracy survives when one more step is added to the chain; taller bars mean less was lost. Left adds a second step, right adds a fourth.
charts.chart_takeaway: The bars shrink from left to right, so damage from each added step keeps growing, meaning the failure is an accelerating collapse rather than one fixed error rate.

qwen35_4b_sketch_coverage_shift_probe.verdict_tag: Only hand-written templates survive a new task
qwen35_4b_sketch_coverage_shift_probe.concept_primer: Think of a fill-in-the-blank blueprint: the system fills the blanks to build many candidate programs, then keeps the ones that run correctly. The test is whether the right program still lands in that candidate pile.
qwen35_4b_sketch_coverage_shift_probe.plain_question: When the task changes to need a genuinely new operation, does the correct program still show up among the candidates the system builds?
qwen35_4b_sketch_coverage_shift_probe.plain_answer: It depends on the blueprint. When the blueprint explicitly names the new operation, the correct program appears every time; when the blueprint is auto-generated it appears only 17% of the time, and a bare blueprint never finds it.
qwen35_4b_sketch_coverage_shift_probe.why_it_matters: Before building a smarter picker to choose among candidates, invest in blueprints that name and shape the new operations. A perfect picker cannot rescue a correct program that was never built in the first place.

key_numbers.label: New-operation task, auto-generated blueprint
key_numbers.sub: how often the correct program was even among the candidates

key_numbers.label: New-operation task, hand-written blueprint
key_numbers.sub: naming the operation in the blueprint fixes it completely

key_numbers.label: New-operation task, bare blueprint
key_numbers.sub: the correct program never appeared at all

charts.chart_plain_title: Does the right program survive when the task shifts?
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars across three task types (familiar task, same task just renamed, task needing a new operation). Within each, three bars for auto-generated, hand-written, and bare blueprints. Taller means the correct program was in the candidate pile more often; full height is best.
charts.chart_takeaway: Hand-written blueprint bars stay at full height everywhere, while the auto-generated bar collapses to a sliver once the task needs a genuinely new operation.

charts.chart_plain_title: Allowing more fill-ins per blank does not rescue auto blueprints
charts.chart_read: Lines show how often the correct program appears on the new-operation task as you allow more options per blank (left to right). Higher is better. One line for hand-written, one for auto-generated, one for bare blueprints.
charts.chart_takeaway: The hand-written line climbs to full, but the auto and bare lines stay flat along the bottom, because the new operation is simply never proposed no matter how many options each blank gets.

charts.chart_plain_title: Extra check-queries sharpen a choice but cannot create a missing program
charts.chart_read: Lines show how often the chosen program is actually correct as the system spends more clarifying queries (left to right). Higher is better. Two lines have the right program available; one line (new operation, auto blueprint) does not.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two lines with the right program available climb to 100% within two or three queries, while the line missing that program stays flat, showing queries help only after the answer already exists.

charts.chart_plain_title: Where the correct answer survives: by task family and sketch style
charts.chart_read: A grid: each row is a task family, each column a way of giving the model a template (auto-built, hand-written, or none). Colour is the share of tasks where the correct program stayed reachable — darker is higher.
charts.chart_takeaway: The hand-written column is dark all the way down, but the auto column turns pale on the primitive families and the no-template column is pale almost everywhere — the template, not the family, decides survival.

qwen35_4b_specialist_policy_integration.verdict_tag: One required target was mathematically impossible
qwen35_4b_specialist_policy_integration.concept_primer: Imagine training four separate tutors, each an expert in one subject, then merging their know-how into a single all-rounder. The plan only counts a tutor if it lifts its subject's score by a fixed margin.
qwen35_4b_specialist_policy_integration.plain_question: Can you build four subject-expert versions of a small AI and merge them into one, if each expert must first clear a fixed improvement margin?
qwen35_4b_specialist_policy_integration.plain_answer: No. The plan never ran. One subject already scored 99 out of 100, so the rule that every expert improve by 10 points demanded an impossible 109. Because all four experts were required, the whole build stopped before any training began.
qwen35_4b_specialist_policy_integration.why_it_matters: Before training and merging expert versions of a model, check each required improvement is reachable under the scoring cap. A subject already near the top leaves no room, and one impossible target can block the whole plan.

key_numbers.label: Strongest subject's starting score
key_numbers.sub: already almost perfect

key_numbers.label: Score that subject was told to reach
key_numbers.sub: impossible past the 100 mark scores top out at

key_numbers.label: Experts required to proceed
key_numbers.sub: one blocked target stops the whole plan

charts.chart_plain_title: One required target rose above the highest possible score
charts.chart_read: Four subject areas sit along the bottom. For each, one bar shows today's score, a taller bar shows the score its expert was required to reach, and a line marks the 100% cap. A required bar poking above the cap is impossible.
charts.chart_takeaway: For the tools subject the required bar rises above the 100% cap while the other three stay below it, and that single impossible target halted everything.

qwen35_4b_static_bridge_ceiling_breaker.concept_primer: Think of a repair technician handed a broken recipe plus a few taste-tests showing how the dish should come out; the job is to rewrite the recipe so it matches. Here the model repairs a broken mini-program the same way.
qwen35_4b_static_bridge_ceiling_breaker.plain_question: Does showing the model a few worked repair examples help it fix brand-new kinds of broken programs it never trained on?
qwen35_4b_static_bridge_ceiling_breaker.plain_answer: It depends. Adding a handful of worked repair examples fixed every practiced problem type and roughly doubled success on brand-new unseen types, from about 20% to 44%, but that still leaves most new problems unfixed.
qwen35_4b_static_bridge_ceiling_breaker.why_it_matters: A few worked examples reliably nail the familiar cases and clearly help on new ones, so they are worth adding, but do not expect them to carry the model across genuinely harder, unfamiliar problems on their own.

key_numbers.label: New unseen problems fixed
key_numbers.sub: without vs with worked examples

key_numbers.label: Practiced problems fixed
key_numbers.sub: worked examples saturate the familiar cases

key_numbers.label: Fixes when the example is removed
key_numbers.sub: success collapses without a correct worked example

charts.chart_plain_title: A few worked examples help on new problems, but only partway
charts.chart_read: Three training setups along the bottom: no worked examples, plus 60, plus 80. For each, two bars: problem types the model practiced versus brand-new unseen types. Taller means more programs fixed correctly; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Adding worked examples lifts the practiced bars to full and roughly doubles the unseen bars, but the unseen bars still sit well below half.

charts.chart_plain_title: The fixes rely on the model actually reading the worked example
charts.chart_read: Three bars for one trained model on unseen problems: given a correct worked example, given none, and given a scrambled one. Height is the share of programs fixed correctly; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The correct-example bar is tallest and the scrambled one is lowest, showing the model reads the example rather than guessing from surface patterns.

charts.chart_plain_title: Some new problem types jump to near-perfect, others stay stuck
charts.chart_read: Six unseen problem types along the bottom, two bars each: the model without worked examples versus with 60 of them. Height is the share fixed correctly; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Several types leap from zero to near-perfect while others stay at zero or slip, showing help is very uneven across problem types.

qwen35_4b_strategy_token_diversity_lora.verdict_tag: Style-tagging guesses did not recover more misses
qwen35_4b_strategy_token_diversity_lora.concept_primer: Imagine a puzzle-solver that fails a few coding puzzles on a quick attempt. To crack them, you can let it guess many more times, or nudge it toward different solving styles — loops, recursion, math — hoping variety helps.
qwen35_4b_strategy_token_diversity_lora.plain_question: When a cheap first pass fails on some coding puzzles, can teaching the model to tag its guesses with a solving style recover more of them than just taking more plain guesses?
qwen35_4b_strategy_token_diversity_lora.plain_answer: No. Teaching the model to label its guesses by solving style recovered about 38% of the missed puzzles — no better than simply taking more plain guesses at the same cost, which recovered 42%. A version with the style labels scrambled did just as well, so the labels themselves added nothing.
qwen35_4b_strategy_token_diversity_lora.why_it_matters: When cheap sampling misses hard problems, spend the budget on more varied plain guessing rather than training a style-tagging add-on. Tagging did not improve recovery, and only far more expensive pooling of many guesses reached noticeably higher.

key_numbers.label: Missed puzzles recovered by style-tagging
key_numbers.sub: out of the 24 the quick pass missed

key_numbers.label: Missed puzzles recovered by plain extra guesses
key_numbers.sub: same cost, and it comes out ahead

key_numbers.label: Same setup with the style labels scrambled
key_numbers.sub: matched the real labels, so the labels did not matter

charts.chart_plain_title: Recovering missed puzzles: style add-on vs simply guessing more
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one sampling approach; its height is the share of the 24 initially-missed puzzles that approach eventually solves. Taller is better. The two rightmost bars pool the most guesses; the middle bars use the style add-on.
charts.chart_takeaway: The style add-on bar sits no higher than plain extra guessing, and the scrambled-label bar matches it — the style labels added nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of all 80 puzzles solved, by sampling approach
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one sampling approach; its height is the fraction of all 80 puzzles solved. Taller is better. The left bar is the cheap quick pass; the far-right bars pool the most guesses.
charts.chart_takeaway: The style add-on bars land in the same 81-to-83% band as plain heavier guessing; only the far more expensive pooled approaches climb higher.

qwen35_4b_structure_or_values.verdict_tag: The bottleneck is the steps, not the numbers
qwen35_4b_structure_or_values.concept_primer: Solving these tasks is like following a recipe: you need the right sequence of steps and the right amounts to plug in. This brief asks which of the two the model actually gets stuck on.
qwen35_4b_structure_or_values.plain_question: When the model fails these multi-step problems, is it choosing the wrong sequence of steps, or the right steps with the wrong numbers plugged in?
qwen35_4b_structure_or_values.plain_answer: The steps. The model fails because it can't find the right sequence of steps; whenever it is handed the correct steps, filling in the numbers always succeeds, so wrong numbers are never the hidden problem.
qwen35_4b_structure_or_values.why_it_matters: To crack these problems, put effort into helping the model find the right sequence of steps, for example by searching or enumerating step-orders, rather than fixing the numbers, which take care of themselves.

key_numbers.label: Solved when handed the correct steps
key_numbers.sub: filling in the numbers always finishes

key_numbers.label: Solved on its own (3-step tasks)
key_numbers.sub: it rarely finds the right steps

key_numbers.label: Extra solved from right steps but wrong numbers
key_numbers.sub: no hidden wrong-number failures to unlock

key_numbers.label: Solved by trying 200 random step-sequences
key_numbers.sub: random steps are far from enough

charts.chart_plain_title: Right steps, not right numbers, is what the model lacks
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is one way of solving the tasks; taller means more solved. Left to right: the model's one try, its best of several tries, whether its steps matched the correct sequence, and the model simply handed the correct steps. Within a cluster, one bar is easier 2-step tasks, the other harder 3-step tasks.
charts.chart_takeaway: The 'steps matched' bar is exactly as tall as the model's own success, while 'handed the correct steps' jumps to full height, so misses are wrong-step, not wrong-number.

charts.chart_plain_title: Random step-sequences barely crack the hard tasks
charts.chart_read: Lines show solve rate (higher is better) as more randomly guessed step-sequences are tried, each filled in with searched numbers. The upper line is easier 2-step tasks, the lower line the harder 3-step tasks.
charts.chart_takeaway: Even after 200 random step-sequences the 3-step line reaches only about 11%, far short of full success when the correct steps are known, so random steps can't stand in for the right ones.

qwen35_4b_structure_search_scaling.verdict_tag: Brute-force search keeps beating the model
qwen35_4b_structure_search_scaling.concept_primer: Think of each task as a multi-step recipe. You can either have the model write the recipe from memory, or mechanically try every possible recipe and keep the one a calculator confirms works.
qwen35_4b_structure_search_scaling.plain_question: As tasks need more steps, does the model's own recipe-writing ever start beating a try-everything search?
qwen35_4b_structure_search_scaling.plain_answer: No. Going from 3-step to 4-step tasks, the model's recipe-writing collapsed from 51% to 10%, while try-everything search stayed near-perfect at 97%, so the gap only widened.
qwen35_4b_structure_search_scaling.why_it_matters: When a calculator-style tool is available, lean on mechanical search to assemble multi-step solutions. Longer tasks make the model a worse structure-proposer, not a better one, so don't expect its instincts to take over.

key_numbers.label: Model writes the recipe (3 vs 4 steps)
key_numbers.sub: collapses as tasks get longer

key_numbers.label: Try-everything search (3 vs 4 steps)
key_numbers.sub: stays near-perfect

key_numbers.label: How far the model trails search
key_numbers.sub: the gap widens, never closes

charts.chart_plain_title: As tasks get longer, the model falls further behind mechanical search
charts.chart_read: Two lines over task length (3 vs 4 steps); vertical axis is success rate, higher is better. One line is the model writing the recipe from memory, the other is trying every possible recipe with a tool.
charts.chart_takeaway: The model's line plunges while the search line stays flat near the top; longer tasks make the model worse, so the two lines spread apart rather than crossing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Number of recipes to test explodes as tasks get longer
charts.chart_read: A single rising line over task length (3 to 5 steps); vertical axis counts how many possible recipes a full search must check, which multiplies roughly 16-fold per added step.
charts.chart_takeaway: The line rockets from a few thousand to about a million by 5 steps, so search eventually gets too costly, but the model's recipe-writing has already collapsed before that point.

qwen35_4b_substrate_coverage_ladder.verdict_tag: Reusable code parts did not generalize
qwen35_4b_substrate_coverage_ladder.concept_primer: Think of a parts kit: instead of writing each hard coding problem from scratch, you try to build it by snapping together reusable code building blocks pulled from a library of past solutions.
qwen35_4b_substrate_coverage_ladder.plain_question: If you build hard coding problems from a library of reusable code parts, can you solve ones that a big batch of direct attempts couldn't?
qwen35_4b_substrate_coverage_ladder.plain_answer: No. Reusing and adapting past solutions solved none of the 9 hard tasks, beating nothing. Parts hand-built for each specific task did solve all 9, but those were custom-carved per task, not reusable, so the reuse idea failed.
qwen35_4b_substrate_coverage_ladder.why_it_matters: Do not expect a shared library of reused solutions to expand what a model can solve, and never trust the small visible sample of tests as a source of correct training targets; they routinely hide wrong answers.

key_numbers.label: Hard tasks solved by reusing adapted past solutions
key_numbers.sub: none — the reuse idea failed

key_numbers.label: Hard tasks solved by parts hand-built per task
key_numbers.sub: but custom-made each time, not reusable

key_numbers.label: Passed the visible sample checks yet secretly wrong
key_numbers.sub: of answers that looked right on shown tests

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of hard tasks solved, by how the answer was built
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one way of building solutions; bar height is the share of the 9 hard tasks fully solved, taller is better. Custom-built-per-task and the combined pool reach the top; reusing adapted past solutions sits flat at the bottom.
charts.chart_takeaway: The reuse bar is flat at zero while the custom-built bar reaches full height, so reusing past solutions solved nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often answers that looked right on sample tests were secretly wrong
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a building method; height is the share of answers that passed the small visible sample of tests but failed the full checks, so lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every bar is high, from 41% to 100%, meaning passing the visible sample tests routinely hides a wrong answer.

charts.chart_plain_title: Answers that passed sample tests versus those that were truly correct
charts.chart_read: For each building method, the taller bar counts answers passing the visible sample tests and the shorter bar counts those that also passed the full checks; a big gap means many false wins.
charts.chart_takeaway: For reusing past solutions, 8 answers passed the sample tests but zero survived the full checks.

charts.chart_plain_title: How many tries each method needed per task
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a building method; height is the average number of candidate answers generated per task, so lower is cheaper.
charts.chart_takeaway: The expanded hand-built method reached full coverage at about 15 tries per task, far cheaper than the combined pool's roughly 57.

qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round1.verdict_tag: Steering the model's word choices backfired
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round1.concept_primer: As a model reasons through a problem step by step, certain moments act like forks in the road, where one next word leads toward a right answer and another toward a wrong one. We tried coaching it to prefer the winning word.
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round1.plain_question: If we find the forks where one word choice led to more right answers, and coach the model to prefer that word at those moments, does it solve more problems?
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round1.plain_answer: No. Coaching the model toward the winning word made it slightly worse, not better, dropping a few points on fresh test problems. And randomly scrambling which word counted as "winning" did nearly identical damage, showing the harm came from the coaching procedure itself, not from any real signal.
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round1.why_it_matters: Before coaching a model to favor certain word choices, check that the word being demoted was a confident mistake, not a near-tie. Forcing a strong preference between near-even options disrupts the flow of reasoning and quietly costs accuracy.

key_numbers.label: Problems solved after coaching, shorter thinking
key_numbers.sub: the coached model scored below the untrained one

key_numbers.label: Problems solved after coaching, longer thinking
key_numbers.sub: the drop grew when the model thought longer

key_numbers.label: How often thinking finished on its own
key_numbers.sub: the coached model ran out of thinking room twice as often

charts.chart_plain_title: Coached and scrambled-label models both scored below the untrained model
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of fresh test problems a model solved, so taller is better. Three models are shown: untrained, coached toward winning words, and a scrambled-label control, at two thinking-length settings side by side.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both trained bars sit below the untrained bar at both settings, and the scrambled control drops almost as much, so the training procedure, not the steering, caused the loss.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often thinking finished on its own, before and after coaching
charts.chart_read: Each bar shows how often the model wrapped up its reasoning on its own within the thinking room it had, instead of being cut off, so taller is better. Untrained is compared against the two trained models.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both trained bars are about half the height of the untrained bar, meaning reasoning stopped on its own only half as often, so the coached model ran out of room and got cut off.

qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round2.verdict_tag: Nudging word choices did not improve reasoning
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round2.concept_primer: This 4-billion-word model thinks out loud, choosing one word at a time. At some forks it confidently picks a word that steers it into a wrong answer. The test: adjust its internal settings to nudge it toward the better word.
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round2.plain_question: If you catch the exact moments where the model confidently picks a wrong word and nudge it toward the word that leads to a right answer, does it get smarter overall?
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round2.plain_answer: No. Nudging those word choices barely helped and often hurt. Pulling the model toward the better word was gentler than pushing the wrong word away, and it beat training on deliberately scrambled labels, but the untouched model still fixed more broken code. The edits spilled into unrelated choices.
