qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round2.why_it_matters: When you adjust a model's internal settings to fix one word choice, the change leaks into many unrelated choices and can quietly erase real skill. Prove an edit stays local before spending effort gathering more training examples.

key_numbers.label: Broken code repositories fully fixed
key_numbers.sub: the untouched model beat every trained version

key_numbers.label: Spillover into unrelated word choices
key_numbers.sub: pulling up was gentler than pushing down, yet still too broad

key_numbers.label: Gap between real and scrambled labels
key_numbers.sub: real outcomes carried signal, but only on familiar tasks

charts.chart_plain_title: Fixing one word still shifts many unrelated word choices
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one training method. Height shows how far the model's other, unrelated word preferences moved as a side effect; shorter is better. A line marks the safe limit that none of the methods stayed under.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three bars clear the safety line, and pushing the wrong word down is the tallest — the most disruptive method — while pulling the right word up spills less.

charts.chart_plain_title: The unchanged model still fixes the most broken code
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one setup working through fresh batches of broken code repositories; height is the share fully fixed and passing hidden tests, so taller is better. The leftmost bar is the original, untrained model given one long work session.
charts.chart_takeaway: The untouched model with one long uninterrupted work session is the tallest bar — every trained version, and the version that split its effort into two short tries, fixed fewer.

charts.chart_plain_title: Real labels help on familiar tasks but not new ones
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one test. Height shows how much better training on real outcomes did than training on scrambled outcomes; above the zero line means real labels helped, below means they hurt. Tests span familiar practice tasks and brand-new ones.
charts.chart_takeaway: Real labels win on the familiar practice tasks and the coding task, but the bar dips below zero at the longer reasoning setting — the benefit does not hold everywhere.

qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_controller.verdict_tag: Cheaper thinking, but no accuracy gain
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_controller.concept_primer: Some models "think" — working through a problem step by step before giving a final answer. Thinking takes effort, and like a student mulling a question, more thinking helps hard problems but wastes effort on easy ones.
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_controller.plain_question: When a model can spend more or less effort thinking before answering a coding task, can a simple self-check let it think longer only when needed, saving effort without losing accuracy?
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_controller.plain_answer: It depends: letting the model draft a quick answer and think harder only when that draft fails an example test shown in the prompt matched the accuracy of moderate fixed-effort settings while using a quarter to half the thinking effort — but it never beat the single best fixed-effort setting.
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_controller.why_it_matters: To cut the effort a reasoning model spends per task, have it draft a quick answer and think harder only when a cheap self-check fails. Expect big savings at the same accuracy, not a higher accuracy ceiling.

key_numbers.label: Thinking effort per task, at matched accuracy
key_numbers.sub: same accuracy as a moderate fixed setting, at under half the thinking

key_numbers.label: Best accuracy reached
key_numbers.sub: the single best fixed setting; the quick-check controller reached 88-89%

key_numbers.label: Accuracy ceiling
key_numbers.sub: the most any perfect per-task effort choice could achieve

key_numbers.label: Wrong answers that slipped past the self-check
key_numbers.sub: drafts that passed the shown example but failed the hidden tests

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy versus thinking effort: quick-check controller against fixed settings
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is average thinking effort per task; the vertical axis is accuracy. One line is fixed-effort settings, another is the quick-check controller, and a single point marks a perfect per-task chooser. Up and to the left is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The controller's line sits well to the left of the fixed-effort line at the same height — matching accuracy for far less thinking — while the fixed line climbs higher only at its peak.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy by strategy: controllers give up a little for big savings
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one strategy's accuracy, from no thinking through fixed-effort settings, two quick-check controllers, and a perfect per-task chooser. Taller bars mean more correct answers.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two controller bars land just below the best fixed bar and the perfect-chooser bar — a small accuracy gap in exchange for the large effort savings.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often the quick self-check passed a wrong answer
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of a strategy's committed answers that passed the shown example test but failed the hidden tests. Lower bars mean a more trustworthy self-check.
charts.chart_takeaway: The controller bars stand taller than the fixed bar — the more the model trusts the quick check, the more wrong answers slip through, which caps its accuracy.

qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_scaling.verdict_tag: Capping the model's thinking boosts real accuracy
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_scaling.concept_primer: Before answering, this small AI can write private scratch-work, reasoning to itself like a student jotting notes before committing to a final answer. You can limit how much scratch-work it does, and this page asks whether allowing it helps.
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_scaling.plain_question: If we let this small AI think to itself before answering basic coding problems, does the answer it would actually ship get more accurate, and does more thinking always help?
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_scaling.plain_answer: Yes. Letting it think lifted the accuracy of the single answer you would ship, from 76% to 91% on coding tasks, a real gain in final answers, not just more varied guesses. But a moderate limit beat letting it think without any cap.
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_scaling.why_it_matters: Turn on this scratch-work mode for basic coding tasks; it delivers real accuracy gains, biggest on the harder problems. But cap how much thinking you allow, because letting the model think without limit performs worse than a sensible ceiling.

key_numbers.label: Accuracy of the answer you would ship
key_numbers.sub: thinking off, then thinking capped

key_numbers.label: Thinking with no limit
key_numbers.sub: worse than a capped amount at 91%

key_numbers.label: Gain on the harder tasks
key_numbers.sub: middling tasks: thinking off vs capped

charts.chart_plain_title: Capped thinking beats both no thinking and unlimited thinking
charts.chart_read: Bars along the bottom run from no thinking up to unlimited thinking. Height is coding accuracy, higher is better. One color is the single answer you would ship; the other is the best of eight tries, an unreachable upper limit.
charts.chart_takeaway: The shippable-answer bars climb most as thinking turns on and peak at a moderate cap, so the gain shows up in real answers, not just the best-of-eight upper limit.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy rises then falls as the thinking budget grows
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how much thinking the model is allowed, from none on the left to unlimited on the right. Height is coding accuracy, higher is better. Three lines are three ways of scoring the answer.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three lines rise to a hump around a mid-sized amount of thinking, then flatten or dip, showing that piling on more thinking eventually stops helping and can hurt.

charts.chart_plain_title: Thinking helps most on the hardest tasks
charts.chart_read: Tasks are grouped by how hard they were without thinking: easy, middling, and never solved. Each group shows two bars, accuracy with thinking off versus with a fixed amount of thinking. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The gap between the two bars widens from left to right: thinking barely changes easy tasks but sharply lifts the hardest ones, including some the model never solved otherwise.

charts.chart_plain_title: Scrambling the thinking still recovers part of the gain
charts.chart_read: At two thinking-budget sizes, three bars each: accuracy with no thinking, with the model's own thinking scrambled into random order, and with its real ordered thinking. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The scrambled-thinking bar sits partway between no thinking and real thinking, so much of the benefit comes from simply doing the extra work, not only from thinking in a sensible order.

qwen35_4b_thinking_content_vs_compute.verdict_tag: The gain is real reasoning, not busywork
qwen35_4b_thinking_content_vs_compute.concept_primer: Before answering, the model can jot private scratch notes. We test whether the payoff comes from the act of writing anything, or from the notes actually making sense.
qwen35_4b_thinking_content_vs_compute.plain_question: Does the model's thinking help because it does genuine reasoning, or just because writing extra words buys more processing time?
qwen35_4b_thinking_content_vs_compute.plain_answer: Genuine reasoning. Real, meaningful notes lifted problems solved from 75% to 86%, while blank filler and scrambled notes added nothing, and notes copied from a different problem sent the model to the wrong answer.
qwen35_4b_thinking_content_vs_compute.why_it_matters: Give the model room to reason and let it write its own coherent notes; padding the output with filler or irrelevant text wastes effort and pasting in unrelated notes actively derails it.

key_numbers.label: Solved with thinking off
key_numbers.sub: baseline, no scratch notes

key_numbers.label: Solved with real reasoning
key_numbers.sub: the model's own coherent notes

key_numbers.label: Solved with another problem's notes
key_numbers.sub: wrong notes drag it to the wrong problem

key_numbers.label: Gain from blank filler
key_numbers.sub: extra processing time alone buys nothing

charts.chart_plain_title: Coding problems solved for each kind of scratch notes
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of coding problems solved (taller is better) under five setups: no notes, blank filler dots, scrambled notes, the model's own real notes, and notes copied from a different problem.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the real-notes bar rises above the no-notes baseline; the copied-from-another-problem bar collapses to almost nothing, showing the model actually follows its notes.

charts.chart_plain_title: What each ingredient of the notes actually adds
charts.chart_read: Bars show how much each added ingredient changes the share of problems solved (above zero is a gain, below is harm): extra blank compute, merely-present-but-scrambled words, coherent meaning, and wrong-problem notes.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the coherent-meaning bar is positive and it carries the whole gain, while blank compute and scrambled words sit at zero and wrong notes plunge far below.

charts.chart_plain_title: Real notes keep their edge as thinking room grows
charts.chart_read: The line shows how much better real notes do than scrambled notes (higher is a bigger edge) as the model is given more room to think, left to right.
charts.chart_takeaway: The line rises rather than falling, so the benefit of coherent thinking grows with more thinking room instead of washing out.

qwen35_4b_thinking_lookahead.verdict_tag: Extra thinking time cannot unlock planning
qwen35_4b_thinking_lookahead.concept_primer: Picture a 3-move puzzle. Recognizing an almost-finished puzzle (one move left) is easy; planning the very first move toward a goal still three moves away is the hard part.
qwen35_4b_thinking_lookahead.plain_question: Can letting the model think longer out loud let it plan the first move toward a goal that is still three moves away?
qwen35_4b_thinking_lookahead.plain_answer: No. Longer thinking leaves the model at random-guess level when it must plan the first of three moves toward a distant goal, even though the same thinking nearly doubles its accuracy when the goal is only one move away.
qwen35_4b_thinking_lookahead.why_it_matters: For genuine multi-step planning, more thinking time won't rescue a model — that gap needs actual training. Reserve extra thinking for recognizing near-finished problems, where it clearly pays off.

key_numbers.label: First move toward a 3-away goal
key_numbers.sub: still random-guess level even with the most thinking

key_numbers.label: Move when goal is 1 away
key_numbers.sub: thinking more than doubles recognition

key_numbers.label: Move when goal is 2 away
key_numbers.sub: thinking helps once the goal is closer

key_numbers.label: Random-guess level
key_numbers.sub: what pure luck would score

charts.chart_plain_title: Thinking helps only when the goal is close, not far
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars show how often the model picks the right next move (taller is better), grouped by how far the goal is (3, 2, or 1 move away) and colored by how much thinking time it got. Around 3% is random-guess level.
charts.chart_takeaway: Far-goal bars stay near random no matter the thinking time, while near-goal bars jump higher with more thinking, showing thinking sharpens recognition, not planning.

charts.chart_plain_title: Planning the first far-off move stays at random no matter the thinking time
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how much thinking time the model gets; the vertical axis is how often it plans the correct first move toward a 3-away goal (higher is better). One line is the model, the flat line marks random-guess level.
charts.chart_takeaway: The model's line hugs the random-guess line and stays flat as thinking time grows, meaning more thinking buys essentially no planning ability.

qwen35_4b_thinking_separability_probe.verdict_tag: Scrambled thinking reads as well as real
qwen35_4b_thinking_separability_probe.concept_primer: Imagine wiring the model like a lie detector: instead of trusting what it says, you read its internal 'brain' signals to guess whether the code it just wrote is actually correct.
qwen35_4b_thinking_separability_probe.plain_question: Can you tell if the model's freshly written code is right just by reading its internal signals, and does thinking help by actually reasoning?
qwen35_4b_thinking_separability_probe.plain_answer: Partly, but not through reasoning. Internal signals reveal correctness a bit better after the model thinks, yet scrambling that thinking into gibberish works just as well, so the gain comes from the act of thinking, not its logic.
qwen35_4b_thinking_separability_probe.why_it_matters: Don't count on the content of the model's step-by-step thinking to make it 'know' its own answer; the mere presence of thinking drives the effect. The internal read-out only weakly flags hidden failures, and only after thinking.

key_numbers.label: Telling right from wrong, no thinking
key_numbers.sub: detector score out of 100; 50 = a coin flip

key_numbers.label: Same detector after thinking
key_numbers.sub: any thinking makes correctness easier to read

key_numbers.label: Scrambled vs real thinking
key_numbers.sub: gibberish thinking reads just as well

key_numbers.label: Cheating safety check
key_numbers.sub: detector drops to coin-flip on fake labels

charts.chart_plain_title: How well internal signals reveal a right answer, by condition
charts.chart_read: Five conditions run along the bottom: no thinking, then real and scrambled thinking at two lengths. Tall dark bars show how reliably the detector reads correctness; short bars are a safety check that should sit near a coin flip. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every thinking bar rises above no-thinking, but the scrambled-thinking bars stand as tall or taller than the real-thinking ones, so the lift is not from reasoning.

charts.chart_plain_title: Detector strength at each internal layer of the model
charts.chart_read: Left to right is depth inside the model, from early to late layers; height is how reliably the detector reads correctness there. Each line is one condition; higher is better, and the no-thinking line sits lowest throughout.
charts.chart_takeaway: The scrambled-thinking lines run at or above the real-thinking lines at nearly every depth, while no-thinking stays pinned to the bottom.

charts.chart_plain_title: Spotting hidden failures among answers that pass the quick check
charts.chart_read: Five conditions along the bottom; height is how well the detector flags answers that pass a quick surface test but secretly fail the full test. Higher is better, and no-thinking sits near a coin flip.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only after the model thinks does the bar clear coin-flip level, and even then the signal is too weak to rely on by itself.

qwen35_4b_tool_seeded_banking.verdict_tag: Hardest programs crack open but barely stick
qwen35_4b_tool_seeded_banking.concept_primer: Think of a small program as a recipe that reshuffles a list of numbers, built by chaining simple steps. A one-step recipe is easy to write; a three-step recipe, needing three correct steps stacked in order, is much harder.
qwen35_4b_tool_seeded_banking.plain_question: Can a small AI learn to write the hardest three-step list programs if we first use a separate search tool to find correct examples for it to study?
qwen35_4b_tool_seeded_banking.plain_answer: It depends. Yes — feeding it correct three-step examples, found by a separate brute-force search tool, lifted its three-step success from a flat zero to about 12% when allowed many tries. But in single-attempt use it stayed at zero, and four-step programs never budged.
qwen35_4b_tool_seeded_banking.why_it_matters: To push a small model toward harder multi-step programs, use an outside search tool to supply correct examples at each new difficulty level. Expect the gains to weaken as programs get deeper, and each level to need its own fresh examples.

key_numbers.label: Three-step programs solved, with many tries
key_numbers.sub: share of new programs it got right, before vs after studying search-found examples

key_numbers.label: Three-step programs solved in one attempt
key_numbers.sub: with a single try the gain vanished — not ready to deploy

key_numbers.label: Two-step programs solved, with many tries
key_numbers.sub: the easier level improved strongly, showing the method itself works

key_numbers.label: Four-step programs solved
key_numbers.sub: the next level up never moved at all

charts.chart_plain_title: Search-found examples unlock three-step programs, but only with many tries
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars for two-, three-, and four-step programs. Within each group: success before training, success after training when allowed many tries, and success in single-attempt use. Taller bars are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The three-step many-tries bar rises from flat zero to a short bar, while its single-attempt bar stays at zero — the gain appears only with repeated tries.

charts.chart_plain_title: More attempts slowly lift three-step success off zero
charts.chart_read: Success rate on three-step programs (up axis) as the model gets more attempts (rightward axis). Three lines: the untrained model, the trained model, and two-step programs for contrast. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained three-step line climbs gently above zero as attempts grow, while the untrained line stays pinned flat at the bottom; two-step climbs far higher.

qwen35_4b_tool_state_policy_lora.verdict_tag: Visible clues let it pick the right answer
qwen35_4b_tool_state_policy_lora.concept_primer: The model has two answers to each table task: a quick guess and a slower computed one. Like a student deciding whether to trust mental math or their worked-out calculation, something must pick which to submit.
qwen35_4b_tool_state_policy_lora.plain_question: Can the model reliably decide when to trust its quick guess versus its slower computed answer, using only clues it can actually see?
qwen35_4b_tool_state_policy_lora.plain_answer: Yes. A simple rule using visible clues picked correctly on 66% of tasks, up from 56% for always trusting the quick guess, matching the best score achievable even with perfect hindsight.
qwen35_4b_tool_state_policy_lora.why_it_matters: When a model can both guess and compute, a cheap check of whether the computed answer passed a visible test is enough to know which to submit, without ever overwriting an answer that was already right.

key_numbers.sub: Baseline that never switches answers

key_numbers.sub: The rule never wrecks a good answer

key_numbers.label: Tasks rescued by switching

charts.chart_read: Each bar is a strategy for choosing which answer to submit; height is the share of test tasks it got exactly right. Taller is better. The rightmost bar is the best-possible score if you knew the answers in advance.
charts.chart_takeaway: The simple clue-based rule bar reaches the same height as the best-possible bar, and clearly beats always-submitting-the-quick-answer, meaning the visible clues alone are enough to pick well.

charts.chart_plain_title: Fixes made versus good answers wrecked, per strategy
charts.chart_read: For each strategy, one bar counts tasks it rescued by switching to the computed answer (good) and the other counts tasks where it wrongly overwrote an already-correct quick answer (bad), out of 50. Fewer wrecked is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The simple rule and the trained chooser rescue several tasks while wrecking zero, whereas the two control strategies wreck 10 to 21, showing the clue-based approach only switches when it helps.

charts.chart_read: Two lines track training error as the chooser learns: one trained on real answers, one on deliberately scrambled answers. Lower means it is learning. The scrambled line is the sanity check that should stay high.
charts.chart_takeaway: The real-answer line drops to nearly zero while the scrambled line stays stuck high, confirming there is a genuine signal in the clues rather than accidental memorization.

qwen35_4b_trained_vs_frozen_repair_mdp.verdict_tag: Trained code fixer loses to simply retrying
qwen35_4b_trained_vs_frozen_repair_mdp.concept_primer: Imagine a student who gets a problem wrong. You could coach them to patch up their own broken answer, or you could just let them attempt the problem fresh a few more times. Which approach recovers more problems?
qwen35_4b_trained_vs_frozen_repair_mdp.plain_question: When a model's first tries at a small programming puzzle all fail, does training it to fix its own broken code recover more puzzles than just trying again?
qwen35_4b_trained_vs_frozen_repair_mdp.plain_answer: No. Across 150 programming puzzles, 57 had no working solution after four fresh tries. A fixer trained to repair broken code recovered just 2 of those, and misfired more often. An untrained fixer recovered 3, but spending the same effort on more fresh tries recovered the most: 5.
qwen35_4b_trained_vs_frozen_repair_mdp.why_it_matters: When first attempts fail, spend extra compute on more diverse fresh attempts rather than on training a specialized code fixer. A trained repair step can produce code that passes the visible checks but quietly fails the hidden ones.

charts.chart_takeaway: The more-fresh-attempts bar is tallest and the trained fixer is shortest, so retrying beat fixing and training the fixer made it worse.

charts.chart_takeaway: The trained fixer's bar is taller, meaning training made it more likely to output code that looks correct but quietly fails.

qwen35_4b_transform_abi_compiler_pilot.verdict_tag: Light tuning picks every right recipe
qwen35_4b_transform_abi_compiler_pilot.concept_primer: Think of a small library of data-cleanup recipes. For each task the model is handed a menu of candidate recipes and must pick the one whose output is correct; a machine then runs the chosen recipe exactly.
qwen35_4b_transform_abi_compiler_pilot.plain_question: Can a little tuning teach the model to pick the correct data-transformation recipe from a menu of candidates?
qwen35_4b_transform_abi_compiler_pilot.plain_answer: Yes. After a light round of tuning the model produced the correct output on 100% of the 48 tasks, up from 92% untuned and matching a perfect answer key. Simple example-matching rules already reached about 90%, so the menu was fairly easy.
qwen35_4b_transform_abi_compiler_pilot.why_it_matters: When answers can be run and checked, a small amount of tuning can push a fixed model to reliably choose the exact right procedure. But easy candidate menus flatter the score, so retest on less-templated tasks.

charts.chart_takeaway: The tuned-model bar rises to the same full height as the perfect answer key, while the untuned model's bar stops short.

charts.chart_read: Each approach shows two bars: one for single-step tasks and one for tasks needing several chained steps; height is the share solved correctly, taller is better. The tuned model reaches full height on both.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the tuned model tops out on both bar colors; the untuned model's multi-step bar dips below its single-step one.

charts.chart_takeaway: The tuned model's bar towers to full height while every other bar sits near or below half.

qwen35_4b_typed_sketch_synthesis.verdict_tag: Fill-in-the-blank templates beat writing whole fixes
qwen35_4b_typed_sketch_synthesis.concept_primer: Instead of writing a whole program fix in one go, the model writes a fill-in-the-blank template, and a separate checker tries every valid way to fill the blanks, keeping fills that pass the visible tests.
qwen35_4b_typed_sketch_synthesis.plain_question: Does letting the model sketch a fill-in-the-blank template, then have a checker complete it, fix broken programs better than writing the whole fix directly?
qwen35_4b_typed_sketch_synthesis.plain_answer: Yes. On the hardest problems, having the model write a fill-in-the-blank template and letting a checker try valid fills raised the pass rate from 33% to 88%, far beyond the model writing the whole fix itself.
qwen35_4b_typed_sketch_synthesis.why_it_matters: When a small model struggles to write deep, correct code in one shot, have it sketch the overall structure and let an automated checker fill and test the fiddly details.

charts.chart_takeaway: The template bar clears whole problem types the direct bar misses entirely, though direct still edges ahead on a couple of text-matching types.

qwen35_4b_unsaturated_frontier_active_br.verdict_tag: Even practice beat targeting weak spots
qwen35_4b_unsaturated_frontier_active_br.concept_primer: Picture a fixed study budget of practice problems. You can spread them evenly across every topic, or cram only the topics you currently flunk. This experiment tests which spending plan trains the model better.
qwen35_4b_unsaturated_frontier_active_br.plain_question: When you can only add a fixed batch of extra practice problems, is it better to target the model's weak spots than to spread practice evenly?
qwen35_4b_unsaturated_frontier_active_br.plain_answer: No. Spreading the extra practice evenly across all task types worked best, solving 98% of unseen tasks, while concentrating practice on the model's current mistakes reached only about 85% and left some task types unsolved.
qwen35_4b_unsaturated_frontier_active_br.why_it_matters: When your training budget is fixed and the task space is broad, spread practice evenly rather than chasing the model's current mistakes. Targeting fixes a few weak spots but quietly starves others.

charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by four training recipes (left to right: no extra practice, evenly-spread practice, two ways of cramming the model's own mistakes). Height is the share of tasks it gets fully right on unseen inputs; taller is better. Two bars per group: one single try versus the best of three tries.
charts.chart_takeaway: The evenly-spread bar towers near the top while both cram-the-mistakes bars sit well below it, so spreading practice broadly beat targeting weak spots.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much the shown worked-out steps actually help
charts.chart_read: Three bars for the same trained model, differing only in what worked-out solution steps it is shown alongside the broken program: correct steps, no steps, or deliberately jumbled steps. Height is the share of tasks solved correctly; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Accuracy falls sharply when the steps are removed and collapses when they are jumbled, showing the model genuinely reads and relies on them rather than ignoring them.

charts.chart_plain_title: On the hardest task types, targeted practice fixes some but abandons others
charts.chart_read: Five hardest task types along the bottom, with four colored bars each for the training recipes (no extra practice, evenly-spread, and two cram-the-mistakes versions). Height is share solved on unseen inputs; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The evenly-spread bars stay tall across every type, while the cram-the-mistakes bars swing to zero on at least one type, exposing the blind spots targeting creates.

qwen35_4b_verified_algorithm_retrieval_a.verdict_tag: Matched past solutions recover missed problems
qwen35_4b_verified_algorithm_retrieval_a.concept_primer: The model usually cracks a coding task by trying many times. For the ones it keeps missing, we hand it code from a similar problem it already solved, like adapting a proven recipe for a new dish.
qwen35_4b_verified_algorithm_retrieval_a.plain_question: When the model keeps failing certain coding problems, does handing it proven solutions to similar problems help it finally solve them?
qwen35_4b_verified_algorithm_retrieval_a.plain_answer: Yes. Giving the model a closely-matched past solution to adapt cracked 3 of the 8 problems that repeated tries kept missing, lifting overall success from 67% to 79%; unrelated solutions helped none.
qwen35_4b_verified_algorithm_retrieval_a.why_it_matters: Relevant past solutions are a cheap boost for stubborn problems, but the model often looks right on the shown examples while failing hidden ones, so add a checker before trusting an answer.

key_numbers.label: Problems solved overall
key_numbers.sub: with a matched past solution to adapt

key_numbers.label: Stuck problems newly cracked
key_numbers.sub: matched solutions vs 0 for unrelated ones

key_numbers.label: Looked right but were wrong
key_numbers.sub: passed shown examples yet failed hidden checks

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of problems solved: trying more alone vs adding matched solutions
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a different way of supplying candidate solutions; height is the share of 24 problems solved, so taller is better. The leftmost bar is trying more alone; the others add various retrieved solutions.
charts.chart_takeaway: The matched-solution bar rises highest, from 67% to 79%, while the unrelated-solution bar stays flat at 67%, so the content of the retrieved solution matters.

charts.chart_plain_title: Stuck problems newly cracked by each type of retrieved solution
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one type of retrieved solution; height is how many of the 8 problems that trying-more kept missing were newly solved, so taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Matched solutions crack 3 stuck problems versus 0 for unrelated ones, showing the match, not just more attempts, does the work.

charts.chart_plain_title: Answers that looked right on shown examples but failed hidden checks
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one type of retrieved solution; height is the share of answers that passed the shown examples yet still failed the hidden checks, so lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every bar is tall, from 57% up to 100%, meaning passing the shown examples alone is not safe enough to trust an answer.

qwen35_4b_verified_edit_closure.verdict_tag: Small verified tweaks recover real capability
qwen35_4b_verified_edit_closure.concept_primer: Think of a spell-checker for tiny programs: after the model writes a fix, a helper proposes small nearby edits, runs each against a few example tests, and keeps the one that passes.
qwen35_4b_verified_edit_closure.plain_question: On the hardest brand-new repair tasks, does trying small verified edits to the model's fix beat just picking its best raw attempt?
qwen35_4b_verified_edit_closure.plain_answer: Yes. On the toughest new tasks, adding small tested edits lifted fully-correct repairs from about 39% to about 52%, close to the 58% best-possible limit of that edit set, though not a step-change on its own.
qwen35_4b_verified_edit_closure.why_it_matters: When a small model writes an almost-right program, cheaply testing nearby edits recovers real wins. But keep edits conservative and expect gains only where the right fix is genuinely close by.

key_numbers.label: Hardest tasks fully fixed
key_numbers.sub: raw best pick vs. adding tested edits

key_numbers.label: Best-possible edit limit
key_numbers.sub: ceiling if you always picked the right edit

key_numbers.label: Tasks made worse
key_numbers.sub: halved by only accepting fully-tested fixes

charts.chart_plain_title: How often the hardest new tasks got fully fixed, by method
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one repair method; taller means more of the 120 hardest new tasks fully fixed. Left bars are the model's own picks, middle bars add small tested edits, and the rightmost bar is the best-possible pick within that edit set.
charts.chart_takeaway: The tested-edit bars jump well above the model's own picks and nearly reach the best-possible bar, showing the edits recover most of the reachable gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which task types the tested edits help, and which stay stuck
charts.chart_read: Each group is a task type; the three bars show the model's own picks, added tested edits, and the best-possible pick, counting tasks fully fixed out of 12. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Some task types leap from zero to nine once edits are added, but two families stay at zero even at best-possible, meaning the edit set simply lacks the needed fix there.

charts.chart_plain_title: Trade-off between tasks helped and tasks harmed by edits
charts.chart_read: Each group is an acceptance rule; the taller bar counts tasks improved and the shorter bar counts tasks made worse, out of 120. More improved and fewer harmed is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The strictest rule, accepting an edit only when it passes every example test, roughly halves the harmed tasks while keeping most of the improvements.

qwen35_4b_verified_macro_capacity_fit_re.verdict_tag: Memory crowding fixed but reasoning still ran away
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_capacity_fit_re.concept_primer: A language model does its step-by-step reasoning inside a fixed pool of working memory on the graphics chip, like a parking lot with a set number of spaces. Run too many long tasks at once and together they demand more room than exists.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_capacity_fit_re.plain_question: When many long reasoning tasks ran at the same time and stalled, was the graphics chip simply being asked to hold more working memory than it physically had room for?
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_capacity_fit_re.plain_answer: It depends. Cutting the number of tasks running at once from 64 down to 19 did pull the peak memory demand safely under what the chip holds. But the outputs still ran away in repeating loops and hit their length caps, so none could be scored.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_capacity_fit_re.why_it_matters: When long reasoning tasks stall on a graphics chip, easing memory crowding is necessary but not sufficient. You must also cap how many run at once and confirm the outputs actually finish before trusting any results.

key_numbers.label: Working memory a batch demanded at peak
key_numbers.sub: pulled below what the chip holds by running fewer long tasks at once

key_numbers.label: What the chip's memory pool holds
key_numbers.sub: the fixed working-memory ceiling every batch had to fit under

key_numbers.label: Outputs that ended cleanly
key_numbers.sub: every sample looped or hit its length cap, so none could be scored

charts.chart_plain_title: Running fewer tasks at once pulled memory demand under the limit
charts.chart_read: Two settings sit side by side: many tasks running at once versus few. For each, one bar shows the peak working memory demanded and another shows what the chip physically holds. Demand sitting below capacity is the safe outcome.
charts.chart_takeaway: With many tasks at once, demand towers far above the memory the chip holds; cutting to nineteen tasks drops it safely under, with room left over.

charts.chart_plain_title: Even with memory fixed, outputs kept looping instead of finishing
charts.chart_read: Three problem types run along the bottom: reasoning that never resolved, outputs stuck repeating themselves, and outputs that hit the length cap. Each shows the observed share beside the allowed maximum. Staying below the allowed level means passing.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every observed bar overshoots its allowed limit, most starkly the repeating-loop share at roughly three times the line, so the whole run was rejected as unusable.

qwen35_4b_verified_macro_exact_cudagraph.verdict_tag: Extra thinking room did not stop looping
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_exact_cudagraph.concept_primer: Before answering, the model writes out its reasoning like scratch work, with a fixed limit on how long that can run. If it never decides it is finished, it gets cut off mid-thought.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_exact_cudagraph.plain_question: When a model writes out its reasoning before answering, does giving it more room to think help it wrap up on its own instead of running on forever?
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_exact_cudagraph.plain_answer: No. Handing the model more room to write out its reasoning did not help it finish. In every one of 48 attempts it ran until it was force-stopped, mostly stuck repeating the same phrases. More room made the looping slightly worse, not better.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_exact_cudagraph.why_it_matters: If a model keeps looping instead of wrapping up, simply giving it more space to think will not fix it. Treat runaway repetition as its own problem to solve before spending effort on longer reasoning.

key_numbers.label: Attempts that finished on their own
key_numbers.sub: every one had to be force-stopped mid-thought

key_numbers.label: Attempts stuck repeating themselves
key_numbers.sub: more room to think, slightly more looping

key_numbers.label: Largest batch the speed shortcut covered
key_numbers.sub: after the fix it spans every parallel answer

charts.chart_plain_title: Speed shortcut now covers the full batch of parallel answers
charts.chart_read: Four setups along the bottom pair two thinking-room sizes, each shown before and after the fix. For each, one bar is how many answers run at once; the other is how many the pre-built speed shortcut covers. Equal is best.
charts.chart_takeaway: In both after-the-fix setups the two bars reach the same height; before the fix the coverage bar stops short, so the shortcut had skipped some parallel answers.

charts.chart_plain_title: More thinking room brought slightly more runaway loops
charts.chart_read: Two setups side by side, less room versus more room to think. For each, three bars show the share of the 48 attempts that got stuck repeating themselves, hit the limit unresolved, or ran out of answer space. Lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The tall repeating-loop bar edges upward from the smaller to the larger thinking-room setting, so extra room fed more repetition rather than cleaner finishes.

qwen35_4b_verified_macro_invention.verdict_tag: AI used shortcuts fluently but not faithfully
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_invention.concept_primer: Picture giving someone a set of named shortcuts, where each single word stands for a fixed, exact sequence of steps. Instead of spelling out every step, they can just name the shortcut and mean the whole sequence.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_invention.plain_question: Can a small AI take a set of pre-built, verified shortcuts and use them to rewrite hard step-by-step tasks correctly and more simply?
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_invention.plain_answer: No answer either way. Before testing whether shortcuts make hard tasks easier, the team checked something simpler: given the shortcuts and the exact plan, could the AI rewrite a task faithfully? It used shortcuts smoothly but reproduced the exact steps in at most two of four tasks, short of the bar, so the study stopped.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_invention.why_it_matters: If you plan to have a small model rewrite problems using pre-built shortcuts, test faithful substitution first. Fluent, well-formatted shortcut use can hide wrong meaning, so confirm each shortcut expands to the exact intended steps before trusting it.

key_numbers.label: Tasks rewritten exactly right
key_numbers.sub: 3 of 4 were required to keep going

key_numbers.label: Outputs in valid format, thinking turned off
key_numbers.sub: up from 4 of 16 when the AI thought out loud

key_numbers.label: Answers cut off before finishing
key_numbers.sub: down from 12 of 16 with thinking on

charts.chart_plain_title: Neither setting let the AI rewrite enough tasks exactly right
charts.chart_read: Each pair of bars is one setting the AI ran under. Out of four tasks, one bar shows how many it rewrote perfectly and the other shows the three-of-four target it had to reach. More perfect rewrites is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: In both settings the exact-rewrite bar falls short of the target line, at most two of four, so the AI never cleared the bar to move forward.

charts.chart_plain_title: Turning off thinking fixed the format but not the meaning
charts.chart_read: Two settings side by side, out of sixteen attempts each. One bar counts cleanly formatted answers, where higher is better; the other counts answers cut off before finishing, where lower is better. The right group had the AI's think-out-loud step off.
charts.chart_takeaway: With thinking off, all sixteen answers came out clean and none were cut off, yet the steps were still wrong, so tidy formatting alone did not fix the task.

qwen35_4b_verified_macro_long_context_re.verdict_tag: More thinking room, still stuck in loops
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_long_context_re.concept_primer: When this AI solves a hard puzzle, it first writes out its reasoning on a scratchpad that has a fixed size limit. If it fills the whole scratchpad repeating itself without ever reaching an answer, that attempt is unusable.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_long_context_re.plain_question: If we give the AI a bigger scratchpad to work out fresh programming puzzles, will it stop getting stuck repeating itself and finish with a usable answer?
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_long_context_re.plain_answer: No. Across every scratchpad size, from small up to the largest the hardware could fit, nearly every fresh-puzzle attempt ran off the end without finishing, often stuck repeating itself. Bigger scratchpads changed how it failed but never made the work usable, so the planned shortcut-library test could not run.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_long_context_re.why_it_matters: Before comparing methods, confirm the model can actually finish on the exact kind of task you will score. A setup that works on guided, step-by-step examples can still stall on open-ended ones, wasting an entire run.

key_numbers.label: Fresh attempts with no usable answer, smallest room
key_numbers.sub: ran out of space still working, never finishing

key_numbers.label: Fresh attempts that looped instead of finishing, largest room
key_numbers.sub: stuck repeating the same reasoning over and over

key_numbers.label: Guided examples finished cleanly
key_numbers.sub: when the steps were supplied, every one produced a correct shortcut

charts.chart_plain_title: Bigger scratchpads changed how it failed, never made the work usable
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars across three scratchpad sizes, small to large. Bar height shows the share of fresh-puzzle attempts that ended badly each way: left unfinished, stuck repeating, or the answer cut off. Lower bars are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: As the scratchpad grows, the 'unfinished' bar shrinks but the 'stuck repeating' bar grows to replace it. The failure just moves around, never disappearing.

charts.chart_plain_title: With the steps supplied, it solved every guided example
charts.chart_read: One test group with two bars: how many of the sixteen guided examples the model solved with a correct shortcut, and the lower count it needed to pass. Reaching or beating that pass line is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The solved bar reaches all sixteen, clearing the pass line of twelve. When the reasoning steps were handed to it, the model finished every guided example cleanly.

qwen35_4b_verifier_conditioned_recovery_.verdict_tag: Recovery-policy study is frozen; model result pending
qwen35_4b_verifier_conditioned_recovery_.concept_primer: A coding assistant may know the right fix yet get stuck after a patch is rejected or a test fails. This study teaches what to do specifically at those moments, while keeping ordinary inspect, test, and submit behavior intact.
qwen35_4b_verifier_conditioned_recovery_.plain_question: Can training on real patch rejections and failed tests make this 4B coding assistant recover more often on completely different kinds of repositories?
qwen35_4b_verifier_conditioned_recovery_.plain_answer: We do not know yet. The study has frozen ten fresh repository families, seven recovery steps, three matched training versions, and strict comparisons before the full model run.
qwen35_4b_verifier_conditioned_recovery_.why_it_matters: A useful coding agent must revise its approach when tools reject an edit or tests expose an underfix. Any trained gain must beat both a simple reminder and spending the same generation budget on another try.

key_numbers.label: Fresh repository families
key_numbers.sub: six for training, four different families for transfer

key_numbers.label: Condition-specific recovery steps
key_numbers.sub: from first inspection through test pass and submit

key_numbers.label: Matched training versions
key_numbers.sub: ordinary states, recovery actions, and recovery actions plus brief planning

charts.chart_plain_title: Every planned recovery step is covered in the smoke check
charts.chart_read: Each bar counts the checked examples for one step in the coding loop, from first inspection to submit after tests pass. Equal height is the intended result before training begins.
charts.chart_takeaway: All seven bars contain ten checked examples, so no rare recovery or completion step disappears from the training design before the model run.

qwen35_4b_verifier_free_banking.verdict_tag: Self-confidence cannot replace running the code
qwen35_4b_verifier_free_banking.concept_primer: A model can get better by generating many answers to practice problems, keeping the good ones, and studying them like flashcards. The catch: something has to decide which answers are actually good enough to keep.
qwen35_4b_verifier_free_banking.plain_question: When a model learns from its own best answers, can it just trust its own confidence about which are right, instead of actually running the code to check?
qwen35_4b_verifier_free_banking.plain_answer: No. Only answers checked by actually running the code improved the model. Answers the model felt confident about taught nothing beyond picking answers at random, even though the confident batch was about fifteen times more likely to be correct than a random one.
qwen35_4b_verifier_free_banking.why_it_matters: When building a self-improvement loop for tasks where correctness must be computed, keep an automatic checker that runs the code. A model's confidence, however well it ranks answers, is not clean enough to train on directly.

key_numbers.label: Accuracy after training on code-checked answers
key_numbers.sub: a real improvement over the untrained model

key_numbers.label: Accuracy after training on confidence-picked answers
key_numbers.sub: no better than picking answers at random

key_numbers.label: How often the confident picks were actually correct
key_numbers.sub: far cleaner than random, yet it still didn't help

charts.chart_plain_title: Only answers verified by running the code improved the model
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one trained model's single-attempt accuracy on a set of held-back puzzles. From left: no training, then three models trained on the same number of kept answers, each chosen by a different rule. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the code-verified bar rises above the untrained baseline; the confidence-picked bar sits level with the random bar, so confidence-picking added nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: The model can only tell right from wrong if it thinks before scoring
charts.chart_read: Bars show how reliably the model separates its own correct answers from its wrong ones. A coin-flip is the floor; higher is better. Left pair: the model judges instantly. Right pair: it judges after thinking. Each pair pairs a strict measure with a lenient one.
charts.chart_takeaway: The instant judge's strict bar sits at coin-flip level; only after thinking does it climb, so judging correctness here itself requires step-by-step reasoning.

charts.chart_plain_title: After self-training the judge still ranks answers but grows overconfident
charts.chart_read: Three models: untrained, trained on verified answers, and trained on its own approved answers. One set of bars shows how well it still separates right from wrong (higher is better); the other shows how confident it wrongly feels about incorrect answers (lower is better).
charts.chart_takeaway: The ranking bars stay flat and high across all three models, so self-training does not break the judge's ordering, even when it trained on its own approvals.

charts.chart_plain_title: A side effect: training narrows the model's quick-answer variety
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a trained model's share of easy puzzles it can solve within a handful of quick, no-thinking attempts. Higher is better. From left: the untrained model, then four models trained on differently filtered answer sets.
charts.chart_takeaway: Most trained bars drop sharply below the untrained one, as quick-mode variety collapsed onto the practiced answer family, except the one arm trained on very few such answers.

qwen35_4b_verifier_guided_self_improveme.verdict_tag: Self-teaching did not improve new-problem coding
qwen35_4b_verifier_guided_self_improveme.concept_primer: Think of a student who tries to get smarter by re-studying only the practice problems they already solved correctly, with a checker confirming each solution actually runs.
qwen35_4b_verifier_guided_self_improveme.plain_question: Can this coding model get better at unseen problems by practicing on its own verified-correct solutions?
qwen35_4b_verifier_guided_self_improveme.plain_answer: No. Re-studying its own correct solutions did not help the model on fresh problems; it landed at 65%, essentially unchanged, while simply having it try more times reached 69%.
qwen35_4b_verifier_guided_self_improveme.why_it_matters: Do not spend effort retraining this model on its own successes to widen its coding reach. Simply letting it make more attempts and picking a working one is the more reliable lever.

key_numbers.label: New problems solved, after self-teaching
key_numbers.sub: No real change versus the original model

key_numbers.label: New problems solved, just trying more times
key_numbers.sub: The approach that actually helped

key_numbers.label: Practiced problems solved
key_numbers.sub: The only place self-teaching nudged up, and it did not carry over

charts.chart_plain_title: New-problem success: self-teaching arms versus simply trying more times
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of unseen problems the model eventually solves, one bar per approach; taller is better. The rightmost bar is the plain 'try more times' approach, the others are self-teaching variants.
charts.chart_takeaway: The 'try more times' bar is the tallest, standing above every self-teaching bar, so extra attempts beat teaching the model on its own work.

charts.chart_plain_title: Self-teaching helps on practiced problems but not on new ones
charts.chart_read: Three pairs of bars compare the original model to the self-taught one across practiced problems, new problems, and a different problem set; taller is better. Left pair is practice, the other two are unseen.
charts.chart_takeaway: The self-taught bar edges higher only on practiced problems and sits slightly lower on both unseen sets, so the gain does not carry over.

charts.chart_plain_title: Self-teaching leaves the model offering fewer distinct attempts
charts.chart_read: Bars show the average number of distinct answers the model produces per new problem, one per approach; more attempts give more chances to land a working one. Rightmost is the 'try more times' approach.
charts.chart_takeaway: Self-taught bars are shorter than the original model's, meaning self-teaching narrows the variety of attempts it makes.

charts.chart_plain_title: Whichever answer-picking rule is used, the original model still wins
charts.chart_read: Four pairs of bars compare final answer accuracy on new problems for the original versus self-taught model, one pair per answer-picking rule; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: In every pair the original-model bar is at least as tall, so switching the picking rule cannot rescue the self-taught model.

charts.chart_plain_title: A tiny 20-problem preview looked promising but reversed at full scale
charts.chart_read: Two pairs of bars compare the original and self-taught model on a small 20-problem preview versus the full 150-problem test; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The self-taught bar leads on the small preview but falls slightly behind on the full test, showing the early promise was noise.

qwen35_4b_verifier_selector_showdown.verdict_tag: Free quick self-check matches costly deliberation
qwen35_4b_verifier_selector_showdown.concept_primer: Imagine solving one coding problem eight different ways, but you can only turn in a single answer. A picking rule is any quick method for deciding which of those eight drafts you submit.
qwen35_4b_verifier_selector_showdown.plain_question: When you have several draft solutions to one coding problem and can submit only one, is it worth paying extra for the model to carefully double-check each draft first?
qwen35_4b_verifier_selector_showdown.plain_answer: No. Running one quick example test to weed out drafts, then having the model give each survivor a fast gut-check, submitted a fully correct answer 87% of the time. That matched a five-times-costlier careful review and nearly reached the best-possible 89%.
qwen35_4b_verifier_selector_showdown.why_it_matters: When you can run even a single example test against candidate answers, use it to filter first, then let the model add a free instant self-rating to break ties. Skip the slow, expensive deliberate review — it buys almost nothing extra.

key_numbers.label: Cost of the careful deliberate review

key_numbers.label: Achievable vs perfect picker
key_numbers.sub: the cheap deployable method versus the unreachable perfect picker that always chooses a correct draft

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each picking rule submits a fully correct answer
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one rule for choosing which of eight drafts to submit; height is the share of picks that fully passed every test. Bars run from a random pick up to the unreachable perfect picker; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The free-combo and expensive-combo bars stand at identical height, showing the pricey deliberate review adds nothing once a quick sample test filters the drafts.

charts.chart_plain_title: What each picking rule costs to run, in tokens per problem
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the estimated work per problem for one picking rule; taller means more expensive. The two deliberate-review rules on the right tower over the cheaper options; here shorter is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two careful-review bars rise roughly five times higher than the free-combo bar, yet buy no extra accuracy — pure cost with no payoff.

charts.chart_plain_title: How well each self-check spots drafts that secretly fail
charts.chart_read: Among drafts that passed the sample test, some are secretly wrong. Each bar shows how well that self-check sorts truly-correct from secretly-wrong, where half-height is coin-flip guessing and full height is perfect; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both bars sit well above coin-flip level and close together — the free quick self-check sorts the survivors almost as well as the costly deliberate one.

qwen35_4b_wall_climbing.verdict_tag: Practicing shallow chains doesn't unlock deeper ones
qwen35_4b_wall_climbing.concept_primer: Each task is solved by chaining small steps: an easy one needs two steps, a harder one needs three. The question is whether getting fluent at two-step tasks carries over to three-step ones.
qwen35_4b_wall_climbing.plain_question: If we drill the model on its own correct two-step solutions, does it start solving three-step tasks it never could before?
qwen35_4b_wall_climbing.plain_answer: No. Drilling the model on its own two-step solutions tripled its two-step success rate, from 12% to 36%, but produced zero three-step solutions — it never learned to reach one step deeper.
qwen35_4b_wall_climbing.why_it_matters: To extend a model to harder tasks, first find deeper solutions with a search tool, then drill on those. Self-practice only sharpens skills the model can already do, never adds new depth.

key_numbers.label: Two-step tasks solved
key_numbers.sub: tripled after self-practice

key_numbers.label: Three-step tasks solved
key_numbers.sub: no change, none unlocked

key_numbers.label: Four-step tasks solved
key_numbers.sub: unchanged, no leap

charts.chart_plain_title: Self-practice tripled two-step success but unlocked no three-step solutions
charts.chart_read: Three task groups: two-, three-, and four-step problems. For each, one bar is the original model and one is the self-practiced model; taller means more new tasks solved. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The self-practiced bar towers over the original only for two-step tasks; the three- and four-step bars stay flat at zero and near-zero, so practice added reach only where the model already had it.

charts.chart_plain_title: More attempts help two-step tasks; three-step stays stuck at zero
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is how many attempts the model gets; vertical is the fraction of new tasks solved, higher is better. The rising line is the self-practiced model on two-step tasks; the two flat bottom lines are three-step tasks for both models.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two-step line climbs steadily with more attempts while both three-step lines lie flat on zero, showing extra tries never surface a single three-step solution.

qwen_action_conditioned_vm_echo_policy_i.verdict_tag: Learning from its own drafts barely helped
qwen_action_conditioned_vm_echo_policy_i.concept_primer: The model writes tiny programs to solve puzzles, and it can actually run each draft to see what it computes. The idea: let it learn from running its own drafts to pick and reuse the good ones.
qwen_action_conditioned_vm_echo_policy_i.plain_question: Can the model get better at writing correct little programs by running its own drafts and learning which ones actually work?
qwen_action_conditioned_vm_echo_policy_i.plain_answer: Partly. Learning to judge its own drafts barely beat the model's gut instinct for picking one, but reusing the best drafts still nudged its direct writing up from 18% to 23% of puzzles solved — far below the 82% it reaches when simply shown correct answers.
qwen_action_conditioned_vm_echo_policy_i.why_it_matters: Running your own drafts to reuse the good ones gives a small, real lift, but the payoff is capped by how well the model can tell a right draft from a wrong one. Improve that judgment first.

key_numbers.label: Fresh puzzles solved by writing directly
key_numbers.sub: before vs after reusing its own best drafts

key_numbers.label: Picking the right draft
key_numbers.sub: learned judgment ~ gut instinct, vs 37% if it always picked the best draft

key_numbers.label: When shown correct answers instead
key_numbers.sub: the ceiling this approach stayed well under

key_numbers.label: Hard puzzles solved when allowed to search drafts
key_numbers.sub: biggest gain from reusing its own drafts

charts.chart_plain_title: Solving puzzles by writing directly, across training methods
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is a group of puzzles; taller bars mean more solved by writing a program directly, with no second tries. Bars compare the frozen starting model, two ways of reusing its own drafts, and a model simply shown correct answers.
charts.chart_takeaway: The reuse bars sit just above the starting model, while the shown-answers bar towers over all of them, so self-teaching helps only slightly.

charts.chart_plain_title: Solving puzzles when allowed to try several drafts
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is a group of puzzles; taller bars mean more solved when the model is allowed to generate and test several drafts. Bars compare the starting model against two ways of reusing its own drafts.
charts.chart_takeaway: Reusing the best drafts gives the clearest lift here, with hard puzzles rising from 35% to 52% solved.

charts.chart_plain_title: How well the model picks its best draft
charts.chart_read: Taller bars mean the chosen draft is more often correct, for practice puzzles and fresh ones. Bars compare gut instinct, learned judgment, and the score if it always grabbed its best available draft.
charts.chart_takeaway: Learned judgment barely rises above gut instinct while the best-possible bar sits far higher, showing weak judgment is the bottleneck.

charts.chart_plain_title: Being pickier about which drafts to reuse
charts.chart_read: Two puzzle groups; taller bars mean more solved by direct writing afterward. Bars compare reusing every draft versus keeping only higher-scoring drafts.
charts.chart_takeaway: Keeping only the pickier drafts raises fresh-puzzle accuracy, showing quality of reused drafts matters more than quantity.

charts.chart_plain_title: Direct puzzle-solving per group, self-teaching vs shown answers
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is a puzzle group; taller bars mean more solved by writing directly. Bars compare the starting model, two self-teaching methods, and a model shown correct answers.
charts.chart_takeaway: Self-teaching bars edge only a few points above the start, dwarfed by the shown-answers bars reaching 52-82%.

qwen_active_crystallizer_public_gate.verdict_tag: Model labels did not build reliable rules
qwen_active_crystallizer_public_gate.concept_primer: Imagine watching someone reformat a few names by hand, then trying to write one recipe that reformats every future name automatically, instead of redoing each one by hand.
qwen_active_crystallizer_public_gate.plain_question: Can the model's yes/no judgments turn a handful of worked examples into one reusable recipe that handles every new case?
qwen_active_crystallizer_public_gate.plain_answer: No. Using the model's judgments to pick a recipe worked on 25% of tasks, barely ahead of the 22.5% you get from deliberately scrambled judgments, and it never rose above the best recipe the tool could build.
qwen_active_crystallizer_public_gate.why_it_matters: The model can reformat individual cases well, but that skill does not compile into one dependable rule. If you need a single recipe that always works, expect to hand-build better recipe options first.

key_numbers.label: Recipe picked using the model's judgments
key_numbers.sub: share of tasks where one recipe handled every new case

key_numbers.label: Recipe picked using scrambled judgments
key_numbers.sub: the same setup with judgments deliberately jumbled — nearly a tie

key_numbers.label: Model answering cases one at a time
key_numbers.sub: same model, but solving each case by hand instead of via a recipe

key_numbers.label: Recipe that worked on every case in a strict check
key_numbers.sub: on a matched set of 40 tasks, versus 37.5% for hand-solving

charts.chart_plain_title: Recipe-picking methods versus solving cases one at a time
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one method; height is the share of tasks solved on unseen cases, taller is better. Bars cover the model's judgments, scrambled judgments, plain shortest-recipe picking, the best recipe available, and hand-solving one case each.
charts.chart_takeaway: The model-judgment bar sits level with the scrambled-judgment bar and the best-available ceiling, all far below the tall hand-solving bar.

charts.chart_plain_title: Getting single cases right versus getting a whole task right
charts.chart_read: Three bars on the same 40 tasks; height is exact-match rate, taller is better. Left: model solving individual cases; middle: model getting every case in a task right; right: one recipe getting every case right.
charts.chart_takeaway: The tall single-case bar collapses when every case must be right, and the recipe bar collapses furthest, showing consistency is the weak point.

charts.chart_plain_title: Where recipe-building breaks down across all tasks
charts.chart_read: Three bars, height is share of tasks, taller is better. Left: tasks where some recipe fits the shown examples; middle: tasks where a fitting recipe also handles unseen cases; right: picking the shortest fitting recipe.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every task has a recipe fitting the examples, yet only about a third of those recipes generalize, so fitting examples is easy but generalizing is not.

charts.chart_plain_title: Best-possible recipe coverage by kind of task
charts.chart_read: Task kinds along the bottom; paired bars show the best recipe available versus the plain shortest-recipe pick; taller is better. The two largest kinds, date-and-time and numbers, appear first.
charts.chart_takeaway: The date-and-time and number bars are lowest despite being the biggest groups, dragging overall recipe coverage down well below simpler task kinds.

charts.chart_plain_title: Recipe-picking results hold steady across three run sizes
charts.chart_read: Three run sizes along the bottom; grouped bars show each method's share of tasks solved on unseen cases, taller is better. Bars include the model's judgments, scrambled judgments, and hand-solving cases one at a time.
charts.chart_takeaway: Across all three sizes the model-judgment bars stay pinned near the scrambled-judgment and best-available bars, while hand-solving stays far higher.

charts.chart_plain_title: Best-possible recipe coverage shifts with the task sample
charts.chart_read: Three sample sizes along the bottom; paired bars show the best recipe available versus the plain shortest-recipe pick; taller is better. Only the largest sample gives a stable reading.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best-available bar swings widely between small samples, so only the full-size run gives a trustworthy estimate of how far recipes can reach.

qwen_active_example_acquisition.verdict_tag: Smartly chosen extra examples help a little
qwen_active_example_acquisition.concept_primer: Before answering hidden test questions, the model is allowed to request a few extra worked examples. This tests whether deliberately choosing which examples to add beats grabbing random ones.
qwen_active_example_acquisition.plain_question: If the model can request a few extra worked examples before a test, does choosing them deliberately beat picking them at random?
qwen_active_example_acquisition.plain_answer: It depends. Deliberately choosing gave a small lift, solving 70% of tasks versus 67% with no extras, while random or answer-scrambled examples actually did worse, at 63% and 60%.
qwen_active_example_acquisition.why_it_matters: When you add examples to steer a small model, choose them deliberately rather than at random. Targeting the confusing cases helps modestly, but random extras can backfire, especially on formatting-heavy tasks.

key_numbers.label: Tasks fully solved with no extra examples
key_numbers.sub: starting point with the four given examples

key_numbers.label: Tasks solved after a deliberately chosen extra
key_numbers.sub: the small lift from choosing well

key_numbers.label: Tasks solved with random extra examples
key_numbers.sub: picking badly does worse than adding nothing

key_numbers.label: Best result from any pick that was tried
key_numbers.sub: the ceiling if every choice were ideal

charts.chart_plain_title: How each way of picking extra examples solves tasks
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of tasks fully solved on unseen test rows, one bar per way of choosing extra examples; taller is better. Leftmost adds nothing, rightmost is the best pick that was tried.
charts.chart_takeaway: The deliberately chosen bars sit above the no-extras baseline while random and label-scrambled bars fall below it, so how you pick matters more than how many you add.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy as more extra examples are added, by method
charts.chart_read: Each line is a strategy for choosing extra examples; the horizontal axis is how many were added (none, one, or three) and the vertical axis is the share of tasks fully solved; up is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the deliberate-choice line stays above its starting point at both amounts, while the random and diversity lines dip below, showing extra examples help only when chosen well.

charts.chart_plain_title: Date and time tasks: the hardest, largest group
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of the 13 date-and-time tasks fully solved, one bar per method, from no extras through deliberately chosen, random, and scrambled-label extras; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: One deliberately chosen example lifts this hard group the most, while random or scrambled extras push it to the lowest bar.

charts.chart_plain_title: Getting every row right versus getting single rows right
charts.chart_read: Two bars per method: the lighter bar is the share of individual test rows answered correctly, the darker bar is the share of tasks where every row is right; the darker one is stricter and higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The whole-task bars always trail the single-row bars, meaning one wrong row sinks a task; deliberate choosing narrows that gap the most.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which task types still have room to improve
charts.chart_read: Two bars per task type: starting accuracy with the four given examples versus the best pick of extras that was tried; each bar is the share of tasks fully solved, higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Six of nine task types are already maxed out, so extra examples can only move the date-time and number groups, and one type stays stuck at zero.

charts.chart_plain_title: Number tasks: smart picks help, scrambled labels hurt
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of the six number-formatting tasks fully solved, one bar per method, from no extras through deliberate, diverse, scrambled-label, and best-pick extras; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Deliberate and diversity picks reach the best-achieved level, while examples with scrambled answers drag accuracy below the no-extras start.

charts.chart_plain_title: Do different self-check styles agree on which example to add?
charts.chart_read: Three bars per hard task, one for each style of self-check the model uses to decide which example to request; each bar is the share of that task's rows solved, higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The three self-check styles mostly agree, but on the hardest tasks one style swings the result by a noticeable chunk in either direction.

qwen_batched_transduction_consistency.verdict_tag: Answering all at once did not help
qwen_batched_transduction_consistency.concept_primer: Think of a clerk given a few worked examples, then a stack of similar entries to reformat. We tested whether letting it see the whole stack at once, versus one entry at a time, makes it more consistent.
qwen_batched_transduction_consistency.plain_question: Does letting the model reformat all of a task's entries together, instead of one at a time, make it get the whole task right more often?
qwen_batched_transduction_consistency.plain_answer: No. Solving one entry at a time got every entry in a task right 50% of the time; doing them all together dropped to 45%. Grouping never rescued a task it had failed, and it broke two that one-at-a-time had solved.
qwen_batched_transduction_consistency.why_it_matters: When you need every row of a task correct, keep reformatting entries one at a time. Bundling them into one shared answer adds a new failure mode where a single wrong item spoils the whole task.

key_numbers.label: Whole task fully correct, one at a time
key_numbers.sub: baseline of tasks with every entry right

key_numbers.label: Whole task fully correct, all together
key_numbers.sub: best grouped approach still trails

key_numbers.label: Tasks the grouping rescued
key_numbers.sub: never fixed a task it had failed

key_numbers.label: Tasks the grouping broke
key_numbers.sub: lost tasks the one-at-a-time solved

charts.chart_plain_title: Doing entries together versus one at a time, six ways
charts.chart_read: Bars group six methods along the bottom, from one-at-a-time to ever-larger bundles; taller is better. Darker bars show how often the whole task was fully correct, lighter bars how often an individual entry was correct.
charts.chart_takeaway: The leftmost one-at-a-time bar is the tallest on whole-task correctness, so no bundling method beats simply handling entries separately.

charts.chart_plain_title: Stricter grouping instructions still fall short
charts.chart_read: Four methods along the bottom, taller is better: one-at-a-time versus three ways of grouping entries with tougher instructions. Darker bars are whole-task correctness, lighter bars single-entry correctness.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every grouped bar sits at or below the one-at-a-time bar, so tighter instructions never close the gap.

charts.chart_plain_title: Task-by-task: did grouping help, tie, or hurt?
charts.chart_read: Three bars count how many of the 40 tasks grouping improved, matched, or worsened versus one-at-a-time. Taller wins bar would be good; taller loses bar is bad.
charts.chart_takeaway: The wins bar is empty and the loses bar shows two tasks, so grouping only ever hurt or made no difference.

qwen_budgeted_action_value_compiler.verdict_tag: Right answers generated but rarely chosen
qwen_budgeted_action_value_compiler.concept_primer: Picture the model as a student who scribbles many candidate answers on scratch paper: the correct one is usually there, but they must circle just one, with no answer key to check against.
qwen_budgeted_action_value_compiler.plain_question: Can a learned scoring model pick the right little program from the model's own guesses without ever peeking at the answer?
qwen_budgeted_action_value_compiler.plain_answer: Mostly no. A learned scorer lifted accuracy only a couple of points over plain search, from 67% to 70% on new tasks, while a version allowed to peek at the answer key reached 82%. The right program is usually generated but rarely chosen.
qwen_budgeted_action_value_compiler.why_it_matters: The bottleneck is picking, not generating. Effort should go into training a stronger judge on complete candidate programs, checked by actually running them, rather than scoring half-finished ones.

key_numbers.label: Learned picker vs plain search
key_numbers.sub: correct answers on new everyday tasks; only a tiny lift

key_numbers.label: If it could peek at the answer key
key_numbers.sub: same tasks when candidates are checked against the answer

key_numbers.label: Correct answer already on the scratch paper
key_numbers.sub: share of tasks where a right program was generated

key_numbers.label: Hardest multi-step tasks
key_numbers.sub: learned picker gives essentially no gain here

charts.chart_plain_title: Learned picker vs peeking at the answer, on two task sets
charts.chart_read: Bars show task accuracy (taller is better) for four ways of choosing a final program, grouped into everyday paired tasks and harder multi-step tasks; the rightmost bar is allowed to check candidates against the answer key.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned-picker bar sits just above plain search, but the answer-checking bar towers far above both, so the learned scorer captures little of the available gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: The correct program is usually generated but not picked
charts.chart_read: For four task sets, the short bar is how often plain search's single top pick is right and the tall bar is how often any generated candidate was right; taller is better, and the gap between them is missed opportunity.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every tall bar sits far above its short partner, meaning a correct program was generated 15-20 points more often than it was actually chosen.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much to trust the learned score before it backfires
charts.chart_read: Lines track accuracy (higher is better) as the learned score is given more weight when choosing the final program; the flat left region is light weight, the right edge is heavy weight.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines dip at the far right, showing that leaning too hard on the learned score makes choices worse, not better.

charts.chart_plain_title: How cleanly each kind of move-label can be told apart
charts.chart_read: Bars show how reliably a learned score separates good partial moves from bad ones (taller is better) for four training signals, from a simple exact-match label to richer graded scores.
charts.chart_takeaway: The simple exact-match bar is tall and clean while the richer graded-score bars are much shorter, so nuance is exactly what the lightweight scorer struggles to learn.

charts.chart_plain_title: More training examples let the compiler actually learn
charts.chart_read: Lines show how accurately the model turns prompts into runnable programs (higher is better) as training progresses, comparing a small-data pilot against the main run that used four times the examples.
charts.chart_takeaway: The pilot line stays flat near the floor while the main-run line climbs steadily to about 72%, showing data volume, not tuning, unlocked the compiler.

charts.chart_plain_title: Exactly rebuilding the intended program stays low everywhere
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often each method reproduces the exact intended program (taller is better) across four task sets, comparing plain search, the best learned picker, and the answer-checking version.
charts.chart_takeaway: All bars stay well below half and collapse to about a third on the hardest set, so exact program recovery is hard even with answer checking.

charts.chart_plain_title: Most moves aren't the textbook move, yet still reach the answer
