charts.chart_plain_title: Task success by method: the gated recipe wins
charts.chart_read: Each group is one way of solving the tasks; taller bars are better. One bar shows whole tasks solved perfectly, the other the share of individual rows correct. The gated recipe stands tallest; the scrambled-example control sits near the floor.
charts.chart_takeaway: The gated recipe is the tallest bar for whole-task success, edging out plain answering, while raw recipes and the scrambled control fall far below both.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tasks each method rescued versus tasks it broke
charts.chart_read: For each method, one bar counts tasks it newly solved and another counts tasks it broke that plain answering had solved, out of 25. More rescued and fewer broken is better. The gate keeps its two rescues while breaking none.
charts.chart_takeaway: Raw recipes break six or seven tasks to win two, but the gated version keeps both wins with a zero-height broken bar.

charts.chart_plain_title: Whole-task success by task type: where recipes help or break
charts.chart_read: Each group is a task type (dates, numbers, names, and so on); bars compare plain answering, raw recipes, and the gated recipe. Taller is better. Raw recipes climb on Number tasks but collapse to zero on dates, file paths, and billing codes.
charts.chart_takeaway: Raw recipes genuinely lift Number tasks yet flatten to zero on several types, while the gated bar matches whichever approach is best in every group.

charts.chart_plain_title: Getting rows right versus getting whole tasks perfect
charts.chart_read: Each method has two bars: the share of individual rows correct and the share of whole tasks correct with no mistakes anywhere. Taller is better. Plain answering scores high on rows but drops sharply once every row in a task must be right.
charts.chart_takeaway: Plain answering loses a big chunk moving from rows to perfect whole tasks, while recipe methods stay low on both because they tend to miss an entire task at once.

charts.chart_plain_title: Fitting the shown examples does not mean solving new rows
charts.chart_read: For each recipe method, one bar shows how often a recipe matched the few shown examples and the other how often it solved the whole task on new rows. A recipe that truly works would have both bars high. The scrambled control fits examples yet solves almost nothing.
charts.chart_takeaway: Raw recipes match the shown examples about half the time but solve fewer new tasks, and the scrambled control fits examples while solving almost nothing, exposing false matches.

charts.chart_plain_title: Row accuracy by task type: where recipes collapse
charts.chart_read: Each task type has two bars comparing plain answering with raw recipes on the share of individual rows correct on new data. Taller is better. Recipes stay even on some types but crater on dates, billing codes, and file paths.
charts.chart_takeaway: Recipes track plain answering on currency, email, phone, and rating tasks but collapse toward zero on dates, billing codes, and file paths even row by row.

qwen_recursive_task_decomposition.verdict_tag: Locking in one recipe hurts accuracy
qwen_recursive_task_decomposition.concept_primer: Instead of solving each item fresh, you can study a few worked examples, write one reusable recipe, and apply that same recipe to every remaining item. This tests whether the recipe route wins.
qwen_recursive_task_decomposition.plain_question: Does writing one reusable recipe first and applying it to every item beat solving each item on its own?
qwen_recursive_task_decomposition.plain_answer: No. Committing to one reusable recipe did worse than solving items one at a time: individual-item accuracy fell from 69% to 59%, and a recipe borrowed from a different task fell further still.
qwen_recursive_task_decomposition.why_it_matters: Let the model reason case by case rather than forcing it to commit to a single rule up front. Locking in one recipe throws away useful item-by-item judgment, and checking the recipe against the practice examples cannot catch this.

key_numbers.label: Items right, solved one at a time
key_numbers.sub: answering each item fresh

key_numbers.label: Items right, one recipe applied to all
key_numbers.sub: commit to a recipe first, then apply it

key_numbers.label: Items right, borrowed recipe
key_numbers.sub: a recipe copied from a different task

key_numbers.label: Best-case tasks solved, step-by-step
key_numbers.sub: breaking into substeps lifts only the ceiling

charts.chart_plain_title: Writing one recipe first does not beat solving items one at a time
charts.chart_read: Three approaches along the bottom: solve each item fresh, commit to one recipe first, or borrow a recipe from another task. Taller bars are better; one bar counts getting every item in a task right, the other counts individual items right.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both bars are tallest for solving items fresh; committing to one recipe drops individual-item accuracy to 59%, and a borrowed recipe drops lower still.

charts.chart_plain_title: Breaking tasks into steps raises the ceiling, not what you can actually pick
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of tasks a fixed formula library solves perfectly, taller is better: a simple one-shot formula and a step-by-step version, each shown as its best-possible pick and the pick chosen from the examples, plus a scrambled-labels control.
charts.chart_takeaway: Step-by-step lifts the best-possible score from 29% to 34%, but the version you can actually choose from examples ties the simple one at 23%, and the scrambled control collapses to near zero.

charts.chart_plain_title: Trying three recipe styles still did not recover the lost accuracy
charts.chart_read: Individual-item accuracy for the same three approaches, comparing a first attempt using one recipe against an improved attempt that writes three recipe styles and keeps the one that best fits the examples. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Even after keeping the best-fitting recipe, the recipe approach stays below solving items one at a time, so more recipe tries did not help.

charts.chart_plain_title: Step-by-step only helps neatly separated task types
charts.chart_read: For each type of task, two bars show the best-possible perfect-task rate: a one-shot formula versus a step-by-step formula. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Step-by-step jumps to 100% on cleanly delimited types like logs and URLs, while dates and numbers barely move.

charts.chart_plain_title: Picking a recipe from a few examples loses most of the step-by-step gain
charts.chart_read: For the largest task types, two bars: the best step-by-step recipe available versus the one actually chosen from the few examples. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Example-picking loses the entire gain on addresses and cities and a large share on names and phones, keeping full coverage only on emails.

charts.chart_plain_title: Locking a recipe rescues two tasks but wrecks several the model already solved
charts.chart_read: For each task where locking a recipe changed things, two bars compare individual-item accuracy: solving fresh versus committing to one recipe. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Locking rescues two date tasks but collapses number tasks the model had solved outright, dropping some from 100% to 17% or even 0%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Matching the practice examples cannot tell which recipes will fail
charts.chart_read: For each task, two bars: how perfectly the recipe matched the practice examples versus how well it then worked on new items. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every recipe scored a perfect match on the practice examples yet many scored zero on new items, so fitting the examples cannot flag a recipe that will fail.

qwen_register_structured_runtime.verdict_tag: Solves short recipes, not long ones
qwen_register_structured_runtime.concept_primer: The model fills a set of invisible slots with a step-by-step recipe, like writing instructions on a form that a fixed calculator then follows exactly to reach an answer.
qwen_register_structured_runtime.plain_question: Can the model reliably write a long, multi-step calculation into hidden slots so a fixed calculator returns the right answer?
qwen_register_structured_runtime.plain_answer: It depends on length. Recipes up to 12 steps were solved perfectly, but 24-step recipes worked only about a quarter of the time, better than the 4% seen without the extra training trick, yet far from reliable.
qwen_register_structured_runtime.why_it_matters: Hidden-slot programs work for modest tasks, but long procedures need a way to check and repair each step, since a nearly-right recipe still fails when every step must be exact.

key_numbers.label: Recipes up to 12 steps
key_numbers.sub: every answer correct

key_numbers.label: 24-step recipes solved
key_numbers.sub: with the extra training trick vs without it

key_numbers.label: Single steps correct
key_numbers.sub: but one slip ruins the whole 24-step answer

charts.chart_plain_title: Long-recipe success: extra training trick vs plain version
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars, one group per prompt wording (standard, reworded, paired). In each group, one bar is training with the consistency trick, the other a matched version without it; bar height is the share of 24-step problems solved exactly, taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trick's bars are taller in every group, most on standard wording, but all stay low, so it helps long recipes without solving them.

charts.chart_plain_title: Success by recipe length in the main run
charts.chart_read: Lines track the share of problems solved exactly as recipe length grows across the horizontal axis, with a separate line for each prompt wording; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All lines sit at the top through 12 steps then plunge near the bottom at 24 steps, showing where the method breaks.

charts.chart_plain_title: Where 24-step recipes go wrong, piece by piece
charts.chart_read: Bars run left to right from the starting value through each recipe piece to the full assembled program; height is how often that piece is correct on 24-step problems, taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Early pieces are near-perfect but the full-program bar falls to a quarter, showing small per-step errors pile up over 24 steps.

qwen_register_token_latent_compiler.verdict_tag: Works for short chains, breaks on long ones
qwen_register_token_latent_compiler.concept_primer: Think of the model jotting a step-by-step math recipe onto a few hidden memory cards, which an unseen calculator then follows exactly to get the answer.
qwen_register_token_latent_compiler.plain_question: Can the model reliably write a correct step-by-step math recipe into hidden memory cards that a hidden calculator then runs?
qwen_register_token_latent_compiler.plain_answer: It depends. Short recipes work well: up to 12 steps run correctly about 89-95% of the time. But 24-step recipes collapse to near random-guess level, far below the shorter chains.
qwen_register_token_latent_compiler.why_it_matters: The hidden-recipe idea genuinely works and clearly beats just guessing the final answer. But long recipes need every step right, so future work should train directly against long-chain mistakes.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy stays high until chains get long
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many steps the recipe has (4 to 24); the vertical axis is the share of recipes the hidden calculator runs correctly, where higher is better. Two lines: standard wording and reworded prompts.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines sit near the top through 12 steps, then plunge at 24 steps, so the method holds for short recipes and breaks on long ones.

charts.chart_plain_title: Even where it struggles, the recipe method beats the shortcuts
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars for three prompt wordings at 24 steps; taller is better. Three approaches compared: the recipe method versus two shortcut methods that skip writing out steps.
charts.chart_takeaway: The recipe bars are tallest in all three groups, while both shortcut methods sit at random-guess level, showing writing the steps out is what helps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Each step is usually right, but all 24 must be
charts.chart_read: Bars for individual recipe parts (starting number, operation, amount) versus whole-recipe correctness; higher is better. Two groups: standard wording and reworded prompts.
charts.chart_takeaway: The individual-part bars near 90-97% tower over the whole-recipe bars near 20%, showing small per-step slips pile up and wreck the full answer.

charts.chart_plain_title: Skills appear late, and long chains lag behind
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is training progress; the vertical axis is the share of recipes run correctly, higher is better. Four lines, one per chain length (4, 8, 12, 24 steps).
charts.chart_takeaway: Lines hug the bottom for the first half of training, then short and medium chains jump up together while the 24-step line trails far behind.

charts.chart_plain_title: Only the final recipe cleared the long-chain hurdle
charts.chart_read: Bars for six versions tried in development order; the vertical axis is the share of 24-step recipes run correctly, where higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Five early versions sit near zero, and only the final version reaches about 22%, so the winning setup came only after several failed attempts.

charts.chart_plain_title: Two wordings agree until chains get long
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is chain length; the vertical axis is a rate, higher is better. One line tracks whether two wordings land on the same hidden steps, the other whether both are correct.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines stay near the top through 12 steps, then drop to near zero at 24 steps, so long recipes no longer agree across wordings.

qwen_register_trace_refiner.verdict_tag: Small edits help a little, rarely enough
qwen_register_trace_refiner.concept_primer: Think of a spell-checker for a step-by-step recipe the model writes: it tries a few small nearby edits and picks the version most likely to be right, without seeing the answer.
qwen_register_trace_refiner.plain_question: Can a learned picker that tries small edits to the model's recipe fix more of the wrong answers?
qwen_register_trace_refiner.plain_answer: It depends. On plainly-worded problems the editor nudged the right-answer rate from 23% to 27%, beating no-edits, but that is a fraction of the 37% a perfect picker could reach, and it helped zero on reworded problems.
qwen_register_trace_refiner.why_it_matters: Trying small local edits is a genuine but weak lever. The bigger obstacle is that the correct recipe usually is not among the nearby edits, so widen the search before trusting the picker.

charts.chart_plain_title: Right-answer rate: original recipe, edited recipe, best possible pick

charts.chart_takeaway: The edited-pick bar sits just above the original on plainly-worded problems but far below the best-possible bar, and gains nothing on reworded problems.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often a correct fix even exists among the tried edits

charts.chart_takeaway: Bars stay low everywhere and collapse to near-nothing on reworded problems, meaning the correct recipe is usually not even among the options.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much of the possible improvement the picker actually captured
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one kind of problem; height shows the share of the achievable improvement that the automatic picker actually captured, out of what a perfect picker could. Taller is better. Reworded problems are flat at zero.
charts.chart_takeaway: Even where a good fix existed, the picker captured only about a quarter of the possible gain on plain problems and none on reworded ones.

qwen_search_augmented_rollout_distillati.concept_primer: Picture the model as a machine operator that solves a task by making one small edit to a program, checking the result, then editing again. The test: does a smarter training routine make it edit better?
qwen_search_augmented_rollout_distillati.plain_question: Does letting the model practice and then retraining it on automatically-found fixes make its step-by-step editing solver more accurate?
qwen_search_augmented_rollout_distillati.plain_answer: No. The fix-retraining did not improve the step-by-step solver: it matched or trailed the simpler training on four of five test sets, and both stayed far below what a perfect edit sequence can reach.
qwen_search_augmented_rollout_distillati.why_it_matters: Collecting verified fixes is the easy part; turning them into better whole-solution choices is the real bottleneck. Future work should reward complete solution attempts rather than scoring single edits in isolation.

key_numbers.label: Fix-retraining on reworded tasks

charts.chart_plain_title: Solve rates by test set: fix-retraining versus simpler training
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is one test set. Within it, bars compare the plain model, the simply-trained editor, the fix-retrained editor, and an ideal solver. Taller bars mean more tasks solved, so higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The fix-retrained bars sit at or below the simply-trained bars on most test sets, while the ideal-solver bars tower far above every trained version.

charts.chart_plain_title: Does allowing more edit steps help the retrained solver?
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many edits the model is allowed; the vertical axis is the share of tasks solved. Each line is one test set, and higher lines mean more solved.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only one line climbs as edits increase; the rest stay flat or drop, so more editing does not reliably improve results.

charts.chart_plain_title: Per-step correctness across the training stages

charts.chart_takeaway: Choosing the right edit fell from the basic stage's peak to the fix-retraining stage, and stronger per-step scores never produced better full solutions.

qwen_semantic_prefix_value_model.verdict_tag: Reachability scoring fails to beat plain search
qwen_semantic_prefix_value_model.concept_primer: Imagine guiding a builder assembling a calculation step by step, but you are never shown the right answer. You must score each half-finished attempt using only a hunch about whether it is on track.
qwen_semantic_prefix_value_model.plain_question: Can scoring half-finished programs by whether they could still reach a correct answer guide the search better than just following the model's own confidence?
qwen_semantic_prefix_value_model.plain_answer: No. Scoring half-finished attempts by whether they could still reach a correct answer did no better than plain confidence-based search. Only a stricter scorer, which checks each step against the one known correct program, added a small edge.
qwen_semantic_prefix_value_model.why_it_matters: The bottleneck is picking the right candidate from those already generated, not generating it. A coarse could-this-still-work signal is too blunt; a scorer that rates how promising each path is looks like the better bet.

charts.chart_takeaway: Only the strict exact-match scorer nudges the everyday-task bar upward; the reachability scorer sits level with plain search, far below the answer-peeking bar.

charts.chart_plain_title: The correct program is usually available but not picked
charts.chart_read: For everyday and harder tasks, bars compare the answer chosen first, the best answer sitting in the candidate shortlist, and an answer-peeking pick. Taller is better; the gap between first pick and shortlist is wasted potential.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best-in-shortlist bar towers over the first-pick bar, showing the right program is usually generated but ranked below wrong ones.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each labeling rule marks a step as good
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of program steps each rule labels as good; higher means a broader, looser target. Exact matching is strict, raw reachability is loose, and filtered reachability sits between them.
charts.chart_takeaway: The raw-reachability bar is roughly seven times the exact bar, marking far more steps as good and giving the scorer a much looser target to learn.

charts.chart_plain_title: Both scorers learn their target well
charts.chart_read: Bars rate each scorer's ability to sort good steps from bad on fresh data, from about 0.5 (coin-flip) up to 1.0 (perfect); taller is better. Left is the exact-match scorer, right the reachability scorer.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both bars sit far above coin-flip, and the exact-match scorer is cleaner, yet even it turns into only a small real-task gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tightening the reachability labels across trial runs
charts.chart_read: Across four successive runs, two bars per run show the share of steps marked good by exact matching versus reachability. Lower reachability bars mean a tighter, less permissive target.
charts.chart_takeaway: The reachability bars shrink run over run while the exact bars stay low and flat, showing the target was progressively tightened.

qwen_shared_parser_compiler.verdict_tag: Teaching each step works but stays brittle
qwen_shared_parser_compiler.concept_primer: A large AI reads a math word problem and works out the answer silently, inside itself. Imagine attaching a small reader that peeks at its internal scratch notes and tries to reconstruct the exact steps it took, one by one.
qwen_shared_parser_compiler.plain_question: When a large AI quietly works through a multi-step math problem in its head, can a small reader peek at its internal notes and rebuild the exact steps it took?
qwen_shared_parser_compiler.plain_answer: It depends. Taught each step directly, the reader rebuilds short programs well — about 78% of four-step problems come out exactly right — but accuracy fades to 39% by twelve steps and collapses to random-guess level on longer or reworded problems. Reading only the final answer never beats chance.
qwen_shared_parser_compiler.why_it_matters: To make an AI's hidden reasoning auditable and re-runnable, teach the reader each step directly rather than hoping it emerges from final answers alone. But expect it to break on longer or reworded problems, so keep the teaching broad.

charts.chart_takeaway: After extra answer-only training the bars for longer programs fall to the floor; even the gentle version keeps only the four-step case standing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tiny per-step reading errors pile up across a long program
charts.chart_read: Three lines track the step-teacher by program length: how often each operation is read right, each number is read right, and the whole program is exactly right. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Per-step reading stays high, but the whole-program line falls far faster — one wrong step anywhere spoils the answer, so small errors compound.

charts.chart_plain_title: Deeper notes and careful number-finding let longer programs run correctly
charts.chart_read: Each line is a different design of the reader, scored by how often the executed answer is right across program lengths. Higher is better. Designs differ in how deep they peek and how they locate each step's number.
charts.chart_takeaway: Reading deeper internal notes and pinning each number beside its operation lift the lines highest, while the shallowest design dies past four steps.

charts.chart_read: Two lines by program length show how much probability the step-teaching system versus a plain answer-reader places on the true answer. Higher is better; the flat lower line marks random-guess level.
charts.chart_takeaway: The step-teacher leads only at four steps, then drops to meet the flat answer-reader line at random-guess level — past four steps the edge disappears.

qwen_slot_repair_distillation.verdict_tag: Learned repair fails on fresh problems
qwen_slot_repair_distillation.concept_primer: A machine turns word problems into short step-by-step arithmetic worksheets, but it often gets the final answer wrong. Imagine training a fast proofreader to spot and fix the few wrong steps in a single pass.
qwen_slot_repair_distillation.plain_question: Can a small proofreader model learn to fix the few wrong steps in a math machine's worksheets, so more final answers come out correct on brand-new problems?
qwen_slot_repair_distillation.plain_answer: No. On the practice problems it was tuned on, the proofreader nudged correct answers from about 33% to 35%. But on brand-new problems it matched or slightly hurt the plain machine, even though a corrected worksheet almost always existed nearby. The learned fixes did not carry over.
qwen_slot_repair_distillation.why_it_matters: Being able to find a fix by searching offline does not mean a model can learn to apply it live in one pass. When teaching a repair step, test it on fresh, differently-worded problems before trusting it, since practice-set gains can vanish.

key_numbers.label: Correct answers on reworded new problems
key_numbers.sub: the proofreader made these worse

key_numbers.label: Correct answers on tuned practice problems

charts.chart_plain_title: Proofreader beats the machine only on practice problems, not new ones
charts.chart_read: Bars group four problem sets. Within each set, three bars: the plain machine, the proofreader-edited version, and a best-possible fix that always searches. Bar height is the share of correct final answers; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The proofreader bar tops the machine only on the leftmost practice set; on every fresh set it ties or drops, far below the tall best-possible-fix bars.

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of fixable errors closed flips to worse on new problems
charts.chart_read: Each bar shows, for one problem set, how much of the gap between the plain machine and a best-possible fix the proofreader closed. Above the zero line is progress; below the line means it made answers worse.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars stand above zero for the two practice sets but fall below zero for all three fresh sets, so the fixing skill reverses on unseen problems.

charts.chart_plain_title: The proofreader only edges past the machine at the last training step
charts.chart_read: The wavy line tracks the proofreader's share of correct answers on practice problems after each training round. The flat line is the plain machine's fixed score. Sitting above the flat line is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The wavy line bounces around and only pokes above the flat machine line at the final round, so the small gain looks like luck rather than a stable skill.

charts.chart_plain_title: Editing makes the same problem get different answers when reworded
charts.chart_read: For three sources, the plain machine, a best-possible fix, and the proofreader, bars show how often one problem asked two ways gets a matching answer, and how often both wordings are right. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The proofreader's bars are the shortest, so its edits make the two wordings disagree more often than even the unedited machine does.

charts.chart_plain_title: No proofreader setup wins on both practice and new problems
charts.chart_read: Each pair of bars is one proofreader setup: one bar for correct answers on practice problems, one for fresh problems. Taller is better, and a good setup would lift both bars together.
charts.chart_takeaway: The setup that is best on practice problems is not best on fresh ones, and no configuration raises both bars at the same time.

charts.chart_plain_title: The proofreader never settles on which steps to fix
charts.chart_read: Two wavy lines across training rounds: how often the proofreader's edits land on genuinely wrong steps, and how many of the truly wrong steps it catches. Higher and steadier is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines keep swinging round after round and never flatten out, so the choice of which steps to fix stays unreliable throughout training.

qwen_slot_stability_compiler.verdict_tag: Reword-consistency rule helps some lengths, not others
qwen_slot_stability_compiler.concept_primer: Picture a multi-step math word problem. Instead of doing the arithmetic in its head, the model acts like a clerk who points at each number and operation and types them into a hidden calculator that runs the steps exactly.
qwen_slot_stability_compiler.plain_question: When the same problem is reworded, does forcing the clerk to point to the same numbers and operations both ways make its final answers more reliable?
qwen_slot_stability_compiler.plain_answer: It depends. The clerk-and-calculator method itself works well, solving most short and medium problems, while a model that just guesses the final answer directly never rises above random. But the extra rule forcing matching choices across rewordings only helped at some problem lengths and hurt at others.
qwen_slot_stability_compiler.why_it_matters: To make a small model do exact multi-step arithmetic, have it point at the numbers and hand them to a real calculator instead of guessing the answer. A reword-consistency penalty on top of that is not worth adding.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each method solves the exact answer, by problem length
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many operation steps a problem has; the vertical axis is how often the exact final answer is right. The two upper lines are the clerk-and-calculator method; the bottom flat line guesses the answer directly. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both clerk-and-calculator lines sit far above the flat bottom line, which never lifts off the floor: pointing-and-copying works, guessing the answer directly does not.

charts.chart_plain_title: Solving reworded problems: accuracy by length for each method
charts.chart_read: Same setup, but every problem is rephrased into different wording. Horizontal axis is problem length, vertical axis is how often the exact answer is right; the two upper lines are the two clerk-and-calculator versions, the bottom line guesses directly. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two clerk lines nearly overlap except at the longest length, where the version with the reword-consistency rule pulls slightly ahead, 23% versus 16%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Do the two wordings give the same answer, with and without the rule?
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by problem length. Height is how often one problem, worded two ways, produces the same final answer. One bar per length adds the reword-consistency rule, the other leaves it out. Taller means more consistent.
charts.chart_takeaway: The rule wins at the shortest length but loses clearly at medium length, 75% versus 88%: it is a mixed bag, not a steady gain in consistency.

charts.chart_plain_title: An early lead for the rule fades by the end of training
charts.chart_read: Bars compare the two versions on medium-length reworded pairs, halfway through training and at the end. The left group is exact-answer accuracy, the right group is how often both wordings agree. One bar per group uses the rule, the other does not. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Halfway through, the rule's version agrees far more often, 36% versus 5%, but by the end both versions land in the same place: the early edge evaporates.

charts.chart_plain_title: Why long problems fail: tiny per-step slips add up
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is problem length. Each upper line tracks how often one piece is identified correctly, the starting number, the operations, or the step numbers. The bottom line is getting the whole sequence exactly right. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Individual pieces stay near-perfect, but the whole-sequence line collapses to zero on the longest problems: one small slip anywhere ruins the entire chain.

charts.chart_plain_title: Most reworded-problem accuracy is gained in the second half of training
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by problem length, for reworded prompts. Each version is measured halfway through training and at the end. Height is how often the exact answer is right. Taller is better; compare halfway against end within each version.
charts.chart_takeaway: End-of-training bars tower over halfway bars at medium and long lengths: the second half of training is where the reworded problems actually get solved.

qwen_span_free_compiler.verdict_tag: The add-on helps only where it drilled
qwen_span_free_compiler.concept_primer: Think of the frozen model as a clerk who silently reads a math word-problem, and a small add-on translator that turns the clerk's private notes into the exact steps a calculator can run.
qwen_span_free_compiler.plain_question: Can a small add-on read a frozen model's private notes and write out the exact math steps to solve the problem, without being handed the numbers?
qwen_span_free_compiler.plain_answer: It depends. On the exact problem style it practiced, the add-on nailed the full sequence of steps 100% of the time, versus random-guess level for every shortcut method. But it fell apart on longer chains or reworded problems.
qwen_span_free_compiler.why_it_matters: A tiny translator can decode a frozen model's internal notes into runnable steps, but only if you teach it where to look. Expect it to memorize one narrow format, not to generalize.

key_numbers.label: Full sequence solved, reworded style

charts.chart_plain_title: Practiced problems: guided translator versus shortcut methods
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one method; height is how often the whole step-sequence came out exactly right on the practiced problem style. Taller is better. The three shortcut methods sit near the floor; the guided translator stands near the top.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the guided translator reaches the top of the chart, hitting nearly 100%, while every shortcut method stays at random-guess level.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy falls off a cliff past the practiced chain length
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many math steps the problem has; the vertical axis is how often the whole sequence is solved exactly. Higher is better. Each line is one method. All lines are high only at the practiced short length and drop to the floor beyond it.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line plunges from near-perfect at the practiced length to near-zero at longer lengths, showing the skill is locked to one chain length.

charts.chart_plain_title: Rewording the problem breaks the translator's number-reading
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by what the translator had to read out: the starting number, each operation, each amount, and the whole sequence. Height is accuracy; taller is better. Trained wording bars are near the top; reworded-prompt bars are much shorter except for the starting number.
charts.chart_takeaway: Reworded bars stay tall only for the starting number and roughly halve for the operations and amounts, dragging the full-sequence bar down to almost nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Good step-by-step accuracy still fails the whole chain
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is chain length; vertical axis is accuracy; higher is better. Two lines track how often each single operation and amount is right; the third tracks solving the whole sequence. The per-step lines stay fairly high while the whole-sequence line sinks fast.
charts.chart_takeaway: Even with each step around 90% right at longer chains, the whole-sequence line drops below 6%, because small per-step errors pile up.

charts.chart_plain_title: Only teaching the translator where to look works
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a different way of building the translator; height is how often it solved the whole practiced sequence exactly. Taller is better. The three simpler pooling designs sit at the floor; the two that learn where to focus stand near the top.
charts.chart_takeaway: Accuracy jumps from random-guess level to near-100% only for the two designs that learn where to point, identifying focus as the key ingredient.

charts.chart_plain_title: No training schedule escapes the longer-chain cliff
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is chain length; vertical axis is how often the whole sequence is solved; higher is better. Each line is a different training schedule. All lines collapse toward the floor by the twelve-step mark, and some start low even at short chains.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every schedule's line falls to near-zero by twelve steps, and the fixed and mixed schedules give up much of their short-chain accuracy too.

charts.chart_plain_title: Robustness to rewording trades against raw accuracy
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by training schedule, with one bar for trained wording and one for reworded wording; height is full-sequence accuracy, taller is better. High-accuracy schedules show a tall trained bar next to a floor-level reworded bar; the milder schedules show two similar mid-height bars.
charts.chart_takeaway: The most accurate schedules drop from near-perfect to random-guess level under rewording, while milder schedules keep similar accuracy both ways but never climb as high.

charts.chart_plain_title: Translator locks onto trained wording early, never learns rewording
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is training progress; vertical axis is how often the whole sequence is solved; higher is better. One line is trained wording, the other is reworded wording. The trained line shoots up early and flattens near the top; the reworded line stays pinned at the bottom throughout.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained-wording line reaches near-perfect within the first stretch and holds, while the reworded line never lifts off random-guess level.

qwen_state_ladder_compiler.verdict_tag: Gradual practice helped, step-checking did not
qwen_state_ladder_compiler.concept_primer: Picture the model working through a recipe of many arithmetic steps in a row, keeping a running total; one slip anywhere ruins the final answer, so staying exact over a long chain is the whole challenge.
qwen_state_ladder_compiler.plain_question: Does forcing the model to show and check its running total after every single step help it follow long arithmetic chains correctly?
qwen_state_ladder_compiler.plain_answer: No. Checking the running total after every step never beat a matched model trained without it, and full-strength checking made things worse. The real gain came from a separate trick, gradually working up to longer chains, which lifted the hardest problems from random-guess level to about 21%.
qwen_state_ladder_compiler.why_it_matters: To get a small model to nail long chains of exact steps, invest in a gradual difficulty ramp rather than forcing it to justify every intermediate step; heavy step-checking can even make it fragile on reworded problems.

charts.chart_takeaway: The ordinary-model bars sit at random-guess level while the gradual-practice bars tower over them, and no step-checking bar tops the gradual-practice control.

charts.chart_takeaway: At its best moment the lighter step-checking bar is tallest, but that peak was never saved, so it doesn't count as a real win.

charts.chart_read: One bar per method showing how often the same 24-step problem, written two different ways, produced matching step-by-step work; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The full step-checking bar nearly vanishes near zero while the no-checking control stands highest, showing heavy checking made the model inconsistent.

charts.chart_plain_title: Step-checking's early lead vanishes with more training
charts.chart_read: Two pairs of bars compare the step-checking model and the control on the hardest 24-step problems, first after short training then after long training; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Step-checking leads at the short budget but slips below the control after longer training, so its early edge evaporates.

charts.chart_plain_title: Full step-checking fails completely on reworded problems
charts.chart_read: Bars compare accuracy on standard versus reworded 24-step problems for three methods; taller is better, and a small gap between the two means the method handles new wording.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every method dips on reworded problems, but the full step-checking bar collapses all the way to zero while the others hold near one-in-seven.

charts.chart_plain_title: Long-chain skill appears only in the final training stage
charts.chart_read: Lines track accuracy on the hardest 24-step problems across training steps for each method; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line hugs zero until the final long-chain stage, spikes near step 800, then dips, so the last saved model is not the best one.

charts.chart_plain_title: Near-perfect parts still add up to failed whole programs
charts.chart_read: Bars show how accurately each part of a program is copied (starting value, operations, arguments) versus getting the entire 24-step program exactly right; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The per-part bars sit near perfect while whole-program bars sink to about one in five, as tiny scattered slips compound over 24 steps.

qwen_structural_compiler_attribution_abl.verdict_tag: Copying steps was not the cause
qwen_structural_compiler_attribution_abl.concept_primer: Picture the model writing a short step-by-step program to reach an answer, then running it. The test is whether that program still works when it grows to 24 steps instead of just a few.
qwen_structural_compiler_attribution_abl.plain_question: When a model writes longer step-by-step programs, does copying its earlier steps into the new ones explain why it handles the longest 24-step programs?
qwen_structural_compiler_attribution_abl.plain_answer: No. Copying earlier steps was not the reason. What actually worked was training on full-length problems from the start while gradually raising difficulty, which reached 97% on the longest programs versus about 2% for the copying approach.
qwen_structural_compiler_attribution_abl.why_it_matters: To get a model handling longer step-by-step problems, train on the full length from the start while gradually raising difficulty, rather than stitching longer programs together from copied earlier pieces.

charts.chart_takeaway: The full-length-plus-rising-difficulty approach towers over the rest on standard wording and twin-prompts; copying steps only helps on reworded prompts.

qwen_structural_latent_compiler_expansio.verdict_tag: Stretched to longer problems without breaking
qwen_structural_latent_compiler_expansio.concept_primer: Picture the model with a built-in calculator that follows a hidden, step-by-step recipe in its head instead of writing the steps out. Here we try to stretch that recipe to more steps.
qwen_structural_latent_compiler_expansio.plain_question: Can the model's hidden step-by-step recipe be stretched to handle much longer problems without losing accuracy on the shorter ones it already solved?
qwen_structural_latent_compiler_expansio.plain_answer: Yes. Growing the hidden recipe from short to long problems kept the shorter ones perfectly solved while the longest reached 83 to 100 percent, all without writing steps out or trying many guesses.
qwen_structural_latent_compiler_expansio.why_it_matters: You can extend a model's internal step-by-step solving to harder, longer tasks by growing it in stages, rather than retraining from scratch or forcing it to spell every step out.

qwen_structured_bridge.verdict_tag: Translate to steps, then a calculator runs them
qwen_structured_bridge.concept_primer: Instead of asking the frozen model to blurt out a final answer, a small add-on translates its reading of the words into a list of button-presses that a separate calculator then executes exactly.
qwen_structured_bridge.plain_question: Can a frozen model's reading of a word problem be turned into steps a hidden calculator runs, beating just asking for the answer?
qwen_structured_bridge.plain_answer: Yes. Translating the words into calculator steps hit 96-100% on chains up to 12 operations, while asking the same model to name the final answer stayed at random-guess level. The win requires showing the translator the correct steps during training.
qwen_structured_bridge.why_it_matters: When a task is a chain of exact operations, wire the model to emit the steps and let a reliable calculator do the math, rather than trusting it to compute the answer in its head.

charts.chart_takeaway: Numbers are read essentially perfectly, so the 12% of failed chains trace to the occasional wrong operation, whose errors pile up over many steps.

qwen_support_contrastive_meta_icl.verdict_tag: Model truly reads examples but trades some accuracy
qwen_support_contrastive_meta_icl.concept_primer: Think of a student handed a few worked examples on an exam. You want them actually reading those examples to solve new problems, not just guessing from the topic. This training method rewards reading the examples.
qwen_support_contrastive_meta_icl.plain_question: Does this training method make the model both more accurate and genuinely reliant on the worked examples it is shown?
qwen_support_contrastive_meta_icl.plain_answer: It depends. The method makes the model genuinely rely on the examples (accuracy collapses to about 7% when they are scrambled) and nearly triples accuracy over the untrained model, but a simpler training recipe scored a few points higher.
qwen_support_contrastive_meta_icl.why_it_matters: If you need a model that actually uses the examples you feed it rather than pattern-matching the task type, this method delivers that reliably, but expect to give up a little raw accuracy for it.

charts.chart_takeaway: This method's real-examples bar is tall near 50% but its scrambled bar collapses to about 7%, unlike other recipes whose scrambled bars stay high, proving it truly reads the examples.

charts.chart_read: Bars show, for each recipe, the accuracy drop when examples are scrambled instead of real, on two test sets. A taller bar means the model leans more heavily on the actual examples, which is the goal.
charts.chart_takeaway: This method's bars are the tallest on both test sets, meaning its answers hinge on the real examples far more than any other recipe's do.

charts.chart_takeaway: With real examples this method scores about 84%, but its scrambled bar drops to about 7% while other recipes stay at 37 to 83%, again showing it depends on the examples.

charts.chart_takeaway: This method lifts most categories, several from zero to 78-100%, with only one category slipping backward and one staying stuck at zero.

charts.chart_takeaway: The main test set stays steady near 49-51% across all three runs, so the gain is repeatable rather than a one-off lucky run.

charts.chart_takeaway: Every run helps 17-18 tasks and hurts exactly 3, so the improvement is broad and consistent rather than a handful of lucky tasks.

qwen_tail_repair_stability_critic.concept_primer: Think of a proofreader who is handed a mostly-correct recipe with a buggy ending and several candidate corrections, and must pick the right fix without ever seeing the answer key.
qwen_tail_repair_stability_critic.plain_question: Can a learned proofreader fix the buggy endings of mostly-correct programs by picking the right candidate, without peeking at the answer?
qwen_tail_repair_stability_critic.plain_answer: No. Both learned proofreaders left the share of correct programs exactly where it started, near 45%, even though a picker allowed to peek could reach 91% from the very same candidate fixes.
qwen_tail_repair_stability_critic.why_it_matters: Choosing the fix, not having one, is the wall. A picker that only reads summary statistics fails; it must actually compare each candidate program against the original prompt to be trusted.

charts.chart_plain_title: Learned repair picker never beats leaving the program alone

charts.chart_takeaway: The two learned-proofreader bars sit exactly on the no-repair bar while the peek-allowed answer bar towers far above, so a good fix exists but is never chosen.

charts.chart_read: Three source generators, two bars each: leaving programs as-is versus a peek-allowed picker. Height is the share that run correctly, so taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The peek-allowed picker lifts the weakest source from near-zero to about 78%, so nearly all the unclaimed gains sit on that weak source.
